Django Focus
anihpj Job Portal

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) complete reference with practical examples: terraform init, plan, apply — featuring the anihpj Job Portal Django application with 5 models (Skills, Author, Location, JobPost) deployed on AWS.

Terraform AWS Django Docker DevOps IaC
Get Started Django Examples Cheatsheets

🔥 anihpj: Complete AWS Deployment Guide with Terraform

Production-ready Django job portal deployment using AWS three-tier architecture

🔥 Overview: From Django to Production on AWS

This comprehensive guide demonstrates deploying anihpj, a Django-based job portal, to AWS using Terraform Infrastructure as Code. Follow along as we build a robust, scalable, production-ready infrastructure using AWS best practices and a three-tier architecture.

🎯
What You'll Learn: Complete AWS infrastructure deployment, Django application hosting, VPC networking, RDS PostgreSQL setup, Application Load Balancer configuration, Auto Scaling Groups, CloudFront CDN, ElastiCache Redis, and production security best practices.

🏗️ Infrastructure Components

  • VPC with Multi-AZ deployment (us-east-1a, us-east-1b)
  • Public subnets for ALB (10.0.1.0/24, 10.0.2.0/24)
  • Private app subnets (10.0.10.0/24, 10.0.11.0/24)
  • Private data subnets (10.0.20.0/24, 10.0.21.0/24)
  • NAT Gateways for outbound internet access

💻 Application Stack

  • Django 4.2 job portal application
  • PostgreSQL RDS with Multi-AZ
  • Redis ElastiCache for session management
  • S3 for static and media file storage
  • CloudFront CDN for global content delivery

🔐 Security & Performance

  • Application Load Balancer with SSL termination
  • Security Groups with least privilege access
  • IAM roles and policies for EC2 instances
  • VPC Flow Logs for network monitoring
  • Auto Scaling Groups for high availability

🚀 Environment Management

  • Separate environments: dev, staging, production
  • Modular Terraform structure
  • Environment-specific variable files
  • State management with S3 backend
  • Automated deployment pipelines

📁 Project Structure

📋
Directory Organization: The anihpj project follows best practices for separating infrastructure code, application code, and environment configurations.
Project Directory Structure 
anihpj-terraform-aws/
├── terraform/                  # Infrastructure as Code
│   ├── environments/          # Environment-specific configs
│   │   ├── dev/
│   │   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   │   └── terraform.tfvars
│   │   ├── staging/
│   │   └── production/
│   ├── modules/               # Reusable Terraform modules
│   │   ├── vpc/
│   │   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   │   └── outputs.tf
│   │   ├── rds/
│   │   ├── ec2/
│   │   ├── alb/
│   │   ├── s3/
│   │   ├── cloudfront/
│   │   ├── elasticache/
│   │   └── iam/
│   └── scripts/              # Deployment and utility scripts
├── application/              # Django application
│   ├── anihpj/              # Main Django project
│   ├── jobpost/             # Job posting app
│   ├── manage.py
│   ├── requirements.txt
│   └── docker/
└── docs/                    # Documentation
    ├── deployment.md
    ├── architecture.md
    └── troubleshooting.md

🐍 Django Application Overview

The anihpj application is a comprehensive job portal built with Django 4.2, featuring user authentication, job posting, and application management capabilities.

Django Models Structure 
# jobpost/models.py
from django.db import models
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

class Author(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    email = models.EmailField()
    bio = models.TextField(blank=True)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)

class Location(models.Model):
    city = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    state = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    country = models.CharField(max_length=50)

class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    category = models.CharField(max_length=50)

class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    description = models.TextField()
    requirements = models.TextField()
    salary_range = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    company = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    author = models.ForeignKey(Author, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    location = models.OneToOneField(Location, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    skills = models.ManyToManyField(Skills)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)

🏗️ AWS Three-Tier Architecture

The anihpj deployment follows AWS best practices with a three-tier architecture separating presentation, application, and data layers for optimal security, scalability, and maintainability.

🌐 Public Tier (Presentation Layer)

Application Load Balancer
  • SSL/TLS termination
  • Health checks
  • Traffic distribution
CloudFront CDN
  • Global content delivery
  • Static asset caching
  • Performance optimization

🔒 Private App Tier (Application Layer)

EC2 Auto Scaling Group
  • Django application instances
  • Automatic scaling
  • Health monitoring
ElastiCache Redis
  • Session management
  • Application caching
  • Performance boost

🗄️ Private Data Tier (Data Layer)

RDS PostgreSQL
  • Multi-AZ deployment
  • Automated backups
  • Read replicas
S3 Storage
  • Static files
  • Media uploads
  • Backup storage

🌐 VPC Module Implementation

The VPC module creates a robust networking foundation with proper subnet segmentation, routing, and security controls across multiple Availability Zones.

⚠️
Network Design: The VPC uses CIDR 10.0.0.0/16 with carefully planned subnet allocation to avoid conflicts and allow for future expansion.
terraform/modules/vpc/main.tf 
# VPC Module - Complete Networking Infrastructure
# terraform/modules/vpc/main.tf

# Data source for availability zones
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

# Main VPC
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.vpc_cidr
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-vpc"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# Internet Gateway
resource "aws_internet_gateway" "main" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-igw"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# Elastic IPs for NAT Gateways
resource "aws_eip" "nat" {
  count      = length(var.public_subnet_cidrs)
  domain     = "vpc"
  depends_on = [aws_internet_gateway.main]
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-nat-eip-${count.index + 1}"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# Public Subnets
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count                   = length(var.public_subnet_cidrs)
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block              = var.public_subnet_cidrs[count.index]
  availability_zone       = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-public-subnet-${count.index + 1}"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
    Tier        = "Public"
  }
}

# Private App Subnets
resource "aws_subnet" "private_app" {
  count             = length(var.private_app_subnet_cidrs)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = var.private_app_subnet_cidrs[count.index]
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-private-app-subnet-${count.index + 1}"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
    Tier        = "Application"
  }
}

# Private Data Subnets
resource "aws_subnet" "private_data" {
  count             = length(var.private_data_subnet_cidrs)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = var.private_data_subnet_cidrs[count.index]
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-private-data-subnet-${count.index + 1}"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
    Tier        = "Data"
  }
}

# NAT Gateways
resource "aws_nat_gateway" "main" {
  count         = length(var.public_subnet_cidrs)
  allocation_id = aws_eip.nat[count.index].id
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public[count.index].id
  depends_on    = [aws_internet_gateway.main]
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-nat-gateway-${count.index + 1}"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# Public Route Table
resource "aws_route_table" "public" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  route {
    cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
    gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.main.id
  }
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-public-rt"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# Private App Route Tables
resource "aws_route_table" "private_app" {
  count  = length(var.private_app_subnet_cidrs)
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  route {
    cidr_block     = "0.0.0.0/0"
    nat_gateway_id = aws_nat_gateway.main[count.index].id
  }
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-private-app-rt-${count.index + 1}"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# Private Data Route Tables
resource "aws_route_table" "private_data" {
  count  = length(var.private_data_subnet_cidrs)
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-private-data-rt-${count.index + 1}"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# Route Table Associations - Public
resource "aws_route_table_association" "public" {
  count          = length(var.public_subnet_cidrs)
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.public[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.public.id
}

# Route Table Associations - Private App
resource "aws_route_table_association" "private_app" {
  count          = length(var.private_app_subnet_cidrs)
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.private_app[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.private_app[count.index].id
}

# Route Table Associations - Private Data
resource "aws_route_table_association" "private_data" {
  count          = length(var.private_data_subnet_cidrs)
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.private_data[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.private_data[count.index].id
}

# VPC Flow Logs
resource "aws_flow_log" "vpc_flow_log" {
  iam_role_arn    = aws_iam_role.flow_log.arn
  log_destination = aws_cloudwatch_log_group.vpc_flow_log.arn
  traffic_type    = "ALL"
  vpc_id          = aws_vpc.main.id
}

# CloudWatch Log Group for VPC Flow Logs
resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_group" "vpc_flow_log" {
  name              = "/aws/vpc/flowlogs/${var.project_name}-${var.environment}"
  retention_in_days = 30
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-vpc-flow-logs"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# IAM Role for VPC Flow Logs
resource "aws_iam_role" "flow_log" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}-vpc-flow-log-role"
  
  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          Service = "vpc-flow-logs.amazonaws.com"
        }
      }
    ]
  })
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project_name}-vpc-flow-log-role"
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project_name
  }
}

# IAM Policy for VPC Flow Logs
resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "flow_log" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}-vpc-flow-log-policy"
  role = aws_iam_role.flow_log.id
  
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Action = [
          "logs:CreateLogGroup",
          "logs:CreateLogStream",
          "logs:PutLogEvents",
          "logs:DescribeLogGroups",
          "logs:DescribeLogStreams"
        ]
        Effect   = "Allow"
        Resource = "*"
      }
    ]
  })
}

🔧 VPC Configuration and Outputs

terraform/modules/vpc/variables.tf 
# VPC Module Variables
# terraform/modules/vpc/variables.tf

variable "project_name" {
  description = "Name of the project"
  type        = string
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Environment (dev, staging, production)"
  type        = string
}

variable "vpc_cidr" {
  description = "CIDR block for VPC"
  type        = string
  default     = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

variable "public_subnet_cidrs" {
  description = "CIDR blocks for public subnets"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
}

variable "private_app_subnet_cidrs" {
  description = "CIDR blocks for private app subnets"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = ["10.0.10.0/24", "10.0.11.0/24"]
}

variable "private_data_subnet_cidrs" {
  description = "CIDR blocks for private data subnets"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = ["10.0.20.0/24", "10.0.21.0/24"]
}
terraform/modules/vpc/outputs.tf 
# VPC Module Outputs
# terraform/modules/vpc/outputs.tf

output "vpc_id" {
  description = "ID of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "vpc_cidr_block" {
  description = "CIDR block of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.cidr_block
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  description = "IDs of the public subnets"
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].id
}

output "private_app_subnet_ids" {
  description = "IDs of the private app subnets"
  value       = aws_subnet.private_app[*].id
}

output "private_data_subnet_ids" {
  description = "IDs of the private data subnets"
  value       = aws_subnet.private_data[*].id
}

output "internet_gateway_id" {
  description = "ID of the Internet Gateway"
  value       = aws_internet_gateway.main.id
}

output "nat_gateway_ids" {
  description = "IDs of the NAT Gateways"
  value       = aws_nat_gateway.main[*].id
}

output "public_route_table_id" {
  description = "ID of the public route table"
  value       = aws_route_table.public.id
}

output "private_app_route_table_ids" {
  description = "IDs of the private app route tables"
  value       = aws_route_table.private_app[*].id
}

output "private_data_route_table_ids" {
  description = "IDs of the private data route tables"
  value       = aws_route_table.private_data[*].id
}

⚙️ Environment Configuration

Each environment (dev, staging, production) has its own configuration to ensure proper isolation and resource allocation based on requirements.

terraform/environments/dev/main.tf 
# Development Environment Configuration
# terraform/environments/dev/main.tf

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5"
  
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
  
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "anihpj-terraform-state-dev"
    key            = "dev/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "anihpj-terraform-locks-dev"
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
  
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = var.project_name
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
      Owner       = "anihpj-team"
    }
  }
}

# VPC Module
module "vpc" {
  source = "../../modules/vpc"
  
  project_name                = var.project_name
  environment                 = var.environment
  vpc_cidr                   = var.vpc_cidr
  public_subnet_cidrs        = var.public_subnet_cidrs
  private_app_subnet_cidrs   = var.private_app_subnet_cidrs
  private_data_subnet_cidrs  = var.private_data_subnet_cidrs
}

# Security Groups Module
module "security_groups" {
  source = "../../modules/security_groups"
  
  project_name = var.project_name
  environment  = var.environment
  vpc_id       = module.vpc.vpc_id
}

# RDS Module
module "rds" {
  source = "../../modules/rds"
  
  project_name           = var.project_name
  environment            = var.environment
  vpc_id                 = module.vpc.vpc_id
  private_subnet_ids     = module.vpc.private_data_subnet_ids
  database_security_group_id = module.security_groups.database_security_group_id
  
  db_instance_class      = var.db_instance_class
  db_name               = var.db_name
  db_username           = var.db_username
  db_password           = var.db_password
}

# ElastiCache Module
module "elasticache" {
  source = "../../modules/elasticache"
  
  project_name               = var.project_name
  environment                = var.environment
  vpc_id                     = module.vpc.vpc_id
  private_subnet_ids         = module.vpc.private_app_subnet_ids
  elasticache_security_group_id = module.security_groups.elasticache_security_group_id
  
  node_type                  = var.elasticache_node_type
  num_cache_nodes            = var.elasticache_num_nodes
}

# S3 Module
module "s3" {
  source = "../../modules/s3"
  
  project_name = var.project_name
  environment  = var.environment
}

# CloudFront Module
module "cloudfront" {
  source = "../../modules/cloudfront"
  
  project_name     = var.project_name
  environment      = var.environment
  s3_bucket_domain = module.s3.bucket_domain_name
}

# ALB Module
module "alb" {
  source = "../../modules/alb"
  
  project_name                = var.project_name
  environment                 = var.environment
  vpc_id                      = module.vpc.vpc_id
  public_subnet_ids           = module.vpc.public_subnet_ids
  alb_security_group_id       = module.security_groups.alb_security_group_id
  
  certificate_arn             = var.ssl_certificate_arn
}

# EC2 Module
module "ec2" {
  source = "../../modules/ec2"
  
  project_name                    = var.project_name
  environment                     = var.environment
  vpc_id                          = module.vpc.vpc_id
  private_subnet_ids              = module.vpc.private_app_subnet_ids
  app_security_group_id           = module.security_groups.app_security_group_id
  target_group_arn                = module.alb.target_group_arn
  
  instance_type                   = var.ec2_instance_type
  min_size                        = var.asg_min_size
  max_size                        = var.asg_max_size
  desired_capacity                = var.asg_desired_capacity
  
  # Application configuration
  db_host                         = module.rds.db_endpoint
  db_name                         = var.db_name
  db_username                     = var.db_username
  db_password                     = var.db_password
  redis_endpoint                  = module.elasticache.redis_endpoint
  s3_bucket_name                  = module.s3.bucket_name
}

🚀 Deployment Next Steps

✅
Implementation Status: This guide shows the foundation VPC module. Complete implementation includes RDS, EC2, ALB, S3, CloudFront, ElastiCache, and IAM modules for a production-ready deployment.

📋 Prerequisites

  • AWS CLI configured with appropriate permissions
  • Terraform >= 1.5 installed
  • S3 bucket for Terraform state storage
  • DynamoDB table for state locking
  • SSL certificate in AWS Certificate Manager

🔄 Deployment Commands

# Navigate to environment directory
cd terraform/environments/dev

# Initialize Terraform
terraform init

# Plan the deployment
terraform plan -var-file=terraform.tfvars

# Apply the infrastructure
terraform apply -var-file=terraform.tfvars

🔍 Monitoring & Verification

  • VPC Flow Logs in CloudWatch
  • Application Load Balancer health checks
  • RDS monitoring and performance insights
  • ElastiCache Redis metrics
  • Auto Scaling Group activity

🛡️ Security Considerations

  • Security Groups follow least privilege principle
  • Database credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager
  • EC2 instances in private subnets only
  • VPC Flow Logs enabled for network monitoring
  • IAM roles with minimal required permissions

⚡ Performance & Cost Optimization

💡
Optimization Strategy: The architecture is designed for cost efficiency while maintaining high performance and availability through strategic resource selection.

💾 Database Optimization

  • RDS Instance: db.t3.micro for dev, db.r6g.large for production
  • Multi-AZ: Enabled for production, disabled for dev
  • Backup Retention: 7 days for dev, 30 days for production
  • Storage: gp3 with automated scaling
  • Performance Insights: Enabled for query optimization

🚀 Compute Optimization

  • Instance Type: t3.small for dev, c5.large for production
  • Auto Scaling: CPU-based scaling policies
  • Spot Instances: Mixed instance types for cost savings
  • Launch Templates: Latest AMI with automatic updates
  • Load Balancing: Least outstanding requests algorithm

🌐 Content Delivery

  • CloudFront: Global edge locations
  • S3 Transfer Acceleration: Faster uploads
  • Gzip Compression: Reduced bandwidth usage
  • Browser Caching: Optimized cache headers
  • Static Assets: Long-term caching strategy

⚡ Caching Strategy

  • Redis ElastiCache: Session and query caching
  • Application-level: Django cache framework
  • Database Query: Query result caching
  • Template Caching: Rendered template storage
  • API Response: REST API response caching

⚡ Terraform Fundamentals

Master the foundations of Infrastructure as Code with HashiCorp Terraform

⚡ Overview: Your Journey to Infrastructure as Code Mastery

Welcome to Terraform Fundamentals - your comprehensive guide to mastering Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with HashiCorp Terraform. This section will take you from zero to productive, covering everything from basic concepts to your first working Terraform project. Whether you're deploying the anihpj Django application to AWS or managing complex multi-cloud infrastructure, this foundation is essential.

ℹ️
What You'll Master

Learn the core concepts, syntax, and workflow of Terraform. Understand how to declare infrastructure, manage state, use providers, and create your first project. By the end of this section, you'll be ready to tackle real-world AWS deployments with confidence.

🔄 The Terraform Workflow

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     1️⃣  WRITE                                     │
│               Define infrastructure in .tf files                  │
│                  (HCL or JSON syntax)                            │
└────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     2️⃣  INIT                                      │
│         terraform init - Download providers & modules             │
│              Initialize backend configuration                     │
└────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     3️⃣  VALIDATE                                  │
│    terraform validate - Check configuration syntax                │
│           Ensure resources are properly defined                   │
└────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     4️⃣  PLAN                                      │
│   terraform plan - Preview infrastructure changes                 │
│        Compare desired state vs current state                     │
│          +create  ~modify  -destroy                              │
└────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     5️⃣  APPLY                                     │
│   terraform apply - Execute the planned changes                   │
│      Create, modify, or delete real infrastructure               │
│           Update terraform.tfstate                               │
└────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 6️⃣  MANAGE & UPDATE                               │
│          Modify .tf files as needed                              │
│     Repeat: plan → apply → update state                         │
│                                                                  │
│              7️⃣  DESTROY (when needed)                           │
│       terraform destroy - Clean up all resources                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📋 Section Breakdown (5 Core Topics)

Topic What You'll Learn Time
1.1 Introduction to Terraform What is IaC, Terraform vs alternatives (Chef, Puppet, Ansible), advantages & limitations ⏱️ 10-12 min
1.2 Core Concepts Providers, Resources, Data Sources, Variables, Outputs, State, Modules, Workspaces, Workflow ⏱️ 15-18 min
1.3 Installation & Setup Install Terraform (Windows/Mac/Linux), Docker-based setup, IDE configuration, verification ⏱️ 8-10 min
1.4 HCL Language Basics HCL syntax, blocks, arguments, expressions, functions, HCL vs JSON, comments, formatting ⏱️ 10-12 min
1.5 First Terraform Project Create project structure, write first .tf file, run init/plan/apply, handle errors ⏱️ 12-15 min

🎯 Declarative Approach

Learn to describe WHAT you want (desired state) rather than HOW to build it - Terraform handles the rest

🔄 Idempotent Operations

Run the same configuration multiple times - Terraform only makes necessary changes, ensuring consistency

🌍 Multi-Cloud Support

Master AWS in this guide, but same concepts apply to Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, and 3000+ providers

📝 Version Control Ready

Infrastructure as text files - commit to Git, review changes, collaborate with teams using PRs

🐍
Why Terraform for Django Applications?

Django applications like anihpj require complex infrastructure:

  • Compute: EC2 instances or ECS/EKS containers running Gunicorn/uWSGI
  • Database: RDS PostgreSQL with Multi-AZ, read replicas, automated backups
  • Storage: S3 for media files, CloudFront for static assets
  • Caching: ElastiCache Redis for sessions, database query caching
  • Load Balancing: ALB with health checks and SSL termination
  • Networking: VPC, subnets, security groups, NAT gateways
  • Secrets: Secrets Manager for database credentials, API keys
  • Monitoring: CloudWatch logs, metrics, alarms

Terraform lets you define all of this in code, deploy consistently across environments (dev/staging/prod), and avoid manual configuration errors.

⚔️ Terraform vs Configuration Management Tools

Tool Primary Focus Approach Best For
Terraform Infrastructure provisioning Declarative Creating VPCs, EC2, RDS, S3 - the infrastructure itself
Ansible Configuration management Procedural Installing packages, configuring servers after they exist
Chef Configuration management Procedural (Ruby DSL) Enterprise config management, complex workflows
Puppet Configuration management Declarative (Puppet DSL) Large-scale server configuration, compliance
CloudFormation AWS infrastructure Declarative (JSON/YAML) AWS-only deployments, native integration

💡 Best Practice: Use Terraform for infrastructure provisioning, then Ansible/Chef for server configuration. Many teams use both together - Terraform creates the servers, Ansible installs Django and dependencies.

💡
Prerequisites for This Section
  • Basic Command Line: Comfortable with terminal/PowerShell commands
  • Text Editor: VS Code, Sublime, or any editor (we recommend VS Code with Terraform extension)
  • AWS Account: Free tier is sufficient for learning (optional for section 1, required for section 3)
  • Git Knowledge: Basic understanding helps but not required
  • Time: Set aside 45-60 minutes for hands-on practice
🚀
After Completing This Section

You'll move to Section 2: Terraform CLI & Commands to master the command-line interface, then Section 3: AWS Provider Deep Dive where you'll deploy the complete anihpj infrastructure on AWS. Each concept you learn here will be applied in real-world scenarios.

📌 Quick Reference: Terraform Essentials

File Extension:
.tf or .tf.json
Main Commands:
init, plan, apply, destroy
State File:
terraform.tfstate
Official Docs:
registry.terraform.io

🗺️ Section 1 Learning Path

1 Introduction
→
2 Core Concepts
→
3 Installation
→
4 HCL Basics
→
5 First Project

⏱️ Estimated completion: 45-60 minutes | 📚 Prerequisites: Basic command line knowledge

1.1 Introduction to Terraform

What is Terraform?

Terraform is an open-source Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool developed by HashiCorp. It allows you to define, provision, and manage cloud infrastructure using a declarative configuration language called HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language). Instead of manually clicking through cloud consoles or writing imperative scripts, you describe your desired infrastructure state, and Terraform figures out how to achieve it.

3000+
Providers
100M+
Downloads
40K+
GitHub Stars
#1
IaC Tool

🎯 Key Features of Terraform

📝

Declarative Infrastructure

Define WHAT you want, not HOW to create it. Terraform handles the implementation details automatically.

🌐

Multi-Cloud Support

Manage AWS, Azure, GCP, and 3000+ providers with a single tool and consistent workflow.

🔄

Version Control Ready

Store infrastructure as code in Git. Track changes, review PRs, and maintain history.

📦

Modular Architecture

Create reusable modules to standardize infrastructure patterns across your organization.

📊

State Management

Terraform tracks real-world resources in a state file, enabling accurate change detection.

🔍

Execution Plans

Preview changes before applying them with terraform plan. No surprises!

🔄 The Terraform Workflow

Write
.tf files
→
Init
Download providers
→
Plan
Preview changes
→
Apply
Create resources
→
Destroy
Teardown (optional)

💡 Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Concepts

💡
Why Infrastructure as Code?

IaC treats infrastructure configuration as software code, enabling version control, testing, automation, and collaboration—the same practices that made software development successful.

📂
Version Control
Track every infrastructure change in Git
🔁
Reproducible
Same code = Same infrastructure, every time
👥
Collaboration
Teams can review and approve changes via PRs
🛡️
Drift Prevention
Detect and fix configuration drift automatically
🔄
GitOps Workflows
Git as single source of truth for infrastructure
🧪
Testable
Validate infrastructure before deployment

⚖️ Terraform vs Other IaC Tools

Feature Terraform CloudFormation Pulumi Ansible
Cloud Support ✓ Multi-cloud (3000+ providers) ~ AWS only ✓ Multi-cloud ✓ Multi-cloud
Language HCL (declarative) YAML/JSON Python, TS, Go, C# YAML (imperative)
State Management ✓ Built-in state file ✓ AWS-managed ✓ Built-in ✗ Stateless
Learning Curve Moderate Easy Steep Easy
Community ✓ Very Large ✓ Large (AWS) ~ Growing ✓ Very Large
Best For Infrastructure provisioning AWS-only projects Developers who prefer real languages Configuration management

🔧 Chef & Puppet Comparison

Feature Terraform Chef Puppet
Primary Purpose Infrastructure provisioning Configuration management Configuration management
Language HCL (declarative) Ruby DSL Puppet DSL
Architecture Agentless (API calls) Agent-based (client/server) Agent-based (master/agent)
Use Together? ✓ Yes! Terraform provisions infrastructure, Chef/Puppet configures software on it

⚖️ Terraform Advantages & Limitations

✅

Advantages

  • Multi-cloud - Single tool for AWS, Azure, GCP
  • Declarative - Describe end state, not steps
  • Plan preview - See changes before applying
  • Large ecosystem - 3000+ providers
  • Active community - Extensive modules & examples
  • State tracking - Accurate drift detection
⚠️

Limitations

  • State management - State files need careful handling
  • Learning curve - HCL is another language to learn
  • No rollback - Must apply previous config manually
  • Secrets in state - State may contain sensitive data
  • Limited logic - Complex conditionals can be awkward
  • Provider delays - New features lag cloud releases

🏗️ Terraform Architecture Overview

How Terraform Works

✅ When to Use Terraform

☁️

Cloud Infrastructure Provisioning

Perfect for creating VPCs, EC2 instances, RDS databases, S3 buckets, and all cloud resources.

🌍

Multi-Cloud Deployments

Deploy to AWS, Azure, and GCP with a unified workflow and consistent tooling.

🔄

Environment Replication

Easily create identical dev, staging, and production environments from the same code.

🛡️

Disaster Recovery

Quickly rebuild infrastructure in a different region using your Terraform configurations.

⚠️
When NOT to Use Terraform

Terraform is not ideal for: application configuration (use Ansible), runtime secrets management (use Vault), container orchestration at runtime (use Kubernetes), or one-off scripts that don't need state tracking.

📦 Terraform Editions

Edition Cost Best For Key Features
Open Source Free Individual developers, small teams Full IaC capabilities, CLI, all providers
Terraform Cloud Free (up to 500 resources) Teams needing collaboration Remote state, VCS integration, team management, Sentinel (paid)
Terraform Enterprise Enterprise pricing Large organizations, on-premises Self-hosted, SSO, audit logging, private network
🐍
Django Example: anihpj Job Portal

Throughout this documentation, we'll use the anihpj Job Portal Django application as our practical example. You'll learn to deploy its 4 models (Skills, Author, Location, JobPost) on AWS with EC2, RDS PostgreSQL, S3, and more—all managed with Terraform!

👀 Your First Look at Terraform Code

Here's a simple example creating an S3 bucket for Django static files:

main.tf 
# Configure the AWS Provider
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

# Create S3 bucket for Django static files
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "django_static" {
  bucket = "anihpj-jobportal-static-files"

  tags = {
    Name        = "Django Static Files"
    Project     = "anihpj"
    Environment = "production"
    ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
  }
}

# Output the bucket name
output "bucket_name" {
  value = aws_s3_bucket.django_static.id
}
📖
What This Code Does

Lines 2-9: Specifies we need the AWS provider version 5.x
Lines 11-13: Configures AWS to use the us-east-1 region
Lines 16-26: Creates an S3 bucket named "anihpj-jobportal-static-files" with tags
Lines 29-31: Outputs the bucket name after creation

1.2 Core Concepts

Understanding Terraform's core concepts is essential for effective infrastructure management. Let's explore each building block.

🧩 Terraform Building Blocks

🔌 Providers

🔌

What are Providers?

Providers are plugins that allow Terraform to interact with cloud platforms, SaaS providers, and other APIs. Each provider adds a set of resource types and data sources that Terraform can manage. The AWS provider, for example, lets you manage EC2, S3, RDS, and hundreds of other AWS services.

📚
Provider Registry
registry.terraform.io hosts 3000+ providers
🔢
Versioning
Pin versions with ~> 5.0 constraints
✅
Official vs Community
HashiCorp maintains official providers
🏷️
Aliases
Multiple instances for multi-region
providers.tf 
# Required providers block
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"  # Use version 5.x
    }
  }
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"
}

# Primary AWS provider (us-east-1)
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project   = "anihpj"
      ManagedBy = "Terraform"
    }
  }
}

# Secondary provider for different region (alias)
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "west"
  region = "us-west-2"
}

📦 Resources

📦

What are Resources?

Resources are the most important element in Terraform. Each resource block describes one or more infrastructure objects, such as EC2 instances, S3 buckets, or RDS databases. Resources have arguments (inputs), attributes (outputs), and can depend on other resources.

Resource Block Structure

ec2.tf - Django Server Resource 
# EC2 Instance for Django Application (anihpj Job Portal)
resource "aws_instance" "django_server" {
  ami                    = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type          = var.instance_type
  subnet_id              = aws_subnet.private[0].id
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.django.id]
  iam_instance_profile   = aws_iam_instance_profile.django.name
  key_name               = aws_key_pair.deployer.key_name

  root_block_device {
    volume_size = 30
    volume_type = "gp3"
    encrypted   = true
  }

  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/scripts/userdata.sh", {
    db_host     = aws_db_instance.postgres.address
    db_name     = "anihpj_db"
    bucket_name = aws_s3_bucket.static.id
  })

  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-django-server"
    Application = "JobPortal"
    Model       = "JobPost, Skills, Author, Location"
  }

  # Lifecycle rules
  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

🔍 Data Sources

🔍

What are Data Sources?

Data Sources allow Terraform to query and use information from existing infrastructure that wasn't created by your Terraform configuration. Use them to fetch AMI IDs, look up existing VPCs, or get information about AWS services.

💡
Data Source vs Resource

Resource: Creates and manages infrastructure (CRUD operations)
Data Source: Only reads existing infrastructure (Read-only)

data.tf - Common Data Sources 
# Get latest Ubuntu AMI for Django server
data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["099720109477"]  # Canonical

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-jammy-22.04-amd64-server-*"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }
}

# Get current AWS account info
data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}

# Get available AZs in region
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

# Look up existing VPC by tag (if not creating new)
data "aws_vpc" "existing" {
  filter {
    name   = "tag:Name"
    values = ["production-vpc"]
  }
}

# Usage in resources:
# ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
# account_id = data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id

🔄 Variables & Outputs

📥

Input Variables

Parameters that allow customization of Terraform configurations without altering the source code. Define once, use across environments.

📤

Output Values

Export important values after apply—like instance IPs, database endpoints, or bucket names—for use in scripts or other configs.

variables.tf - Input Variables for anihpj 
# Environment variable
variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment (dev, staging, production)"
  type        = string
  default     = "dev"

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "production"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or production."
  }
}

# Instance type with different defaults per environment
variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type for Django server"
  type        = string
  default     = "t3.small"
}

# Database configuration as an object
variable "database" {
  description = "RDS PostgreSQL configuration"
  type = object({
    instance_class    = string
    allocated_storage = number
    db_name           = string
  })
  default = {
    instance_class    = "db.t3.micro"
    allocated_storage = 20
    db_name           = "anihpj_db"
  }
}

# Sensitive variable for secrets
variable "db_password" {
  description = "Database master password"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true  # Won't show in logs
}
outputs.tf - Export Values 
# Output the Django server's public IP
output "django_server_ip" {
  description = "Public IP of the Django EC2 instance"
  value       = aws_instance.django_server.public_ip
}

# Output the RDS endpoint for Django settings.py
output "database_endpoint" {
  description = "RDS PostgreSQL endpoint"
  value       = aws_db_instance.postgres.endpoint
}

# Output S3 bucket for static files
output "static_bucket" {
  description = "S3 bucket name for Django static files"
  value       = aws_s3_bucket.static.id
}

# Sensitive output (hidden in CLI)
output "database_connection_string" {
  description = "Full database connection string"
  value       = "postgresql://${var.db_username}:${var.db_password}@${aws_db_instance.postgres.endpoint}/${var.database.db_name}"
  sensitive   = true
}

📊 State Management

📊

What is Terraform State?

State is Terraform's record of the infrastructure it manages. The state file (terraform.tfstate) maps your configuration to real-world resources, tracks metadata, and improves performance. Without state, Terraform wouldn't know which resources it created or how to update them.

State File Role in Terraform

Your Code
.tf files
↔
State File
terraform.tfstate
↔
Real World
AWS Resources
⚠️
State File Security

The state file contains sensitive information including passwords, keys, and resource IDs. Never commit terraform.tfstate to Git! Use remote backends with encryption (S3 + DynamoDB).

backend.tf - Remote State Configuration 
# Store state remotely in S3 with locking via DynamoDB
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "anihpj-terraform-state"
    key            = "production/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-lock"
  }
}

📦 Modules

📦

What are Modules?

Modules are containers for multiple resources that are used together. They're the primary way to package and reuse Terraform code. Think of modules like functions in programming—define once, call many times with different parameters.

🏠
Root Module
Your main .tf files in the working directory
👶
Child Modules
Modules called by your root module
🌐
Registry Modules
Public modules from Terraform Registry
🔒
Private Modules
Your organization's internal modules
main.tf - Using Modules 
# Use official AWS VPC module from Terraform Registry
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.0.0"

  name = "anihpj-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs             = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24", "10.0.103.0/24"]

  enable_nat_gateway = true
  single_nat_gateway = true

  tags = {
    Project     = "anihpj"
    Environment = var.environment
  }
}

# Use a local module for Django-specific resources
module "django_app" {
  source = "./modules/django-app"

  vpc_id            = module.vpc.vpc_id
  private_subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnets
  instance_type     = var.instance_type
  environment       = var.environment
}

🗂️ Workspaces

🗂️

What are Workspaces?

Workspaces allow you to manage multiple state files from a single configuration. Each workspace has its own state, enabling you to create separate environments (dev, staging, prod) without duplicating code. Access the current workspace name with terraform.workspace.

Workspace Commands 
# List workspaces (* indicates current)
terraform workspace list
# * default
#   dev
#   staging
#   production

# Create new workspace
terraform workspace new dev

# Switch workspace
terraform workspace select production

# Show current workspace
terraform workspace show
Using Workspaces in Config 
# Workspace-aware resource naming
resource "aws_instance" "django" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = terraform.workspace == "production" ? "t3.large" : "t3.small"

  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-django-${terraform.workspace}"
    Environment = terraform.workspace
  }
}

# Workspace-specific variable files
locals {
  env_config = {
    dev = {
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
      min_size      = 1
      max_size      = 2
    }
    staging = {
      instance_type = "t3.small"
      min_size      = 2
      max_size      = 4
    }
    production = {
      instance_type = "t3.large"
      min_size      = 3
      max_size      = 10
    }
  }
  
  config = local.env_config[terraform.workspace]
}

🔄 The Terraform Workflow (Write → Plan → Apply)

1️⃣ Write - Create Configuration

Define your infrastructure in .tf files using HCL. Organize code into logical files (main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf).

2️⃣ Init - Initialize Working Directory

Run terraform init to download providers, initialize backend, and prepare the working directory.

3️⃣ Validate - Check Syntax

Run terraform validate to check configuration syntax and internal consistency without accessing remote services.

4️⃣ Plan - Preview Changes

Run terraform plan to see what changes Terraform will make. Review carefully before proceeding!

5️⃣ Apply - Create/Update Resources

Run terraform apply to execute the plan and create/modify real infrastructure. Requires confirmation.

6️⃣ Destroy - Teardown (Optional)

Run terraform destroy to remove all resources. Use with caution in production!

💡
Pro Tip: Save Your Plan

Use terraform plan -out=tfplan to save the plan, then terraform apply tfplan to apply exactly what you reviewed. This prevents drift between plan and apply in CI/CD pipelines.

1.3 Installation & Setup

Get Terraform installed and configured on your development machine with proper IDE integration and AWS authentication.

💻 Installing Terraform

🪟

Windows

Use Chocolatey, Scoop, or manual download from HashiCorp.

🍎

macOS

Use Homebrew for easy installation and updates.

🐧

Linux

Use apt, yum, or download the binary directly.

Windows Installation (PowerShell) 
# Option 1: Using Chocolatey (Recommended)
choco install terraform

# Option 2: Using Scoop
scoop install terraform

# Option 3: Using winget
winget install Hashicorp.Terraform

# Verify installation
terraform -version
# Terraform v1.7.0
macOS Installation 
# Using Homebrew (Recommended)
brew tap hashicorp/tap
brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform

# Verify installation
terraform -version
Linux Installation (Ubuntu/Debian) 
# Add HashiCorp GPG key
wget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg

# Add repository
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list

# Install Terraform
sudo apt update && sudo apt install terraform

# Verify
terraform -version
💡
Use tfenv for Version Management

Install tfenv to manage multiple Terraform versions. This is essential when working on projects that require different versions: tfenv install 1.7.0 and tfenv use 1.7.0

💡
Pro Tip: Use tfenv for Version Management

Managing multiple Terraform versions? Use tfenv to switch between versions easily:

git clone https://github.com/tfutils/tfenv.git ~/.tfenv
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.tfenv/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
tfenv install 1.7.0
tfenv use 1.7.0

🐳 Docker-Based Terraform

Run Terraform in a container for consistent environments across your team:

Docker Installation 
# Pull official HashiCorp Terraform image
docker pull hashicorp/terraform:latest

# Run Terraform commands via Docker
docker run --rm -it \
  -v $(pwd):/workspace \
  -w /workspace \
  -e AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID \
  -e AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY \
  -e AWS_REGION \
  hashicorp/terraform:latest init

# Create an alias for convenience (add to .bashrc/.zshrc)
alias terraform='docker run --rm -it -v $(pwd):/workspace -w /workspace -e AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID -e AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY -e AWS_REGION hashicorp/terraform:latest'

# Now use normally
terraform plan
terraform apply

✅ Verifying Installation & PATH Configuration

Verify Terraform Installation 
# Check Terraform version
terraform -version
# Output: Terraform v1.7.0
# on linux_amd64

# Check Terraform location
which terraform      # Linux/macOS
where terraform      # Windows

# Get help on all commands
terraform -help

# Test with simple command
terraform -help init
⚠️
PATH Issues?

If terraform command not found, add the binary location to your PATH:

  • Windows: Add to System Environment Variables > PATH
  • Linux/macOS: Add export PATH="$PATH:/path/to/terraform" to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc

🖥️ IDE Setup (VS Code)

🖥️

HashiCorp Terraform Extension

The official HashiCorp Terraform VS Code extension provides syntax highlighting, IntelliSense, code formatting, and validation. It's essential for productive Terraform development.

🎨
Syntax Highlighting
HCL syntax colors & themes
💡
IntelliSense
Auto-complete for resources & args
📐
Format on Save
Auto terraform fmt on save
⚠️
Validation
Real-time error detection
VS Code settings.json 
{
  // Terraform settings
  "[terraform]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true,
    "editor.formatOnSaveMode": "file"
  },
  "[terraform-vars]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true
  },
  "terraform.languageServer.enable": true,
  "terraform.validation.enableEnhancedValidation": true,
  
  // Recommended: File associations
  "files.associations": {
    "*.tf": "terraform",
    "*.tfvars": "terraform-vars",
    "*.hcl": "hcl"
  }
}

🛠️ Alternative IDEs

🧠

IntelliJ IDEA / PyCharm

  • Install "Terraform and HCL" plugin
  • Supports syntax highlighting & completion
  • Format on save with external tools
📝

Vim / Neovim

  • hashivim/vim-terraform plugin
  • Auto-format: let g:terraform_fmt_on_save=1
  • LSP support with terraform-ls
🎯

Emacs

  • terraform-mode package
  • Syntax highlighting & indentation
  • Format: terraform-format-on-save-mode

🔐 AWS Provider & Authentication

⚠️
Never Hardcode Credentials!

Never put AWS access keys directly in your Terraform files. Use environment variables, AWS CLI profiles, or IAM roles instead.

Auth Method Best For Security
Environment Variables CI/CD pipelines, containers Good
AWS CLI Profile Local development Good
IAM Instance Profile EC2-based deployments Best
AWS SSO Enterprise teams Best
Static Credentials Never use in production! Poor
AWS Authentication Methods 
# Method 1: Environment Variables (CI/CD)
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="your-access-key"
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="your-secret-key"
export AWS_REGION="us-east-1"

# Method 2: AWS CLI Profile (Local Development)
aws configure --profile anihpj-dev
# Enter your credentials when prompted

# Set profile for Terraform
export AWS_PROFILE=anihpj-dev

# Method 3: AWS SSO (Enterprise)
aws sso login --profile my-sso-profile
export AWS_PROFILE=my-sso-profile
provider.tf - AWS Provider Configuration 
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"
  
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

# Provider using AWS CLI profile
provider "aws" {
  region  = var.aws_region
  profile = var.aws_profile  # Optional: uses AWS_PROFILE env var if not set

  # Default tags applied to ALL resources
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "anihpj"
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
      Repository  = "github.com/yourorg/anihpj-infra"
    }
  }
}

# Cross-account access using AssumeRole
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "production"
  region = "us-east-1"

  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::PROD_ACCOUNT_ID:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name = "TerraformSession"
  }
}

📁 Project Structure Best Practices

Recommended Directory Structure for anihpj

terraform/ ├── environments/ │ ├── dev/ │ │ ├── main.tf │ │ ├── variables.tf │ │ ├── terraform.tfvars │ │ └── backend.tf │ ├── staging/ │ │ └── ... (same structure) │ └── production/ │ └── ... (same structure) ├── modules/ │ ├── vpc/ │ │ ├── main.tf │ │ ├── variables.tf │ │ └── outputs.tf │ ├── rds/ │ ├── ecs/ │ └── alb/ ├── global/ │ ├── iam/ │ └── route53/ └── .gitignore

🚫 .gitignore for Terraform

.gitignore 
# Local .terraform directories
**/.terraform/*

# .tfstate files (contains sensitive data!)
*.tfstate
*.tfstate.*

# Crash log files
crash.log
crash.*.log

# Exclude tfvars files that may contain secrets
*.tfvars
!example.tfvars

# Ignore override files
override.tf
override.tf.json
*_override.tf
*_override.tf.json

# Ignore CLI configuration files
.terraformrc
terraform.rc

# Ignore plan output files
*.tfplan
tfplan

# Lock file (optional - some teams commit this)
# .terraform.lock.hcl
📝
Should You Commit .terraform.lock.hcl?

Yes! The lock file ensures consistent provider versions across your team and CI/CD. It should be committed to version control.

1.4 HCL Language Basics

HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL) is Terraform's configuration language. It's designed to be both human-readable and machine-friendly.

📝

What is HCL?

HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) is a declarative language designed for defining infrastructure. It uses a block-based structure that's easier to read than JSON but maintains machine parseability. Terraform also supports JSON syntax, but HCL is preferred for human-written configurations.

📄 HCL vs JSON Syntax

Terraform accepts both HCL (.tf) and JSON (.tf.json) formats. Here's the same configuration in both:

main.tf (HCL - Recommended) 
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "static" {
  bucket = "anihpj-static"
  
  tags = {
    Name = "Django Static"
    Env  = "production"
  }
}
main.tf.json (JSON) 
{
  "resource": {
    "aws_s3_bucket": {
      "static": {
        "bucket": "anihpj-static",
        "tags": {
          "Name": "Django Static",
          "Env": "production"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
💡
When to Use JSON?

Use JSON only for machine-generated configurations (e.g., from other tools). For human-written code, always use HCL for readability.

📦 Blocks, Arguments & Expressions

HCL Structure Anatomy

📊 Type System

Type Description Example
string Text values "anihpj-production"
number Numeric values (int or float) 3, 3.14
bool Boolean true/false true, false
list(type) Ordered collection ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b"]
set(type) Unordered unique collection toset(["web", "api"])
map(type) Key-value pairs { Name = "Django", Env = "prod" }
object({...}) Structured type with named attributes { name = string, port = number }
tuple([...]) Fixed-length sequence [string, number, bool]
any Accepts any type (use sparingly) type = any
null Absence of value value = null
Type Examples 
# Primitive types
variable "app_name" {
  type    = string
  default = "anihpj"
}

variable "instance_count" {
  type    = number
  default = 2
}

variable "enable_monitoring" {
  type    = bool
  default = true
}

# Collection types
variable "availability_zones" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
}

variable "instance_tags" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    Project = "anihpj"
    Team    = "DevOps"
  }
}

# Complex object type (for Django database config)
variable "database_config" {
  type = object({
    engine         = string
    instance_class = string
    storage        = number
    multi_az       = bool
    db_name        = string
  })
  default = {
    engine         = "postgres"
    instance_class = "db.t3.micro"
    storage        = 20
    multi_az       = false
    db_name        = "anihpj_db"
  }
}

🔢 Expressions & Operators

➕
Arithmetic
+, -, *, /, %
⚖️
Comparison
==, !=, <, >, <=, >=
🔀
Logical
&&, ||, !
❓
Conditional
condition ? true : false
Expressions Examples 
# Conditional expression
instance_type = var.environment == "production" ? "t3.large" : "t3.small"

# Arithmetic
storage_size = var.base_storage * 2

# String interpolation
bucket_name = "anihpj-${var.environment}-static"

# Heredoc syntax (multi-line strings)
user_data = <<-EOF
  #!/bin/bash
  echo "Environment: ${var.environment}"
  echo "Project: anihpj"
EOF

# Splat expression (get all IDs from a list of resources)
subnet_ids = aws_subnet.private[*].id

# For expression (transform a list)
upper_names = [for name in var.names : upper(name)]

# For expression with map
instance_map = {
  for idx, az in var.azs : az => aws_instance.web[idx].id
}

# Conditional resource creation
count = var.create_replica ? 1 : 0

💬 Comments & Formatting

Comments in HCL 
# This is a single-line comment (hash style)
// This is also a single-line comment (C style)

/*
  This is a multi-line comment.
  Use for longer explanations or
  temporarily disabling code blocks.
*/

resource "aws_instance" "django" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id  # Inline comment
  instance_type = "t3.medium"             // Also valid inline
}

📚 Documentation Comments

Use comments strategically to document your infrastructure:

Documentation Patterns 
#--------------------------------------------------------------
# VPC Configuration for anihpj Django Application
# 
# This module creates a 3-tier VPC architecture:
#   - Public subnets:  ALB, NAT Gateway
#   - Private subnets: EC2/ECS application servers  
#   - Data subnets:    RDS PostgreSQL, ElastiCache
#
# Author: DevOps Team
# Last Updated: 2024-01-15
#--------------------------------------------------------------

# Django application servers
# NOTE: These instances require the anihpj-ec2 IAM role
# TODO: Add auto-scaling group in next iteration
resource "aws_instance" "django" {
  # ... configuration
}

📏 Formatting Standards & Best Practices

Rule Standard Example
Indentation 2 spaces (no tabs) terraform fmt enforces this
Line Length Max 80-120 characters Break long strings with heredoc
Blank Lines 1 between blocks Separate resources logically
Argument Alignment Align = signs terraform fmt handles this
Block Order terraform → provider → data → resource → output Logical flow top to bottom

🔤 Unicode & Special Characters

Special Characters in HCL 
# Unicode in strings (UTF-8 supported)
description = "Django アプリケーション (日本語)"

# Escape sequences
newline     = "Line1\nLine2"
tab         = "Column1\tColumn2"  
backslash   = "C:\\Users\\path"
quote       = "Say \"Hello\""

# Dollar sign (literal vs interpolation)
literal     = "$${NOT_INTERPOLATED}"   # Outputs: ${NOT_INTERPOLATED}
interpolate = "${var.name}"            # Outputs: actual value

# Percent sign in strings
percentage  = "%%{for item in items}..."  # Escape %{ directive

🔧 terraform fmt & validate

📐

terraform fmt

Automatically formats your code to follow HCL style conventions. Run before committing!

✅

terraform validate

Checks configuration syntax and internal consistency without accessing remote services.

Format & Validate Commands 
# Format all .tf files in current directory
terraform fmt

# Format recursively (all subdirectories)
terraform fmt -recursive

# Check if files are formatted (CI/CD)
terraform fmt -check

# Show diff of formatting changes
terraform fmt -diff

# Validate configuration
terraform validate

# Validate with JSON output (for CI/CD)
terraform validate -json
💡
Pre-commit Hook

Add terraform fmt -check and terraform validate to your Git pre-commit hooks to ensure code quality before commits. Use tools like pre-commit framework.

🔗 Pre-commit Framework Integration

.pre-commit-config.yaml 
# Install: pip install pre-commit && pre-commit install
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.86.0
    hooks:
      - id: terraform_fmt
      - id: terraform_validate
      - id: terraform_tflint
        args:
          - --args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.tflint.hcl
      - id: terraform_docs
        args:
          - --hook-config=--path-to-file=README.md
          - --hook-config=--add-to-existing-file=true
          - --hook-config=--create-file-if-not-exist=true
      - id: terraform_tfsec
Terminal - Setup Pre-commit 
# Install pre-commit framework
pip install pre-commit

# Install hooks in your repo
cd terraform-anihpj/
pre-commit install

# Run against all files (first time)
pre-commit run --all-files

# Now hooks run automatically on git commit!

📋 HCL Quick Reference Card

📦 Block Types
  • terraform - Settings
  • provider - API config
  • resource - Create infra
  • data - Query existing
  • variable - Inputs
  • output - Outputs
  • locals - Local values
  • module - Reusable code
🔤 Data Types
  • string - "hello"
  • number - 42, 3.14
  • bool - true, false
  • list - ["a", "b"]
  • map - {key = "val"}
  • set - toset(["a"])
  • object - Complex struct
  • tuple - Fixed list
🔗 References
  • var.name
  • local.value
  • aws_s3_bucket.name.id
  • data.aws_ami.id
  • module.vpc.id
  • path.module
  • terraform.workspace
⚡ Common Functions
  • join("-", list)
  • split(",", str)
  • length(list)
  • lookup(map, key)
  • format("%s-%s", a, b)
  • file("path")
  • templatefile()

1.5 First Terraform Project

Let's create your first Terraform project—an S3 bucket for Django static files. You'll learn the complete workflow from writing code to destroying resources.

🎯
What We'll Build

An S3 bucket configured for hosting Django static files from the anihpj Job Portal, with proper versioning, encryption, and public access blocking.

📝 Step 1: Create Your Configuration

Create a new directory and the following files:

main.tf 
# ===========================================
# Terraform Configuration for anihpj Job Portal
# S3 Bucket for Django Static Files
# ===========================================

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

# Configure AWS Provider
provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "anihpj"
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
    }
  }
}

# S3 Bucket for Django Static Files
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "django_static" {
  bucket = "${var.project_name}-static-${var.environment}"

  tags = {
    Name        = "Django Static Files"
    Application = "JobPortal"
  }
}

# Enable versioning
resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "django_static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.django_static.id

  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

# Enable server-side encryption
resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "django_static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.django_static.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm = "AES256"
    }
  }
}

# Block all public access (we'll use CloudFront)
resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "django_static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.django_static.id

  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}
variables.tf 
# ===========================================
# Input Variables
# ===========================================

variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region for resources"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

variable "project_name" {
  description = "Project name for resource naming"
  type        = string
  default     = "anihpj"
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Environment (dev, staging, production)"
  type        = string
  default     = "dev"

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "production"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or production."
  }
}
outputs.tf 
# ===========================================
# Output Values
# ===========================================

output "bucket_name" {
  description = "Name of the S3 bucket"
  value       = aws_s3_bucket.django_static.id
}

output "bucket_arn" {
  description = "ARN of the S3 bucket"
  value       = aws_s3_bucket.django_static.arn
}

output "bucket_region" {
  description = "Region of the S3 bucket"
  value       = aws_s3_bucket.django_static.region
}

output "django_settings_config" {
  description = "Configuration for Django settings.py"
  value       = <<-EOT
    # Add to your Django settings.py:
    AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = '${aws_s3_bucket.django_static.id}'
    AWS_S3_REGION_NAME = '${var.aws_region}'
  EOT
}

🚀 Step 2: terraform init

🔧

What init Does

terraform init initializes your working directory by downloading provider plugins, setting up the backend for state storage, and preparing Terraform to run.

Terminal - Basic Init 
# Navigate to your project directory
cd terraform-anihpj

# Initialize Terraform
terraform init
🔧 terraform init Flags
Terminal - Init Flags 
# Skip backend initialization (local dev without remote state)
terraform init -backend=false

# Skip module download (when modules haven't changed)
terraform init -get=false

# Upgrade providers to latest matching version
terraform init -upgrade

# Reconfigure backend (when changing backend settings)
terraform init -reconfigure

# Migrate state to new backend
terraform init -migrate-state

# Use plugin cache (speeds up init across projects)
export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"
terraform init

# Initialize without interactive prompts (CI/CD)
terraform init -input=false
💡
Plugin Cache Directory

Set TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR to share downloaded providers across projects, saving disk space and speeding up initialization. Add to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc.

Expected Output

Initializing the backend...

Initializing provider plugins...
- Finding hashicorp/aws versions matching "~> 5.0"...
- Installing hashicorp/aws v5.31.0...
- Installed hashicorp/aws v5.31.0 (signed by HashiCorp)

Terraform has created a lock file .terraform.lock.hcl to record the provider
selections it made above. Include this file in your version control repository
so that Terraform can guarantee to make the same selections by default when
you run "terraform init" in the future.

Terraform has been successfully initialized!

You may now begin working with Terraform. Try running "terraform plan" to see
any changes that are required for your infrastructure.

📋 Step 3: terraform plan

📋

What plan Does

terraform plan creates an execution plan, showing what actions Terraform will take to reach the desired state. Always review this carefully before applying!

Terminal 
# Preview changes
terraform plan

# Save plan to file (recommended for CI/CD)
terraform plan -out=tfplan

Plan Output (Resources to Create)

Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # aws_s3_bucket.django_static will be created
  + resource "aws_s3_bucket" "django_static" {
      + arn                         = (known after apply)
      + bucket                      = "anihpj-static-dev"
      + id                          = (known after apply)
      + region                      = (known after apply)
      + tags                        = {
          + "Application" = "JobPortal"
          + "Name"        = "Django Static Files"
        }
    }

  # aws_s3_bucket_versioning.django_static will be created
  + resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "django_static" {
      + bucket = (known after apply)
      + versioning_configuration {
          + status = "Enabled"
        }
    }

Plan: 4 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.
💡
Plan Symbols

+ Create    ~ Update    - Destroy    -/+ Replace

✅ Step 4: terraform apply

Terminal 
# Apply changes (will prompt for confirmation)
terraform apply

# Apply saved plan (no confirmation needed)
terraform apply tfplan

# Auto-approve (use with caution! CI/CD only)
terraform apply -auto-approve

Apply Output

Do you want to perform these actions?
  Terraform will perform the actions described above.
  Only 'yes' will be accepted to approve.

  Enter a value: yes

aws_s3_bucket.django_static: Creating...
aws_s3_bucket.django_static: Creation complete after 2s [id=anihpj-static-dev]
aws_s3_bucket_versioning.django_static: Creating...
aws_s3_bucket_versioning.django_static: Creation complete after 1s
...

Apply complete! Resources: 4 added, 0 changed, 0 destroyed.

Outputs:

bucket_name = "anihpj-static-dev"
bucket_arn = "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-static-dev"
bucket_region = "us-east-1"

🔍 Step 5: Verify & Query Outputs

Terminal 
# List all outputs
terraform output

# Get specific output
terraform output bucket_name

# Get output as JSON (for scripts)
terraform output -json

# Get raw value (no quotes)
terraform output -raw bucket_name

# Verify with AWS CLI
aws s3 ls | grep anihpj

💥 Step 6: terraform destroy (Cleanup)

⚠️
Caution: Destructive Operation

terraform destroy will permanently delete ALL resources managed by this configuration. Always review the plan before confirming. In production, use prevent_destroy lifecycle rules.

Terminal 
# Preview what will be destroyed
terraform plan -destroy

# Destroy all resources (will prompt)
terraform destroy

# Destroy specific resource only
terraform destroy -target=aws_s3_bucket.django_static

⚠️ Common Initialization Errors & Solutions

Error Cause Solution
Provider not found Typo in provider source or registry down Check spelling, retry later, or use mirror
Backend configuration changed Backend settings modified after init Run terraform init -reconfigure
Version constraints not met Provider version incompatible Update version constraint or run -upgrade
Lock file mismatch .terraform.lock.hcl doesn't match current platform Delete lock file and re-init (coordinate with team)
Access denied to backend Missing IAM permissions for S3/DynamoDB Check AWS credentials and IAM policy

🔧 Error Handling & Recovery Strategies

Common Recovery Commands 
# Apply failed partway through? Target specific resource
terraform apply -target=aws_s3_bucket.django_static

# State corrupted? Try state operations
terraform state list
terraform state show aws_s3_bucket.django_static

# Resource exists but not in state? Import it
terraform import aws_s3_bucket.django_static anihpj-static-dev

# Need to recreate a resource? Taint it (deprecated) or use replace
terraform apply -replace=aws_s3_bucket.django_static

# State locked from crashed run? Force unlock (use carefully!)
terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID

# Complete disaster? Check for state backup
ls *.tfstate.backup
cp terraform.tfstate.backup terraform.tfstate
⚠️
Rollback Strategy

Terraform doesn't have built-in rollback. To rollback: 1) Check out previous Git commit, 2) Run terraform plan to see reverse changes, 3) Apply carefully. Always test in non-production first!

📂 Understanding the .terraform Directory

Project Structure After Init

terraform-anihpj/ ├── main.tf # Your configuration ├── variables.tf # Input variables ├── outputs.tf # Output values ├── .terraform.lock.hcl # Provider lock file (COMMIT THIS) ├── terraform.tfstate # State file (DON'T COMMIT!) └── .terraform/ # Downloaded providers (DON'T COMMIT!) └── providers/ └── registry.terraform.io/ └── hashicorp/ └── aws/ └── 5.31.0/ └── ... (provider binary)
🎉
Congratulations!

You've completed your first Terraform project! You learned the complete workflow: Write → Init → Plan → Apply → Destroy. Next, we'll dive deeper into CLI commands and AWS resources.

✅ First Project Checklist

  • terraform init
  • terraform plan
  • terraform apply
  • terraform destroy
📁 Files Created
  • main.tf - Resources
  • variables.tf - Inputs
  • outputs.tf - Outputs
  • .gitignore - Exclusions
