Next: Terraform CLI & Commands →

🔧 Terraform CLI & Commands

Master the Terraform command-line interface—your primary tools for managing infrastructure

🔧 Overview: Command-Line Mastery for Infrastructure Control

The Terraform CLI is your command center for infrastructure management. This section provides comprehensive coverage of all essential commands, from basic init and apply to advanced state manipulation and debugging techniques. Master these tools to efficiently deploy and manage the anihpj Django application across multiple environments with confidence.

ℹ️
What You'll Master

Learn every Terraform command you need for daily operations: initializing projects, planning changes, managing state files, working with workspaces for multiple environments, and debugging complex issues. Each command includes practical examples using AWS resources for the anihpj Django app.

🎯 Terraform CLI Command Categories

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      🔧 TERRAFORM CLI COMMANDS                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  📦 ESSENTIAL COMMANDS (Core Workflow)                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  terraform init       → Initialize working directory & download plugins │
│  terraform validate   → Check configuration syntax                      │
│  terraform plan       → Preview infrastructure changes                  │
│  terraform apply      → Execute planned changes                         │
│  terraform destroy    → Remove all managed infrastructure               │
│  terraform refresh    → Update state with real infrastructure (⚠️ v1.5+)│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  💾 STATE MANAGEMENT (Critical for Team Collaboration)                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  terraform state list       → List all resources in state              │
│  terraform state show       → Display resource details                 │
│  terraform state mv         → Move/rename resources                    │
│  terraform state rm         → Remove resources from state              │
│  terraform state pull       → Download remote state                    │
│  terraform state push       → Upload state to remote backend           │
│  terraform import          → Import existing infrastructure            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  🌍 WORKSPACE COMMANDS (Multi-Environment Management)                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  terraform workspace new      → Create new workspace                   │
│  terraform workspace select   → Switch to workspace                    │
│  terraform workspace list     → Show all workspaces                    │
│  terraform workspace show     → Display current workspace              │
│  terraform workspace delete   → Remove workspace                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ⚡ ADVANCED COMMANDS (Optimization & Utilities)                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  terraform fmt           → Format code to canonical style              │
│  terraform console       → Interactive expression evaluation           │
│  terraform graph         → Generate visual dependency graph            │
│  terraform taint         → Mark resource for recreation (⚠️ deprecated)│
│  terraform untaint       → Remove taint mark (⚠️ deprecated)           │
│  terraform providers     → Show provider requirements                  │
│  terraform output        → Display output values                       │
│  terraform force-unlock  → Manually unlock state                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  🐛 DEBUGGING & TROUBLESHOOTING (Problem Solving)                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  TF_LOG=DEBUG           → Enable detailed logging                      │
│  TF_LOG=TRACE           → Maximum verbosity                            │
│  TF_LOG_PATH            → Save logs to file                            │
│  terraform show         → Inspect state or plan files                  │
│  terraform version      → Display Terraform & provider versions        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📋 Section Breakdown (5 Command Categories)

Category Key Commands Use Case
2.1 Essential Commands init, validate, plan, apply, destroy, refresh Daily workflow operations
2.2 State Management state list/show/mv/rm, import, pull, push Managing terraform.tfstate
2.3 Workspace Commands workspace new/select/list/delete Dev/staging/prod environments
2.4 Advanced Commands fmt, console, graph, taint, providers, output Optimization & utilities
2.5 Debugging & Troubleshooting TF_LOG, show, version, error analysis Problem diagnosis & resolution

⚡ terraform apply

Most powerful command - creates, updates, or deletes infrastructure to match your configuration

👁️ terraform plan

Safety net - always preview changes before applying. Shows +create, ~modify, -destroy

💾 terraform state

State management suite - critical for importing existing resources or fixing state drift

🌍 terraform workspace

Environment isolation - separate state files for dev, staging, and production

💡
Typical Daily Workflow
# 1. Initialize (first time or after adding providers)
$ terraform init

# 2. Format code for consistency
$ terraform fmt

# 3. Validate syntax
$ terraform validate

# 4. Preview changes (ALWAYS do this)
$ terraform plan -out=tfplan

# 5. Apply changes (with saved plan for safety)
$ terraform apply tfplan

# 6. Verify outputs
$ terraform output
🐍
Real-World Scenario: Deploying anihpj to Production

You're deploying the anihpj Django job posting app to AWS production. Here's the command sequence:

# Switch to production workspace
$ terraform workspace select production

# Initialize (downloads AWS provider v5.0)
$ terraform init

# Plan changes (creates VPC, EC2, RDS, S3, ALB)
$ terraform plan -var-file="production.tfvars" -out=prod.tfplan

# Review plan output carefully!
Plan: 47 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.

# Apply the plan
$ terraform apply prod.tfplan

# Get ALB DNS for Django ALLOWED_HOSTS
$ terraform output alb_dns_name
anihpj-alb-123456789.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com

🚩 Essential Command Flags

Flag Command Purpose
-auto-approve apply, destroy Skip interactive approval (use in CI/CD)
-out=FILE plan Save plan to file for later apply
-var-file=FILE plan, apply Load variables from file (prod.tfvars)
-target=RESOURCE plan, apply, destroy Operate on specific resource only
-lock=false plan, apply Disable state locking (emergency only)
-refresh=false plan, apply Skip state refresh (faster, less safe)
-input=false apply, plan Disable interactive prompts
-json show, output Output in JSON format for parsing
⚠️
Common CLI Mistakes to Avoid
  • Running apply without plan: Always run terraform plan first to preview changes
  • Using -auto-approve in production: Reserve auto-approve for CI/CD only, never manual deployments
  • Ignoring state lock errors: Don't force-unlock unless you're certain no one else is running Terraform
  • Editing state files manually: Always use terraform state commands, never edit .tfstate directly
  • Forgetting workspace context: Always check terraform workspace show before applying
  • Not saving plan files: Use -out=plan.tfplan to ensure apply matches plan exactly
📚
Prerequisites for This Section
  • Section 1 Complete: Understanding of Terraform fundamentals and HCL syntax
  • Terraform Installed: Version 1.5.0+ installed and verified
  • Practice Project: Have a test .tf file ready (can use Section 1 examples)
  • AWS Access: Configured credentials for hands-on practice
  • Time: 60-90 minutes for comprehensive practice

📌 Quick Reference: Essential Commands

Help:
terraform -help
terraform plan -help
Version:
terraform version
terraform -v
Safe Destroy:
terraform plan -destroy
terraform destroy
Emergency:
terraform force-unlock ID
TF_LOG=DEBUG terraform apply

→

→

5 Debugging

⚡ Quick Command Reference (Copy & Paste Ready)

# Initialize project
terraform init
# Preview changes
terraform plan -out=tfplan
# Apply saved plan
terraform apply tfplan
# List all resources
terraform state list
# Format all files
terraform fmt -recursive
# Debug mode
$env:TF_LOG="DEBUG"
📈
Most Frequently Used Commands (by Usage %)
plan
95%
apply
90%
init
75%
state
60%
destroy
25%

2.1 Essential Commands

These are the core commands you'll use daily. Master these first before diving into advanced operations.

🎯 Command Decision Tree: Which Command Should I Use?

❓ Starting a new project? → terraform init
❓ Want to see what will change? → terraform plan
❓ Check for syntax errors? → terraform validate
❓ Ready to create resources? → terraform apply
❓ Need to see current resources? → terraform state list
❓ Tear everything down? → terraform destroy
🔧

terraform init

Frequency: First time only

Initialize working directory, download providers and modules

# Basic init
terraform init
Providers Modules Backend
✅

terraform validate

Frequency: After changes

Check configuration syntax and internal consistency

# Validate config
terraform validate
Fast Local No AWS
📋

terraform plan

Frequency: Before every apply

Preview changes before applying - NO infrastructure changes made

# Save plan
terraform plan -out=tfplan
Safe Preview Read-only
🚀

terraform apply

Frequency: When ready to deploy

Create or update infrastructure - MAKES REAL CHANGES

# Apply saved plan
terraform apply tfplan
Creates Modifies Write
💥

terraform destroy

Frequency: End of lifecycle

Remove all managed infrastructure - DELETES RESOURCES

# Destroy all
terraform destroy
Dangerous Deletes Irreversible
📊

terraform output

Frequency: As needed

Display output values from state for reference or scripting

# Show outputs
terraform output
Info JSON Scripting

🔧 terraform init (Deep Dive)

💡
What Does Init Actually Do?

Think of terraform init as preparing your workspace. It downloads provider plugins (like AWS, Azure), fetches modules, and configures the backend for state storage. Run it once per project, or when you add new providers/modules.

terraform init Execution Flow

Terminal - terraform init options 
# Basic initialization
terraform init

# Upgrade providers to latest versions
terraform init -upgrade

# Reconfigure backend (when changing backend config)
terraform init -reconfigure

# Migrate state to new backend
terraform init -migrate-state

# Skip backend initialization (local dev)
terraform init -backend=false

# Initialize with backend config from file
terraform init -backend-config="backend.hcl"

# Initialize with specific backend variables
terraform init \
  -backend-config="bucket=anihpj-terraform-state" \
  -backend-config="key=prod/terraform.tfstate" \
  -backend-config="region=us-east-1"

# Skip interactive prompts (CI/CD)
terraform init -input=false

# Get provider from specific directory
terraform init -plugin-dir=/path/to/plugins

# Lock provider versions
terraform init -lockfile=readonly

✅ terraform validate

Terminal - terraform validate 
# Validate configuration syntax
terraform validate

# JSON output (for CI/CD parsing)
terraform validate -json

# Validate a specific directory
terraform -chdir=environments/prod validate

Validation Output Examples

✓ Success! The configuration is valid.

╷
│ Error: Invalid reference
│ 
│   on main.tf line 15, in resource "aws_s3_bucket" "example":
│   15:   bucket = var.undefined_variable
│ 
│ A reference to a variable that has not been declared.
╵

📋 terraform plan (Deep Dive)

Terminal - terraform plan options 
# Basic plan
terraform plan

# Save plan to file
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Plan for destruction
terraform plan -destroy

# Compact output (less verbose)
terraform plan -compact-warnings

# Target specific resources
terraform plan -target=aws_s3_bucket.django_static

# Pass variables
terraform plan -var="environment=production"
terraform plan -var-file="prod.tfvars"

# Refresh-only mode (sync state with real resources)
terraform plan -refresh-only

# Replace a resource (force recreation)
terraform plan -replace=aws_instance.web

# Generate machine-readable output
terraform plan -json

# Parallelism control
terraform plan -parallelism=20

# Lock timeout
terraform plan -lock-timeout=60s
Plan Mode Command Use Case
Normal terraform plan Standard change preview
Destroy terraform plan -destroy Preview resource deletion
Refresh-Only terraform plan -refresh-only Detect drift from external changes
Replace terraform plan -replace=RESOURCE Force resource recreation
Targeted terraform plan -target=RESOURCE Plan specific resource only
📖 Understanding Plan Output Symbols
+
Create
New resource will be created
-
Destroy
Resource will be deleted
~
Update In-Place
Resource will be modified
-/+
Replace
Destroy then create (forces new)
≈
Update (forces replacement)
Will be computed during apply
→
Read
Data source will be read
💡 Pro Tip: Green (+) is safe, yellow (~) needs review, red (-) and orange (-/+) require careful attention!

🚀 terraform apply (Deep Dive)

Terminal - terraform apply options 
# Apply with confirmation prompt
terraform apply

# Apply saved plan (no prompt)
terraform apply tfplan

# Auto-approve (CI/CD only!)
terraform apply -auto-approve

# Apply with variables
terraform apply -var="environment=staging"
terraform apply -var-file="environments/staging.tfvars"

# Target specific resources
terraform apply -target=aws_rds_instance.database

# Replace specific resource
terraform apply -replace=aws_instance.web

# Limit parallelism (reduce API rate)
terraform apply -parallelism=5

# Refresh-only apply (sync state)
terraform apply -refresh-only

# Compact warnings
terraform apply -compact-warnings
⚠️
Auto-Approve Warning

Never use -auto-approve in interactive sessions! Reserve it for CI/CD pipelines where plan has been reviewed separately.

💥 terraform destroy (Deep Dive)

Terminal - terraform destroy options 
# Destroy with confirmation prompt
terraform destroy

# Preview destroy plan
terraform plan -destroy

# Auto-approve (DANGEROUS - CI/CD only!)
terraform destroy -auto-approve

# Target specific resources
terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web

# Destroy with variables
terraform destroy -var-file="prod.tfvars"

# Limit parallelism
terraform destroy -parallelism=5

# Lock timeout
terraform destroy -lock-timeout=60s
Consideration Details Recommendation
Dependency Order Resources destroyed in reverse dependency order Let Terraform handle order automatically
prevent_destroy Lifecycle meta-argument blocks destruction Use for critical resources (DBs, S3 buckets)
State After Destroy State file remains but resources are empty Commit state changes after destroy
Cost Implications Stops billing for destroyed resources Destroy dev/test environments after hours
Data Loss EBS, RDS, S3 data may be permanently lost Enable snapshots/backups before destroy
main.tf - prevent_destroy Example 
# Protect critical resources from accidental deletion
resource "aws_db_instance" "anihpj_postgres" {
  identifier = "anihpj-postgres-prod"
  # ... other configuration ...

  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true  # Terraform will error on destroy
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "django_static" {
  bucket = "anihpj-static-prod"

  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
  }
}

🔄 terraform refresh (State Synchronization)

📝
Deprecation Note (Terraform 1.5+)

terraform refresh is deprecated. Use terraform apply -refresh-only instead, which provides a plan preview before updating state.

Terminal - refresh commands 
# Legacy refresh (deprecated)
terraform refresh

# Modern approach: refresh-only plan
terraform plan -refresh-only

# Apply refresh-only changes
terraform apply -refresh-only

# Skip refresh during plan (faster, use when state is known good)
terraform plan -refresh=false
Scenario When to Refresh Command
Manual AWS Console changes Sync state with actual resources terraform apply -refresh-only
Drift detection Find unauthorized changes terraform plan -refresh-only
CI/CD optimization Skip refresh when state is trusted terraform plan -refresh=false
Large infrastructure Refresh can be slow with many resources Target specific resources

✅ terraform validate (Deep Dive)

Validation vs Plan terraform validate terraform plan
Speed Fast (local only) Slower (contacts providers)
Requires Init Yes (for modules) Yes
AWS Credentials Not required Required
Checks Syntax ✓ Yes ✓ Yes
Checks Resources Exist ✗ No ✓ Yes
Shows Changes ✗ No ✓ Yes
Best For CI pre-commit checks Pre-apply verification
Common Validation Errors
Error Cause Solution
Invalid reference Referencing undefined variable/resource Check spelling, ensure resource exists
Invalid expression Syntax error in interpolation Check brackets, quotes, function names
Missing required argument Required attribute not provided Add the missing argument
Unsupported block type Invalid block name in resource Check provider documentation
Cycle detected Circular dependency between resources Break cycle with depends_on or restructure

2.2 State Management Commands

State management is critical for maintaining synchronization between your configuration and real infrastructure. These commands let you inspect, modify, and troubleshoot state.

📊 State Management Architecture

📜

terraform state list

List all resources in state

🔍

terraform state show

Show attributes of a resource

✏️

terraform state mv

Move/rename resources in state

🗑️

terraform state rm

Remove resource from state

📥

terraform state pull

Download and output state

📤

terraform state push

Upload local state to backend

📜 terraform state list

Terminal - state list 
# List all resources
terraform state list

# Filter with wildcard
terraform state list 'aws_s3_bucket.*'
terraform state list 'module.networking.*'

# List resources from specific state file
terraform state list -state=backup.tfstate

Example Output (anihpj Project)

$ terraform state list
aws_db_instance.anihpj_postgres
aws_s3_bucket.django_static
aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block.django_static
aws_s3_bucket_versioning.django_static
aws_security_group.rds
aws_security_group.web
module.vpc.aws_internet_gateway.main
module.vpc.aws_subnet.private[0]
module.vpc.aws_subnet.private[1]
module.vpc.aws_subnet.public[0]
module.vpc.aws_subnet.public[1]
module.vpc.aws_vpc.main

🔍 terraform state show

Terminal - state show 
# Show all attributes of a resource
terraform state show aws_s3_bucket.django_static

# Show module resource
terraform state show 'module.vpc.aws_vpc.main'

# Show resource with index
terraform state show 'aws_subnet.private[0]'

Example Output

$ terraform state show aws_s3_bucket.django_static
# aws_s3_bucket.django_static:
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "django_static" {
    arn                         = "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-static-dev"
    bucket                      = "anihpj-static-dev"
    bucket_domain_name          = "anihpj-static-dev.s3.amazonaws.com"
    bucket_regional_domain_name = "anihpj-static-dev.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com"
    hosted_zone_id              = "Z3AQBSTGFYJSTF"
    id                          = "anihpj-static-dev"
    region                      = "us-east-1"
    request_payer               = "BucketOwner"
    tags                        = {
        "Application" = "JobPortal"
        "Environment" = "dev"
        "ManagedBy"   = "Terraform"
        "Name"        = "Django Static Files"
        "Project"     = "anihpj"
    }
}

✏️ terraform state mv (Moving Resources)

📝
When to Use state mv
  • Renaming a resource in your config
  • Moving resources into or out of modules
  • Refactoring resource organization
  • Preventing destroy/recreate when restructuring
Terminal - state mv 
# Rename a resource
terraform state mv aws_s3_bucket.static aws_s3_bucket.django_static

# Move resource into a module
terraform state mv aws_vpc.main module.networking.aws_vpc.main

# Move resource out of a module
terraform state mv module.old.aws_instance.web aws_instance.web

# Move between state files
terraform state mv -state=old.tfstate -state-out=new.tfstate \
  aws_instance.web aws_instance.web

# Dry run (preview only)
terraform state mv -dry-run aws_s3_bucket.old aws_s3_bucket.new

🗑️ terraform state rm (Removing from State)

⚠️
Important: Doesn't Delete Resources

state rm only removes the resource from Terraform's tracking. The actual infrastructure remains unchanged. Use this when you want Terraform to "forget" about a resource.

Terminal - state rm 
# Remove single resource from state
terraform state rm aws_s3_bucket.legacy

# Remove module and all its resources
terraform state rm module.old_networking

# Remove indexed resource
terraform state rm 'aws_subnet.private[0]'

# Dry run
terraform state rm -dry-run aws_instance.web

📥 terraform import (Adding Existing Resources)

Terminal - importing existing AWS resources 
# Import existing S3 bucket
terraform import aws_s3_bucket.existing_bucket my-existing-bucket

# Import RDS instance
terraform import aws_db_instance.database anihpj-postgres-prod

# Import EC2 instance
terraform import aws_instance.web i-1234567890abcdef0

# Import VPC
terraform import aws_vpc.main vpc-abc123

# Import with module path
terraform import module.networking.aws_vpc.main vpc-abc123
🦄
Django Migration Pattern

When migrating existing Django infrastructure to Terraform:

  1. Write resource blocks matching existing infrastructure
  2. Import each resource: terraform import aws_db_instance.db mydb-instance
  3. Run terraform plan to check for drift
  4. Adjust configuration until plan shows no changes

2.3 Workspace Commands

Workspaces allow you to manage multiple environments (dev, staging, production) with the same configuration but separate state files.

🗂️ Workspace Architecture: Same Code, Different State

⚡ Workspace Commands Cheat Sheet
terraform workspace list
Show all workspaces
terraform workspace new dev
Create workspace
terraform workspace select prod
Switch workspace
terraform workspace show
Current workspace
terraform workspace delete old
Remove workspace
Terminal - workspace commands 
# List all workspaces (* indicates current)
terraform workspace list

# Show current workspace
terraform workspace show

# Create new workspace
terraform workspace new staging
terraform workspace new production

# Switch to workspace
terraform workspace select production

# Delete workspace (must not be current)
terraform workspace delete staging

Workspace Example Output

$ terraform workspace list
  default
  dev
* staging
  production

$ terraform workspace select production
Switched to workspace "production".

$ terraform workspace show
production

Using Workspaces in Configuration

main.tf - Workspace-aware configuration 
# Access current workspace name
locals {
  environment = terraform.workspace
  
  # Environment-specific settings
  instance_types = {
    dev        = "t3.micro"
    staging    = "t3.small"
    production = "t3.medium"
  }
  
  instance_counts = {
    dev        = 1
    staging    = 2
    production = 3
  }
}

# Use workspace in naming
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "django_static" {
  bucket = "anihpj-static-${local.environment}"
}

# Dynamic sizing based on workspace
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count         = local.instance_counts[local.environment]
  instance_type = local.instance_types[local.environment]
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-web-${local.environment}-${count.index + 1}"
    Environment = local.environment
  }
}
Workspace S3 Bucket Name Instance Type Instance Count
dev anihpj-static-dev t3.micro 1
staging anihpj-static-staging t3.small 2
production anihpj-static-production t3.medium 3

🔀 Multi-Environment Strategies with Workspaces

Strategy Description Best For Limitations
Workspaces Same config, different state files Simple env separation, dev/staging/prod Can't have different providers per env
Directory Structure Separate folders: /dev, /prod Complete isolation, different configs Code duplication, harder maintenance
tfvars Files dev.tfvars, prod.tfvars Same code, variable-driven differences Still single state per workspace
Terragrunt Wrapper tool for DRY configs Complex multi-account, multi-region Additional tool, learning curve
Project Structure - Multi-Environment Pattern 
anihpj-terraform/
├── modules/                 # Reusable modules
│   ├── vpc/
│   ├── database/
│   └── compute/
├── environments/            # Environment-specific configs
│   ├── dev/
│   │   ├── main.tf          # References modules
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars
│   │   └── backend.tf       # Dev state backend
│   ├── staging/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars
│   │   └── backend.tf       # Staging state backend
│   └── prod/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── variables.tf
│       ├── terraform.tfvars
│       └── backend.tf       # Prod state backend
└── README.md
💡
Workspace vs Directory: When to Use Which?
  • Use Workspaces: Small teams, identical infra across envs, quick switching
  • Use Directories: Large teams, different configs per env, separate access controls
  • Hybrid: Directories for major envs (dev/prod), workspaces for feature branches

2.4 Advanced Commands

🎨

terraform fmt

Format configuration files

🔄

terraform refresh

Sync state with real resources

📈

terraform graph

Generate resource dependency graph

💡

terraform console

Interactive expression evaluator

🔐

terraform taint

Mark resource for recreation

📦

terraform providers

Show provider requirements

🎨 terraform fmt (Code Formatting)

Terminal - terraform fmt 
# Format current directory
terraform fmt

# Format and show changed files
terraform fmt -list=true

# Show diff of changes
terraform fmt -diff

# Check formatting (returns non-zero if changes needed)
terraform fmt -check

# Format recursively
terraform fmt -recursive

# Format specific file
terraform fmt main.tf

# Write output to stdout (don't modify files)
terraform fmt -write=false

💡 terraform console (Interactive Testing)

Terminal - terraform console 
# Start interactive console
terraform console

Console Session Example

$ terraform console

> var.environment
"dev"

> terraform.workspace
"default"

> aws_s3_bucket.django_static.arn
"arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-static-dev"

> length(var.availability_zones)
2

> [for az in var.availability_zones : upper(az)]
[
  "US-EAST-1A",
  "US-EAST-1B",
]

> cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, 1)
"10.0.1.0/24"

> exit

📈 terraform graph (Dependency Visualization)

Terminal - generating graphs 
# Generate DOT format graph
terraform graph

# Save to file
terraform graph > graph.dot

# Generate PNG (requires Graphviz)
terraform graph | dot -Tpng > graph.png

# Generate SVG
terraform graph | dot -Tsvg > graph.svg

# Plan graph (only planned changes)
terraform graph -type=plan

# Apply graph
terraform graph -type=apply

🔐 terraform taint / untaint (Force Recreation)

📝
Note: Deprecated Commands

taint and untaint are deprecated. Use terraform apply -replace=RESOURCE instead.

Terminal - modern replacement approach 
# Modern approach (Terraform 0.15.2+)
terraform apply -replace=aws_instance.web

# Multiple resources
terraform apply \
  -replace=aws_instance.web \
  -replace=aws_ebs_volume.data

# Legacy approach (deprecated)
terraform taint aws_instance.web
terraform untaint aws_instance.web

📦 terraform providers

Terminal - provider commands 
# Show required providers
terraform providers

# Show provider lock file
terraform providers lock

# Update provider platforms
terraform providers lock \
  -platform=linux_amd64 \
  -platform=darwin_amd64 \
  -platform=windows_amd64

# Mirror providers locally
terraform providers mirror /path/to/mirror

# Provider schema (JSON)
terraform providers schema -json

🔓 terraform force-unlock (State Lock Management)

⚠️
Danger Zone: Use With Extreme Caution!

force-unlock should only be used when you're certain no other Terraform process is running. Force unlocking while another process is active can corrupt your state!

Terminal - force-unlock commands 
# When you see this error:
# Error: Error acquiring the state lock
# Lock Info:
#   ID:        12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012
#   Path:      terraform.tfstate
#   Operation: OperationTypePlan
#   Who:       user@hostname
#   Created:   2024-01-15 10:30:00.000000000 +0000 UTC

# Force release the lock (use the ID from error message)
terraform force-unlock 12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012

# Skip confirmation prompt (even more dangerous!)
terraform force-unlock -force 12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012
Scenario Safe to Force-Unlock? Recommended Action
CI/CD job crashed mid-run ✓ Usually safe Wait 5 min, then force-unlock
Terraform process killed (Ctrl+C) ✓ Safe Force-unlock immediately
Network timeout during apply ⚠️ Check state first Verify state, then unlock
Another team member is running ✗ Not safe Wait for them to finish
Lock from different machine/user ⚠️ Investigate first Contact lock owner first

📤 terraform output (Query Output Values)

Terminal - terraform output commands 
# List all outputs
terraform output

# Get specific output
terraform output db_endpoint

# Get output as JSON
terraform output -json

# Get raw value (no quotes for strings)
terraform output -raw db_password

# Use in scripts
DB_HOST=$(terraform output -raw db_endpoint)
echo "Database: $DB_HOST"

# State file outputs (from remote state)
terraform output -state=terraform.tfstate

2.5 Debugging & Troubleshooting

🐛 Environment Variables for Debugging

Terminal (PowerShell) 
# Enable detailed logging (Windows PowerShell)
$env:TF_LOG = "DEBUG"
$env:TF_LOG_PATH = "terraform.log"

# Log levels: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR
$env:TF_LOG = "TRACE"

# Disable logging
$env:TF_LOG = ""

# Run with logging enabled
terraform plan
Terminal (Bash) 
# Enable detailed logging (Linux/macOS)
export TF_LOG=DEBUG
export TF_LOG_PATH=terraform.log

# One-liner
TF_LOG=DEBUG terraform plan

# Provider-specific logging
export TF_LOG_PROVIDER=DEBUG

# Core-only logging
export TF_LOG_CORE=DEBUG
Log Level Verbosity Use Case
ERROR Lowest Only errors
WARN Low Warnings and errors
INFO Medium General operational info
DEBUG High Detailed debugging info
TRACE Highest All internal operations
📊 TF_LOG Verbosity Levels (Visual Guide)
ERROR Minimal Output
Only critical failures | Production default
Error: Provider configuration not found
WARN Light Output
Errors + warnings | Good for CI/CD monitoring
Warning: Deprecated attribute usage detected
INFO Medium Output
General operations | Standard troubleshooting
aws_s3_bucket.static: Creating... | aws_s3_bucket.static: Creation complete
DEBUG High Output
Detailed execution steps | Recommended for debugging
[DEBUG] Plugin: AWS provider making API call: DescribeBuckets
TRACE Maximum Output
Everything | Core Terraform debugging | Very verbose
[TRACE] dag/walk: vertex dependencies: aws_s3_bucket.static | [TRACE] GRPCProvider: ApplyResourceChange
💡 Recommendation: Start with DEBUG for most issues, use TRACE only when HashiCorp support asks for it.

🔍 Common Debugging Scenarios

🌳 Terraform Troubleshooting Decision Tree
❓ Is it a syntax error?
✓ YES
→ Run terraform validate
→ Check for typos, missing brackets
→ Use terraform fmt to fix formatting
✗ NO
→ Continue to next question ↓
❓ Is it an AWS credentials error?
✓ YES
→ Run aws configure
→ Check ~/.aws/credentials
→ Verify IAM permissions
✗ NO
→ Continue to next question ↓
❓ Is it a state lock error?
✓ YES
→ Wait for other process to finish
→ Check who has the lock
→ terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID (careful!)
✗ NO
→ Continue to next question ↓
❓ Is it a provider/plugin error?
✓ YES
→ Run terraform init -upgrade
→ Check provider version constraints
→ Clear .terraform/ and re-init
✗ NO
→ Deep debugging required ↓
🔥 Advanced Troubleshooting Required
1. Enable Logging
$env:TF_LOG="DEBUG"
2. Target Specific Resource
terraform plan -target=resource
3. Check State
terraform state show resource
4. Test in Console
terraform console
Terminal - debugging techniques 
# Check state vs real resources
terraform refresh
terraform plan

# Identify specific resource issues
terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web

# Validate configuration
terraform validate

# Check provider configuration
terraform providers

# View state details
terraform state show aws_s3_bucket.django_static

# Check outputs
terraform output

# Force state unlock (use with caution!)
terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID
💡
Debug Workflow

1. Run terraform validate to check syntax

2. Enable TF_LOG=DEBUG for detailed output

3. Check state with terraform state list/show

4. Use terraform console to test expressions

5. Target specific resources to isolate issues

❌ Common Error Messages & Solutions

Error Message Cause Solution
Error: Plugin not installed Provider not downloaded Run terraform init or terraform init -upgrade
Error: Provider configuration not present Provider block missing or misconfigured Check provider block syntax and credentials
Error acquiring the state lock Another Terraform process holds lock Wait or use terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID
Error: No valid credential sources found AWS credentials not configured Run aws configure or set environment variables
Error: Resource already exists Resource exists but not in state Use terraform import to bring into state
Error: Cycle detected Circular dependency between resources Use depends_on or restructure dependencies
Error: Invalid count argument Count depends on unknown value Use -target to apply dependencies first
Error: Backend initialization required Backend config changed Run terraform init -reconfigure
Error: Unsupported Terraform Core version Version constraint not met Update Terraform or adjust required_version
Error: Module not installed Module source not downloaded Run terraform get or terraform init

🔥 Crash Log Analysis

Terminal - Crash log location and analysis 
# Crash logs are written to the current directory
# Look for files named: crash.log

# View crash log
cat crash.log

# Or on Windows
type crash.log

# Crash logs contain:
# - Terraform version
# - Go runtime version
# - OS/Arch info
# - Full stack trace
# - Recent log output
Crash Log Section What It Contains How to Use
Version Info Terraform & Go versions, OS, Arch Check compatibility, report bugs with this info
Stack Trace Call stack at time of crash Identify which component failed
Panic Message The actual error that caused crash Search for known issues online
Provider Info Active provider at crash time Determine if provider or core issue
Terminal - Reporting Terraform crashes 
# When reporting a bug to HashiCorp:
# 1. Capture Terraform version
terraform version

# 2. Enable verbose logging and reproduce
$env:TF_LOG = "TRACE"
$env:TF_LOG_PATH = "debug.log"
terraform plan

# 3. Include in your bug report:
# - crash.log file
# - debug.log file  
# - Minimal reproducer configuration
# - Expected vs actual behavior
# - Screenshots if applicable

# GitHub Issues:
# https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform/issues
📝
Before Filing a Bug Report
  • Search existing issues first
  • Try with latest Terraform version
  • Redact sensitive info from logs
  • Create minimal reproducible example

⚡ Command Performance & Timing Guide

Approximate execution times for typical anihpj project (50 resources)

terraform init
10-30s
First time only
terraform validate
~2s
Instant
terraform plan
15-45s
AWS API calls
terraform apply
2-10 min
Creates resources
terraform destroy
1-5 min
Deletes resources
terraform state list
<1s
Local operation
terraform output
<1s
Instant
💡 Pro Tips for Performance:
  • Use -parallelism=N to control concurrent operations (default: 10)
  • Enable -refresh=false in trusted environments to skip state refresh
  • Large infrastructures (100+ resources) may take significantly longer
  • Network latency to AWS regions affects timing

📚 Section 2 Summary: CLI & Commands

🎓 Section Complete! You've mastered 25+ CLI commands
⚡ Essential Commands
  • terraform init - Initialize project
  • terraform validate - Check syntax
  • terraform plan - Preview changes
  • terraform apply - Deploy infrastructure
  • terraform destroy - Remove all resources
📊 State Commands
  • terraform state list - List resources
  • terraform state show - Show details
  • terraform state mv - Rename/move
  • terraform state rm - Remove from state
  • terraform import - Add existing
🗂️ Workspace Commands
  • terraform workspace list - Show all
  • terraform workspace new - Create new
  • terraform workspace select - Switch
  • terraform workspace delete - Remove
🔬 Advanced Commands
  • terraform fmt - Format code
  • terraform console - Test expressions
  • terraform graph - Show dependencies
  • terraform output - Show outputs
  • terraform force-unlock - Unlock state
🐛 Debug Commands
  • TF_LOG=DEBUG - Enable logging
  • TF_LOG_PATH=file - Log to file
  • terraform validate - Check errors
  • terraform refresh - Sync state
  • apply -refresh-only - Modern refresh
💡 Key Takeaways - Remember These!
✓ Always run plan before apply
Use -out=tfplan to save plans for later execution
✗ Never use -auto-approve interactively
Reserve it for CI/CD pipelines only
⚡ Use workspaces or directories
For environment isolation (dev/staging/prod)
🐛 Enable TF_LOG=DEBUG for issues
First step in troubleshooting any problem
🔒 Use prevent_destroy for critical resources
Protect databases, S3 buckets from accidental deletion
📊 Use apply -refresh-only over refresh
Modern approach for state synchronization
🎯 What's Next?
Now that you've mastered the CLI, you're ready to dive into the AWS Provider and learn how to provision real infrastructure:
  • EC2 instances for Django application servers
  • RDS databases for PostgreSQL
  • S3 buckets for static files and media
  • VPC networking, security groups, load balancers
  • Complete production-ready Django infrastructure
← Previous: Terraform Fundamentals Next: AWS Provider Deep Dive →

3. AWS Provider Deep Dive

🌐 Overview: Building Production-Ready Django on AWS

The AWS Provider is the most comprehensive Terraform provider, supporting hundreds of AWS services. This section provides a complete, production-grade infrastructure blueprint for deploying the anihpj Django job posting application on AWS, covering everything from basic compute to advanced container orchestration and security services.

ℹ️
What You'll Learn

Master 18 comprehensive AWS service categories, each with practical Terraform configurations, Django integration examples, and production best practices. From EC2 instances to Kubernetes clusters, from S3 storage to real-time data streaming.

🏗️ Complete AWS Architecture for anihpj

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         🌍 Route 53 (DNS)                            │
│                    anihpj.com → CloudFront CDN                       │
└────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                                 │
┌────────────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ☁️ CloudFront Distribution                        │
│              (SSL/TLS Certificates from ACM)                         │
│                  Global Edge Caching                                 │
└────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                                 │
┌────────────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  🛡️ AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall)              │
│           Rate Limiting, SQL Injection, XSS Protection               │
└────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                                 │
┌────────────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────────┐
│              ⚖️ Application Load Balancer (ALB)                      │
│         Health Checks, SSL Termination, Path Routing                 │
└──────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────┘
       │                                                 │
┌──────▼─────────┐                              ┌────────▼───────────┐
│  EC2 Instances │                              │  ECS/EKS Clusters  │
│  Django App    │◄────────┐                    │  Containerized     │
│  (Auto Scaling)│         │                    │  Django Services   │
└────┬───────────┘         │                    └────────┬───────────┘
     │                     │                             │
     │    ┌────────────────▼─────────────────┐          │
     │    │   🔐 IAM Roles & Policies        │          │
     │    │   Secrets Manager + SSM          │          │
     │    │   (Credentials & Config)         │          │
     │    └──────────────────────────────────┘          │
     │                                                   │
┌────▼───────────────────────────────────────────────────▼───────────┐
│                        📊 Data Layer                                │
│                                                                     │
│  🗄️ RDS PostgreSQL     📦 S3 Buckets        ⚡ ElastiCache Redis   │
│  (Multi-AZ, Backups)   (Media, Static)      (Sessions, Cache)      │
│                                                                     │
│  📮 SQS Queues         🔔 SNS Topics        📨 EventBridge          │
│  (Background Jobs)     (Notifications)       (Event-Driven)         │
│                                                                     │
│  🔍 DynamoDB            🌊 Kinesis           🐝 Step Functions       │
│  (NoSQL Sessions)      (Real-time Data)      (Workflows)            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                 │
┌────────────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 📡 Monitoring & Analytics Layer                      │
│                                                                     │
│  📊 CloudWatch          🔍 Athena + Glue    🔐 AWS Cognito          │
│  (Logs, Metrics)        (Data Analytics)     (User Auth)            │
│                                                                     │
│  🚀 Lambda Functions    🌐 API Gateway      📊 Kinesis Analytics    │
│  (Serverless Tasks)     (REST APIs)          (Streaming)            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📋 Complete Service Breakdown (18 Categories)

Category Key Services Use Case
3.1 Provider Configuration AWS Provider Setup, Multi-region Foundation setup
3.2 VPC & Networking VPC, Subnets, Security Groups, NAT Network isolation
3.3 EC2 & Compute EC2 Instances, Auto Scaling, AMI Application servers
3.4 RDS Database PostgreSQL, Multi-AZ, Read Replicas Primary database
3.5 S3 Storage Buckets, Lifecycle, Versioning Media & static files
3.6 Load Balancing ALB, Target Groups, Health Checks Traffic distribution
3.7 ElastiCache Redis, Cluster Mode, Replication Session & caching
3.8 IAM & Security Roles, Policies, Instance Profiles Access control
3.9 Route 53 & DNS Hosted Zones, Records, Health Checks Domain management
3.10 CloudFront CDN Distributions, Origins, Cache Global content delivery
3.11 ACM & SSL Certificates, Validation, Renewal HTTPS encryption
3.12 CloudWatch Logs, Metrics, Alarms, Dashboards Monitoring & alerting
3.13 Lambda & Serverless Functions, Triggers, Layers Event-driven tasks
3.14 SQS & SNS Queues, Topics, Subscriptions Async messaging
3.15 Container Services ECS, ECR, Fargate, Task Definitions Docker containers
🆕 3.16 EKS & Kubernetes Clusters, Node Groups, IRSA, Fargate K8s orchestration
🆕 3.17 Secrets Manager & SSM Secrets, Parameters, Rotation, KMS Secure credentials
3.18 Additional Services DynamoDB, EventBridge, WAF, Cognito, API Gateway, Step Functions, Kinesis, Athena, Glue Advanced features

🎯 Production-Ready

Multi-AZ deployments, auto-scaling, disaster recovery, and high availability configurations included

🐍 Django-Focused

Every example tailored for anihpj Django app - models, views, settings integration provided

🔒 Security First

IAM best practices, secrets management, encryption at rest/transit, WAF rules, and compliance

💰 Cost-Optimized

Spot instances, reserved capacity, lifecycle policies, and budget-friendly configurations

💡
Recommended Learning Path
  1. Foundation (Start Here): Provider Config → VPC → EC2 → RDS → S3
  2. Production Essentials: Load Balancing → IAM → CloudWatch → Secrets Manager
  3. Performance: ElastiCache → CloudFront → Route 53
  4. Modern Architecture: ECS/EKS → Lambda → SQS/SNS → EventBridge
  5. Advanced Topics: Step Functions → Kinesis → Athena/Glue → API Gateway
🐍
Prerequisites for Following This Guide
  • Basic understanding of Django framework and Python
  • AWS account with appropriate permissions (AdministratorAccess or custom policy)
  • Terraform installed (v1.5.0+) - see Section 1 for installation
  • AWS CLI configured with credentials
  • Familiarity with command line and basic networking concepts

🏗️ Choose Your Django Deployment Architecture

STARTER
🚀 Simple EC2
Perfect for MVPs and small projects
  • ✓ Single EC2 Instance
  • ✓ RDS PostgreSQL
  • ✓ S3 for Static Files
  • ✓ Route 53 + ACM
  • ✓ CloudWatch Monitoring
💰 Monthly Cost
~$50-80
t3.micro + db.t3.micro
Best For: Personal projects, prototypes, low-traffic apps (<1K users)
RECOMMENDED
⚖️ High Availability
Production-ready with redundancy
  • ✓ ALB + Auto Scaling (2-10 instances)
  • ✓ RDS Multi-AZ + Read Replicas
  • ✓ ElastiCache Redis
  • ✓ CloudFront CDN
  • ✓ SQS + Celery
  • ✓ Secrets Manager + WAF
💰 Monthly Cost
~$300-500
t3.medium + Multi-AZ
Best For: Growing businesses, SaaS products, 10K-100K users
ENTERPRISE
🚀 Kubernetes (EKS)
Microservices-ready architecture
  • ✓ EKS Cluster + Fargate
  • ✓ Multi-Region Deployment
  • ✓ Aurora Serverless v2
  • ✓ ElastiCache + DynamoDB
  • ✓ Kinesis Data Streams
  • ✓ Full Observability Stack
💰 Monthly Cost
~$1K-3K
EKS + Managed nodes
Best For: Large enterprises, 100K+ users, complex microservices

3.1 Provider Configuration

provider.tf - Comprehensive AWS Configuration 
# =========================================
# AWS Provider Configuration for anihpj
# =========================================

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

# Primary region provider
provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  # Assume role for cross-account access
  assume_role {
    role_arn     = var.assume_role_arn
    session_name = "TerraformSession"
    external_id  = var.external_id
  }

  # Default tags applied to ALL resources
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "anihpj"
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
      Team        = "DevOps"
      CostCenter  = "Engineering"
    }
  }

  # Retry configuration
  retry_mode  = "standard"
  max_retries = 3
}

# Secondary region (for disaster recovery)
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "dr"
  region = "us-west-2"

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "anihpj"
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
      Purpose     = "Disaster Recovery"
    }
  }
}

# Global resources (CloudFront, Route53, IAM)
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "global"
  region = "us-east-1"

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "anihpj"
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
      Scope       = "Global"
    }
  }
}

Authentication Methods

🔑

Environment Variables

AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

📄

Shared Credentials

~/.aws/credentials file with profiles

🎭

IAM Role

Instance profile or assumed role

🔐

SSO

AWS IAM Identity Center integration

Terminal - Authentication setup 
# Method 1: Environment variables (PowerShell)
$env:AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
$env:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = "wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
$env:AWS_DEFAULT_REGION = "us-east-1"

# Method 2: AWS CLI profile
aws configure --profile anihpj-dev

# Use profile in provider
# provider "aws" { profile = "anihpj-dev" }

# Method 3: SSO login
aws sso login --profile anihpj-prod

# Verify authentication
aws sts get-caller-identity

3.2 VPC & Networking

A well-designed VPC is the foundation of secure Django deployments. Here's a production-ready network architecture:

🏗️ anihpj Network Architecture

vpc.tf - Production VPC for anihpj 
# =========================================
# VPC Configuration for anihpj Job Portal
# =========================================

# VPC
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.vpc_cidr
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-vpc-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Internet Gateway
resource "aws_internet_gateway" "main" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-igw-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Public Subnets
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count                   = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block              = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index + 1)
  availability_zone       = var.availability_zones[count.index]
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-public-${count.index + 1}-${var.environment}"
    Type = "Public"
  }
}

# Private Subnets (for Django application)
resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count             = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index + 10)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-private-${count.index + 1}-${var.environment}"
    Type = "Private"
  }
}

# Database Subnets
resource "aws_subnet" "database" {
  count             = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index + 20)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-database-${count.index + 1}-${var.environment}"
    Type = "Database"
  }
}

# Elastic IPs for NAT Gateways
resource "aws_eip" "nat" {
  count  = length(var.availability_zones)
  domain = "vpc"

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-nat-eip-${count.index + 1}-${var.environment}"
  }

  depends_on = [aws_internet_gateway.main]
}

# NAT Gateways
resource "aws_nat_gateway" "main" {
  count         = length(var.availability_zones)
  allocation_id = aws_eip.nat[count.index].id
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public[count.index].id

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-nat-${count.index + 1}-${var.environment}"
  }

  depends_on = [aws_internet_gateway.main]
}

# Route Tables
resource "aws_route_table" "public" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  route {
    cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
    gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.main.id
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-public-rt-${var.environment}"
  }
}

resource "aws_route_table" "private" {
  count  = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  route {
    cidr_block     = "0.0.0.0/0"
    nat_gateway_id = aws_nat_gateway.main[count.index].id
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-private-rt-${count.index + 1}-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Route Table Associations
resource "aws_route_table_association" "public" {
  count          = length(var.availability_zones)
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.public[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.public.id
}

resource "aws_route_table_association" "private" {
  count          = length(var.availability_zones)
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.private[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.private[count.index].id
}

# DB Subnet Group
resource "aws_db_subnet_group" "main" {
  name       = "anihpj-db-subnet-group-${var.environment}"
  subnet_ids = aws_subnet.database[*].id

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-db-subnet-group-${var.environment}"
  }
}

3.3 EC2 & Compute

Deploy Django applications on EC2 with auto-scaling, load balancing, and security best practices.

ec2.tf - Django Application Server 
# =========================================
# EC2 Configuration for anihpj Django App
# =========================================

# Latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["al2023-ami-*-x86_64"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }
}

# Security Group for Web Servers
resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name        = "anihpj-web-sg-${var.environment}"
  description = "Security group for Django web servers"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  # HTTP from ALB only
  ingress {
    description     = "HTTP from ALB"
    from_port       = 8000
    to_port         = 8000
    protocol        = "tcp"
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.alb.id]
  }

  # SSH (restricted to bastion/VPN)
  ingress {
    description = "SSH from bastion"
    from_port   = 22
    to_port     = 22
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = [var.bastion_cidr]
  }

  # Outbound internet access
  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-web-sg-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# IAM Role for EC2
resource "aws_iam_role" "ec2_role" {
  name = "anihpj-ec2-role-${var.environment}"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com"
        }
      }
    ]
  })
}

# Attach policies
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "ssm" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.ec2_role.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore"
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "s3_access" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.ec2_role.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess"
}

# Custom policy for Secrets Manager
resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "secrets_access" {
  name = "anihpj-secrets-access"
  role = aws_iam_role.ec2_role.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"
        ]
        Resource = aws_secretsmanager_secret.django_secret_key.arn
      }
    ]
  })
}

# Instance Profile
resource "aws_iam_instance_profile" "ec2_profile" {
  name = "anihpj-ec2-profile-${var.environment}"
  role = aws_iam_role.ec2_role.name
}

# Launch Template
resource "aws_launch_template" "web" {
  name          = "anihpj-web-lt-${var.environment}"
  image_id      = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type

  iam_instance_profile {
    name = aws_iam_instance_profile.ec2_profile.name
  }

  network_interfaces {
    associate_public_ip_address = false
    security_groups             = [aws_security_group.web.id]
  }

  user_data = base64encode(templatefile("${path.module}/user_data.sh", {
    environment    = var.environment
    db_host        = aws_db_instance.main.address
    redis_host     = aws_elasticache_replication_group.main.primary_endpoint_address
    s3_bucket      = aws_s3_bucket.static.id
    secret_arn     = aws_secretsmanager_secret.django_secret_key.arn
    aws_region     = var.aws_region
  }))

  tag_specifications {
    resource_type = "instance"
    tags = {
      Name        = "anihpj-web-${var.environment}"
      Application = "JobPortal"
    }
  }

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

# Auto Scaling Group
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "web" {
  name                = "anihpj-web-asg-${var.environment}"
  desired_capacity    = var.asg_desired
  max_size            = var.asg_max
  min_size            = var.asg_min
  vpc_zone_identifier = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  target_group_arns   = [aws_lb_target_group.web.arn]

  launch_template {
    id      = aws_launch_template.web.id
    version = "$Latest"
  }

  health_check_type         = "ELB"
  health_check_grace_period = 300

  instance_refresh {
    strategy = "Rolling"
    preferences {
      min_healthy_percentage = 50
    }
  }

  tag {
    key                 = "Name"
    value               = "anihpj-web-${var.environment}"
    propagate_at_launch = true
  }
}

# Scaling Policies
resource "aws_autoscaling_policy" "scale_up" {
  name                   = "anihpj-scale-up"
  scaling_adjustment     = 1
  adjustment_type        = "ChangeInCapacity"
  cooldown               = 300
  autoscaling_group_name = aws_autoscaling_group.web.name
}

resource "aws_autoscaling_policy" "scale_down" {
  name                   = "anihpj-scale-down"
  scaling_adjustment     = -1
  adjustment_type        = "ChangeInCapacity"
  cooldown               = 300
  autoscaling_group_name = aws_autoscaling_group.web.name
}
user_data.sh - EC2 Bootstrap Script 
#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Update system
dnf update -y

# Install Python 3.11 and dependencies
dnf install -y python3.11 python3.11-pip python3.11-devel postgresql-devel gcc

# Create application user
useradd -m -s /bin/bash django
mkdir -p /opt/anihpj
chown django:django /opt/anihpj

# Install application dependencies
pip3.11 install gunicorn django psycopg2-binary boto3 django-storages redis

# Get Django secret key from Secrets Manager
export DJANGO_SECRET_KEY=$(aws secretsmanager get-secret-value \
  --secret-id ${secret_arn} \
  --region ${aws_region} \
  --query 'SecretString' \
  --output text | jq -r '.DJANGO_SECRET_KEY')

# Create environment file
cat > /opt/anihpj/.env << EOF
DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings
DJANGO_SECRET_KEY=$DJANGO_SECRET_KEY
DB_HOST=${db_host}
DB_NAME=anihpj
REDIS_HOST=${redis_host}
AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME=${s3_bucket}
AWS_S3_REGION_NAME=${aws_region}
ENVIRONMENT=${environment}
EOF

# Setup systemd service
cat > /etc/systemd/system/gunicorn.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Gunicorn daemon for anihpj
After=network.target

[Service]
User=django
Group=django
WorkingDirectory=/opt/anihpj
EnvironmentFile=/opt/anihpj/.env
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/gunicorn --workers 3 --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 anihpj.wsgi:application

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable gunicorn
systemctl start gunicorn

3.4 RDS Database

PostgreSQL is the recommended database for Django. Configure RDS with high availability, backups, and security.

🦄
Django Database Configuration

The anihpj Job Portal uses PostgreSQL for the JobPost, Author, Skills, and Location models. RDS provides managed PostgreSQL with automated backups, Multi-AZ failover, and performance insights.

rds.tf - PostgreSQL for anihpj 
# =========================================
# RDS PostgreSQL for anihpj Job Portal
# =========================================

# Security Group for RDS
resource "aws_security_group" "rds" {
  name        = "anihpj-rds-sg-${var.environment}"
  description = "Security group for RDS PostgreSQL"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  # PostgreSQL from web servers only
  ingress {
    description     = "PostgreSQL from web servers"
    from_port       = 5432
    to_port         = 5432
    protocol        = "tcp"
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.web.id]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-rds-sg-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# DB Parameter Group (optimized for Django)
resource "aws_db_parameter_group" "main" {
  name   = "anihpj-postgres-${var.environment}"
  family = "postgres15"

  parameter {
    name  = "log_connections"
    value = "1"
  }

  parameter {
    name  = "log_disconnections"
    value = "1"
  }

  parameter {
    name  = "log_statement"
    value = "ddl"
  }

  parameter {
    name  = "shared_preload_libraries"
    value = "pg_stat_statements"
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-postgres-params-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Random password for database
resource "random_password" "db_password" {
  length  = 32
  special = false  # Avoid special chars for easier escaping
}

# Store password in Secrets Manager
resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret" "db_credentials" {
  name = "anihpj/db-credentials-${var.environment}"

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-db-credentials-${var.environment}"
  }
}

resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_credentials" {
  secret_id = aws_secretsmanager_secret.db_credentials.id
  secret_string = jsonencode({
    username = var.db_username
    password = random_password.db_password.result
    host     = aws_db_instance.main.address
    port     = 5432
    database = var.db_name
  })
}

# RDS Instance
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  identifier = "anihpj-postgres-${var.environment}"

  # Engine
  engine               = "postgres"
  engine_version       = "15.4"
  instance_class       = var.db_instance_class
  allocated_storage    = var.db_allocated_storage
  max_allocated_storage = var.db_max_storage  # Auto-scaling

  # Database
  db_name  = var.db_name
  username = var.db_username
  password = random_password.db_password.result

  # Network
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds.id]
  publicly_accessible    = false
  port                   = 5432

  # High Availability
  multi_az = var.environment == "production" ? true : false

  # Backup & Maintenance
  backup_retention_period   = var.environment == "production" ? 30 : 7
  backup_window             = "03:00-04:00"
  maintenance_window        = "Mon:04:00-Mon:05:00"
  copy_tags_to_snapshot     = true
  delete_automated_backups  = var.environment != "production"
  final_snapshot_identifier = var.environment == "production" ? "anihpj-final-${formatdate("YYYYMMDD", timestamp())}" : null
  skip_final_snapshot       = var.environment != "production"

  # Performance
  parameter_group_name          = aws_db_parameter_group.main.name
  performance_insights_enabled  = true
  performance_insights_retention_period = 7

  # Storage
  storage_type          = "gp3"
  storage_encrypted     = true
  kms_key_id            = aws_kms_key.rds.arn

  # Monitoring
  monitoring_interval = 60
  monitoring_role_arn = aws_iam_role.rds_monitoring.arn
  enabled_cloudwatch_logs_exports = ["postgresql", "upgrade"]

  # Protection
  deletion_protection = var.environment == "production" ? true : false

  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-postgres-${var.environment}"
    Application = "JobPortal"
  }

  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = false  # Set to true for production!
  }
}

# KMS Key for RDS Encryption
resource "aws_kms_key" "rds" {
  description             = "KMS key for anihpj RDS encryption"
  deletion_window_in_days = 7
  enable_key_rotation     = true

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-rds-key-${var.environment}"
  }
}

resource "aws_kms_alias" "rds" {
  name          = "alias/anihpj-rds-${var.environment}"
  target_key_id = aws_kms_key.rds.key_id
}

# IAM Role for Enhanced Monitoring
resource "aws_iam_role" "rds_monitoring" {
  name = "anihpj-rds-monitoring-${var.environment}"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          Service = "monitoring.rds.amazonaws.com"
        }
      }
    ]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "rds_monitoring" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.rds_monitoring.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AmazonRDSEnhancedMonitoringRole"
}
Environment Instance Class Storage Multi-AZ Backup
dev db.t3.micro 20 GB No 7 days
staging db.t3.small 50 GB No 14 days
production db.r6g.large 100 GB+ Yes 30 days

3.5 S3 Storage

S3 is essential for Django applications—storing static files, media uploads, and backups. Configure with proper security and CDN integration.

s3.tf - Django Static & Media Storage 
# =========================================
# S3 Configuration for anihpj Job Portal
# =========================================

# Static Files Bucket
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "static" {
  bucket = "anihpj-static-${var.environment}-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"

  tags = {
    Name    = "Django Static Files"
    Purpose = "STATIC_URL"
  }
}

# Media Files Bucket (user uploads - resumes, logos)
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "media" {
  bucket = "anihpj-media-${var.environment}-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"

  tags = {
    Name    = "Django Media Files"
    Purpose = "MEDIA_URL"
  }
}

# Versioning for media (protect user uploads)
resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "media" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.media.id

  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

# Lifecycle rules for old versions
resource "aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration" "media" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.media.id

  rule {
    id     = "cleanup-old-versions"
    status = "Enabled"

    noncurrent_version_expiration {
      noncurrent_days = 30
    }
  }

  rule {
    id     = "transition-to-glacier"
    status = "Enabled"

    filter {
      prefix = "archives/"
    }

    transition {
      days          = 90
      storage_class = "GLACIER"
    }
  }
}

# Server-side encryption
resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm = "AES256"
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "media" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.media.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
      kms_master_key_id = aws_kms_key.s3.arn
    }
  }
}

# Block public access (use CloudFront instead)
resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id

  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "media" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.media.id

  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}

# CORS for Django uploads
resource "aws_s3_bucket_cors_configuration" "media" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.media.id

  cors_rule {
    allowed_headers = ["*"]
    allowed_methods = ["GET", "PUT", "POST"]
    allowed_origins = [
      "https://${var.domain_name}",
      "https://www.${var.domain_name}"
    ]
    expose_headers  = ["ETag"]
    max_age_seconds = 3600
  }
}

# Bucket policy for CloudFront access
resource "aws_s3_bucket_policy" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid       = "AllowCloudFrontAccess"
        Effect    = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          Service = "cloudfront.amazonaws.com"
        }
        Action   = "s3:GetObject"
        Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.static.arn}/*"
        Condition = {
          StringEquals = {
            "AWS:SourceArn" = aws_cloudfront_distribution.main.arn
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  })
}

# KMS Key for S3 encryption
resource "aws_kms_key" "s3" {
  description             = "KMS key for anihpj S3 encryption"
  deletion_window_in_days = 7
  enable_key_rotation     = true

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-s3-key-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# IAM Policy for Django to access S3
resource "aws_iam_policy" "django_s3_access" {
  name = "anihpj-django-s3-access-${var.environment}"

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "StaticBucketAccess"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "s3:GetObject",
          "s3:PutObject",
          "s3:DeleteObject",
          "s3:ListBucket"
        ]
        Resource = [
          aws_s3_bucket.static.arn,
          "${aws_s3_bucket.static.arn}/*"
        ]
      },
      {
        Sid    = "MediaBucketAccess"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "s3:GetObject",
          "s3:PutObject",
          "s3:DeleteObject",
          "s3:ListBucket"
        ]
        Resource = [
          aws_s3_bucket.media.arn,
          "${aws_s3_bucket.media.arn}/*"
        ]
      },
      {
        Sid    = "KMSAccess"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "kms:Encrypt",
          "kms:Decrypt",
          "kms:GenerateDataKey"
        ]
        Resource = aws_kms_key.s3.arn
      }
    ]
  })
}
🦄
Django Settings Integration
# settings.py - Django S3 Configuration
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    ...
    'storages',
]

# S3 Static Files
AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = 'anihpj-static-dev-123456789'
AWS_S3_REGION_NAME = 'us-east-1'
AWS_S3_CUSTOM_DOMAIN = f'{AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME}.s3.amazonaws.com'

STATICFILES_STORAGE = 'storages.backends.s3boto3.S3Boto3Storage'
STATIC_URL = f'https://{AWS_S3_CUSTOM_DOMAIN}/static/'

# S3 Media Files
DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE = 'storages.backends.s3boto3.S3Boto3Storage'
AWS_LOCATION = 'media'
MEDIA_URL = f'https://{AWS_S3_CUSTOM_DOMAIN}/media/'

3.6 Load Balancing (ALB)

Application Load Balancer distributes traffic across Django instances with health checks, SSL termination, and path-based routing.

alb.tf - Application Load Balancer 
# =========================================
# Application Load Balancer for anihpj
# =========================================

# Security Group for ALB
resource "aws_security_group" "alb" {
  name        = "anihpj-alb-sg-${var.environment}"
  description = "Security group for ALB"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  # HTTP (redirect to HTTPS)
  ingress {
    description = "HTTP"
    from_port   = 80
    to_port     = 80
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  # HTTPS
  ingress {
    description = "HTTPS"
    from_port   = 443
    to_port     = 443
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-alb-sg-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Application Load Balancer
resource "aws_lb" "main" {
  name               = "anihpj-alb-${var.environment}"
  internal           = false
  load_balancer_type = "application"
  security_groups    = [aws_security_group.alb.id]
  subnets            = aws_subnet.public[*].id

  enable_deletion_protection = var.environment == "production"

  access_logs {
    bucket  = aws_s3_bucket.alb_logs.id
    prefix  = "alb"
    enabled = true
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-alb-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Target Group
resource "aws_lb_target_group" "web" {
  name     = "anihpj-web-tg-${var.environment}"
  port     = 8000
  protocol = "HTTP"
  vpc_id   = aws_vpc.main.id

  health_check {
    enabled             = true
    healthy_threshold   = 2
    unhealthy_threshold = 3
    timeout             = 5
    interval            = 30
    path                = "/health/"  # Django health check endpoint
    port                = "traffic-port"
    protocol            = "HTTP"
    matcher             = "200"
  }

  stickiness {
    type            = "lb_cookie"
    cookie_duration = 3600
    enabled         = true
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-web-tg-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# HTTPS Listener
resource "aws_lb_listener" "https" {
  load_balancer_arn = aws_lb.main.arn
  port              = "443"
  protocol          = "HTTPS"
  ssl_policy        = "ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06"
  certificate_arn   = aws_acm_certificate.main.arn

  default_action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.web.arn
  }
}

# HTTP to HTTPS Redirect
resource "aws_lb_listener" "http_redirect" {
  load_balancer_arn = aws_lb.main.arn
  port              = "80"
  protocol          = "HTTP"

  default_action {
    type = "redirect"

    redirect {
      port        = "443"
      protocol    = "HTTPS"
      status_code = "HTTP_301"
    }
  }
}

# ACM Certificate
resource "aws_acm_certificate" "main" {
  domain_name               = var.domain_name
  subject_alternative_names = ["*.${var.domain_name}"]
  validation_method         = "DNS"

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-cert-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Route53 records for certificate validation
resource "aws_route53_record" "cert_validation" {
  for_each = {
    for dvo in aws_acm_certificate.main.domain_validation_options : dvo.domain_name => {
      name   = dvo.resource_record_name
      record = dvo.resource_record_value
      type   = dvo.resource_record_type
    }
  }

  allow_overwrite = true
  name            = each.value.name
  records         = [each.value.record]
  ttl             = 60
  type            = each.value.type
  zone_id         = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
}

# Certificate validation
resource "aws_acm_certificate_validation" "main" {
  certificate_arn         = aws_acm_certificate.main.arn
  validation_record_fqdns = [for record in aws_route53_record.cert_validation : record.fqdn]
}

# ALB DNS Record
resource "aws_route53_record" "alb" {
  zone_id = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
  name    = var.domain_name
  type    = "A"

  alias {
    name                   = aws_lb.main.dns_name
    zone_id                = aws_lb.main.zone_id
    evaluate_target_health = true
  }
}

🔄 Request Flow Diagram

3.7 ElastiCache (Redis)

Redis provides caching, session storage, and Celery message broker for Django. Essential for scaling the anihpj Job Portal.

elasticache.tf - Redis for Django 
# =========================================
# ElastiCache Redis for anihpj
# =========================================

# Security Group for Redis
resource "aws_security_group" "redis" {
  name        = "anihpj-redis-sg-${var.environment}"
  description = "Security group for ElastiCache Redis"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  ingress {
    description     = "Redis from web servers"
    from_port       = 6379
    to_port         = 6379
    protocol        = "tcp"
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.web.id]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-redis-sg-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Subnet Group
resource "aws_elasticache_subnet_group" "main" {
  name       = "anihpj-redis-subnet-${var.environment}"
  subnet_ids = aws_subnet.private[*].id

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-redis-subnet-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Parameter Group (optimized for Django)
resource "aws_elasticache_parameter_group" "main" {
  name   = "anihpj-redis-params-${var.environment}"
  family = "redis7"

  parameter {
    name  = "maxmemory-policy"
    value = "allkeys-lru"
  }

  parameter {
    name  = "notify-keyspace-events"
    value = "Ex"  # Enable key expiration events for Celery
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-redis-params-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Redis Replication Group
resource "aws_elasticache_replication_group" "main" {
  replication_group_id = "anihpj-redis-${var.environment}"
  description          = "Redis cluster for anihpj Job Portal"

  node_type                  = var.redis_node_type
  num_cache_clusters         = var.environment == "production" ? 2 : 1
  port                       = 6379
  parameter_group_name       = aws_elasticache_parameter_group.main.name
  subnet_group_name          = aws_elasticache_subnet_group.main.name
  security_group_ids         = [aws_security_group.redis.id]

  engine                     = "redis"
  engine_version             = "7.0"
  
  automatic_failover_enabled = var.environment == "production"
  multi_az_enabled           = var.environment == "production"

  at_rest_encryption_enabled = true
  transit_encryption_enabled = true
  auth_token                 = random_password.redis_auth.result

  snapshot_retention_limit   = var.environment == "production" ? 7 : 1
  snapshot_window            = "03:00-05:00"
  maintenance_window         = "Mon:05:00-Mon:07:00"

  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-redis-${var.environment}"
    Application = "JobPortal"
  }
}

# Random password for Redis AUTH
resource "random_password" "redis_auth" {
  length  = 32
  special = false
}

# Store Redis credentials in Secrets Manager
resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret" "redis_credentials" {
  name = "anihpj/redis-credentials-${var.environment}"

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-redis-credentials-${var.environment}"
  }
}

resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "redis_credentials" {
  secret_id = aws_secretsmanager_secret.redis_credentials.id
  secret_string = jsonencode({
    host      = aws_elasticache_replication_group.main.primary_endpoint_address
    port      = 6379
    auth_token = random_password.redis_auth.result
    url       = "rediss://:${random_password.redis_auth.result}@${aws_elasticache_replication_group.main.primary_endpoint_address}:6379"
  })
}
🦄
Django Redis Configuration
# settings.py - Redis Configuration
import os

REDIS_URL = os.environ.get('REDIS_URL')  # From Secrets Manager

# Django Cache
CACHES = {
    'default': {
        'BACKEND': 'django_redis.cache.RedisCache',
        'LOCATION': REDIS_URL,
        'OPTIONS': {
            'CLIENT_CLASS': 'django_redis.client.DefaultClient',
            'CONNECTION_POOL_KWARGS': {'ssl_cert_reqs': None},
        }
    }
}

# Session Storage
SESSION_ENGINE = 'django.contrib.sessions.backends.cache'
SESSION_CACHE_ALIAS = 'default'

# Celery Configuration
CELERY_BROKER_URL = REDIS_URL
CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND = REDIS_URL
Use Case Redis Configuration Django Setting
Page Cache Database 0 CACHES['default']
Session Storage Database 1 SESSION_CACHE_ALIAS
Celery Broker Database 2 CELERY_BROKER_URL
JobPost Search Cache Database 3 Custom cache alias

3.8 IAM & Security

Implement least-privilege access with IAM roles, policies, and security best practices.

iam.tf - Security Configuration 
# =========================================
# IAM Configuration for anihpj
# =========================================

# Django Application Role
resource "aws_iam_role" "django_app" {
  name = "anihpj-django-role-${var.environment}"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          Service = [
            "ec2.amazonaws.com",
            "ecs-tasks.amazonaws.com"
          ]
        }
      }
    ]
  })

  tags = {
    Name = "anihpj-django-role-${var.environment}"
  }
}

# Secrets Manager Access Policy
resource "aws_iam_policy" "secrets_access" {
  name        = "anihpj-secrets-access-${var.environment}"
  description = "Access to Secrets Manager for Django secrets"

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "GetSecrets"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "secretsmanager:GetSecretValue",
          "secretsmanager:DescribeSecret"
        ]
        Resource = [
          aws_secretsmanager_secret.django_secret_key.arn,
          aws_secretsmanager_secret.db_credentials.arn,
          aws_secretsmanager_secret.redis_credentials.arn
        ]
      },
      {
        Sid    = "KMSDecrypt"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "kms:Decrypt"
        ]
        Resource = aws_kms_key.secrets.arn
      }
    ]
  })
}

# SES Email Policy (for Django email)
resource "aws_iam_policy" "ses_send" {
  name        = "anihpj-ses-send-${var.environment}"
  description = "Send emails via SES for job notifications"

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
