17.1 Complete CLI Reference

⚡ Terraform CLI Workflow

1️⃣
init
→
2️⃣
fmt
→
3️⃣
validate
→
4️⃣
plan
→
5️⃣
apply
🔄 Iterate: plan → apply → plan → apply
🚀 Core Commands
init → Initialize
plan → Preview
apply → Execute
destroy → Remove
🗄️ State Commands
state list → Show all
state show → Details
state mv → Rename
state rm → Remove
📁 Workspace Commands
workspace list → All
workspace new → Create
workspace select → Switch
workspace delete → Remove
🔧 Utility Commands
fmt → Format code
validate → Check syntax
console → Interactive
graph → Dependencies
Command Purpose Common Flags
terraform init Initialize working directory -upgrade, -reconfigure, -backend=false
terraform plan Preview changes -out=FILE, -target=ADDR, -var=VAR
terraform apply Apply changes -auto-approve, -parallelism=N
terraform destroy Destroy infrastructure -auto-approve, -target=ADDR
terraform fmt Format configuration -recursive, -check, -diff
terraform validate Validate configuration -json
terraform output Show outputs -json, -raw
terraform state list List state resources -state=FILE
terraform state show Show resource details ADDR
terraform state mv Move resource in state SOURCE DEST
terraform state rm Remove from state ADDR
terraform import Import existing resource ADDR ID
terraform workspace list List workspaces —
terraform workspace new Create workspace NAME
terraform workspace select Switch workspace NAME
terraform console Interactive console —
terraform graph Generate dependency graph -type=plan
terraform providers Show providers lock, mirror
terraform force-unlock Release state lock LOCK_ID
terraform taint Mark for recreation ADDR (deprecated, use -replace)

🌐 Environment Variables

Variable Purpose Example
TF_LOG Set log verbosity level TF_LOG=DEBUG
TF_LOG_PATH Write logs to file TF_LOG_PATH=./terraform.log
TF_INPUT Disable interactive prompts TF_INPUT=0
TF_VAR_name Set variable value TF_VAR_region=us-east-1
TF_CLI_ARGS Add CLI arguments TF_CLI_ARGS="-input=false"
TF_CLI_ARGS_plan Add args to specific command TF_CLI_ARGS_plan="-out=plan.out"
TF_DATA_DIR Change .terraform directory TF_DATA_DIR=./.tf-data
TF_WORKSPACE Set active workspace TF_WORKSPACE=staging
TF_IN_AUTOMATION CI/CD mode (reduces output) TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true
TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR Cache providers locally TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR=~/.tf-cache

📋 Most-Used Commands (Quick Copy)

# Initialize project
terraform init -upgrade
# Format all files
terraform fmt -recursive
# Plan with output file
terraform plan -out=tfplan
# Apply saved plan
terraform apply tfplan
# Target specific resource
terraform apply -target=aws_instance.web
# Force resource recreation
terraform apply -replace=aws_instance.web

17.2 HCL Syntax Quick Reference

📦
Types
string, number, bool, list, map, object
⚙️
Operators
+ - * / == != && || ? :
🔄
Loops
for, count, for_each, dynamic
📐
Functions
100+ built-in functions

📐 Function Categories

String
lower, upper, trim, split, join, format, replace
Collection
length, element, concat, flatten, keys, values, merge
Numeric
abs, ceil, floor, min, max, pow, log
Filesystem
file, fileexists, templatefile, abspath
Encoding
jsonencode, jsondecode, base64encode, yamlencode
Type Conversion
tostring, tonumber, tolist, toset, tomap
HCL Syntax Patterns 
# =========================================
# TYPES
# =========================================
string_val  = "hello"
number_val  = 42
bool_val    = true
list_val    = ["a", "b", "c"]
map_val     = { key1 = "val1", key2 = "val2" }
object_val  = { name = "test", count = 5, enabled = true }
tuple_val   = ["string", 123, true]

# =========================================
# OPERATORS
# =========================================
# Arithmetic: + - * / %
# Comparison: == != < > <= >=
# Logical: && || !
# Conditional: condition ? true_val : false_val

# =========================================
# STRING TEMPLATES
# =========================================
simple       = "Hello, ${var.name}!"
directive    = "Items: %{ for i in var.items }${i}, %{ endfor }"
heredoc      = <<-EOT
  Multi-line
  string content
EOT

# =========================================
# COLLECTION FUNCTIONS
# =========================================
length(list)              # Count elements
element(list, index)      # Get element
concat(list1, list2)      # Combine lists
flatten([[1,2], [3,4]])   # Flatten nested
keys(map)                 # Get map keys
values(map)               # Get map values
lookup(map, key, default) # Safe lookup
merge(map1, map2)         # Combine maps
contains(list, value)     # Check membership
distinct(list)            # Remove duplicates
sort(list)                # Sort list
reverse(list)             # Reverse list
slice(list, start, end)   # Get sublist

# =========================================
# STRING FUNCTIONS
# =========================================
lower("HELLO")            # "hello"
upper("hello")            # "HELLO"
title("hello world")      # "Hello World"
trim(" hello ")           # "hello"
split(",", "a,b,c")       # ["a", "b", "c"]
join("-", ["a", "b"])     # "a-b"
replace("hello", "l", "L") # "heLLo"
substr("hello", 0, 2)     # "he"
format("%s-%d", "num", 1) # "num-1"
formatlist("%s", list)    # Format each element
regex("[a-z]+", "abc123") # "abc"
regexall("[0-9]", "a1b2") # ["1", "2"]

# =========================================
# NUMERIC FUNCTIONS
# =========================================
abs(-5)                   # 5
ceil(4.3)                 # 5
floor(4.9)                # 4
min(1, 2, 3)              # 1
max(1, 2, 3)              # 3
parseint("10", 10)        # 10
signum(-5)                # -1
pow(2, 3)                 # 8
log(10, 10)               # 1

# =========================================
# FILESYSTEM FUNCTIONS
# =========================================
file("path/to/file")      # Read file contents
fileexists("path")        # Check file exists
filebase64("path")        # Base64 encode file
templatefile("tpl", vars) # Render template
pathexpand("~/file")      # Expand home dir
abspath("relative")       # Get absolute path
dirname("/a/b/c")         # "/a/b"
basename("/a/b/c.txt")    # "c.txt"

# =========================================
# DATE/TIME FUNCTIONS
# =========================================
timestamp()               # Current UTC time
formatdate("YYYY-MM-DD", timestamp())
timeadd(timestamp(), "24h")
timecmp(t1, t2)           # Compare times

# =========================================
# ENCODING FUNCTIONS
# =========================================
base64encode("hello")     # Encode to base64
base64decode("aGVsbG8=")  # Decode from base64
jsonencode(object)        # Convert to JSON
jsondecode(string)        # Parse JSON
yamlencode(object)        # Convert to YAML
yamldecode(string)        # Parse YAML
urlencode("hello world")  # URL encode

# =========================================
# TYPE CONVERSION
# =========================================
tostring(123)             # "123"
tonumber("42")            # 42
tobool("true")            # true
tolist(set)               # Convert to list
toset(list)               # Convert to set
tomap(object)             # Convert to map

# =========================================
# FOR EXPRESSIONS
# =========================================
# List comprehension
[for s in var.list : upper(s)]

# Map comprehension
{for s in var.list : s => upper(s)}

# With filtering
[for s in var.list : s if s != ""]

# With index
[for i, v in var.list : "${i}: ${v}"]

# =========================================
# SPLAT EXPRESSIONS
# =========================================
aws_instance.web[*].id              # All IDs from list
aws_instance.web[*].private_ip      # All IPs
var.users[*].name                   # All names

📦 Block Types

Block Type Purpose Example
terraform Settings, required providers, backend terraform { required_version = ">= 1.5" }
provider Configure provider (AWS, Azure, etc.) provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" }
resource Define infrastructure object resource "aws_instance" "web" { ... }
data Query existing resources data "aws_ami" "latest" { ... }
variable Input parameter definition variable "region" { type = string }
output Export values output "ip" { value = aws_instance.web.public_ip }
locals Define local values locals { env = "prod" }
module Call reusable module module "vpc" { source = "./modules/vpc" }

⚙️ Argument Types

Type Syntax Example Value
string Quoted text "hello world"
number Numeric (int or float) 42, 3.14
bool Boolean true, false
list(type) Ordered collection ["a", "b", "c"]
set(type) Unordered unique values toset(["a", "b"])
map(type) Key-value pairs { key = "value" }
object({...}) Structured type { name = string, age = number }
tuple([...]) Fixed-length sequence [string, number, bool]
any Accept any type variable "data" { type = any }

✨ Expression Syntax Patterns

INTERPOLATION
"Hello, ${var.name}!"
CONDITIONAL
condition ? true_val : false_val
FOR LIST
[for s in var.list : upper(s)]
FOR MAP
{for k, v in var.map : k => upper(v)}
SPLAT
aws_instance.web[*].public_ip
FILTER
[for s in list : s if s != ""]

17.3 AWS Provider Quick Reference

☁️ Most-Used AWS Resources

🌐
VPC
Networking
🖥️
EC2/ECS
Compute
🗄️
RDS
Database
📦
S3
Storage
⚖️
ALB
Load Balancer
🔐
IAM
Security
Service Common Resources Django Use Case
VPC aws_vpc, aws_subnet, aws_route_table Network isolation for anihpj
EC2 aws_instance, aws_security_group, aws_key_pair Django web servers
ECS aws_ecs_cluster, aws_ecs_service, aws_ecs_task_definition Container orchestration
RDS aws_db_instance, aws_db_subnet_group, aws_db_parameter_group PostgreSQL for JobPost models
ElastiCache aws_elasticache_cluster, aws_elasticache_replication_group Redis for Django cache/Celery
S3 aws_s3_bucket, aws_s3_bucket_policy, aws_s3_object Static files, media uploads
CloudFront aws_cloudfront_distribution, aws_cloudfront_origin_access_identity CDN for static/media
Route53 aws_route53_zone, aws_route53_record DNS for anihpj.com
ACM aws_acm_certificate, aws_acm_certificate_validation SSL certificates
ALB aws_lb, aws_lb_target_group, aws_lb_listener Load balancing
IAM aws_iam_role, aws_iam_policy, aws_iam_role_policy_attachment Service permissions
Secrets Manager aws_secretsmanager_secret, aws_secretsmanager_secret_version Django secrets
CloudWatch aws_cloudwatch_log_group, aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm Logging & monitoring
SES aws_ses_domain_identity, aws_ses_email_identity Email for notifications
ECR aws_ecr_repository, aws_ecr_lifecycle_policy Docker image registry

🔍 Common Data Sources

Data Source Purpose Common Use
aws_ami Find AMI IDs Latest Amazon Linux/Ubuntu
aws_availability_zones List available AZs Dynamic subnet creation
aws_caller_identity Current AWS account Account ID for ARNs
aws_region Current region Dynamic region references
aws_vpc Existing VPC info Reference existing VPC
aws_subnet Existing subnet info Deploy to existing subnet
aws_secretsmanager_secret Retrieve secret Get DB password
aws_ssm_parameter SSM parameter value Config values
aws_iam_policy_document Generate IAM JSON Build policies in HCL

⚙️ Provider Configuration Options

AWS Provider Configuration 
# Basic Configuration
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

# With Profile (from ~/.aws/credentials)
provider "aws" {
  region  = "us-east-1"
  profile = "production"
}

# With Assume Role
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name = "terraform-session"
    external_id  = "my-external-id"
  }
}

# With Default Tags (applied to all resources)
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Environment = "production"
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
      Project     = "anihpj"
    }
  }
}

# Multiple Providers (Aliased)
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "us-west"
  region = "us-west-2"
}

provider "aws" {
  alias  = "eu"
  region = "eu-west-1"
}

# Use aliased provider
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "backup" {
  provider = aws.us-west
  bucket   = "my-backup-bucket"
}

17.4 Version Constraints Reference

🔢 Semantic Versioning

5
MAJOR
Breaking
.
3
MINOR
Features
.
1
PATCH
Bug fixes
Constraint Meaning Example
= 1.5.0 Exact version Only 1.5.0
!= 1.5.0 Exclude version Any except 1.5.0
>= 1.5.0 Minimum version 1.5.0 or higher
> 1.5.0 Greater than Higher than 1.5.0
<= 2.0.0 Maximum version 2.0.0 or lower
~> 1.5.0 Pessimistic (patch) 1.5.x (>= 1.5.0, < 1.6.0)
~> 1.5 Pessimistic (minor) 1.x (>= 1.5, < 2.0)
>= 1.0, < 2.0 Range 1.0 to 1.x.x
Recommended Version Constraints 
# Terraform version
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"  # Minimum for import blocks
}

# Provider versions
required_providers {
  aws = {
    source  = "hashicorp/aws"
    version = "~> 5.0"    # 5.x, allows minor updates
  }
  random = {
    source  = "hashicorp/random"
    version = ">= 3.0.0"  # 3.0.0+
  }
}

# Module versions
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"  # 5.x
}
✅ DO
  • Use ~> for modules
  • Pin major versions
  • Test upgrades in dev first
❌ DON'T
  • Use >= alone in prod
  • Skip version constraints
  • Auto-upgrade in CI/CD

17.5 File Templates

📁 Recommended Project Structure

📂 terraform-project/
├── 📄 main.tf           # Resources, modules, data
├── 📄 variables.tf      # Input variable definitions
├── 📄 outputs.tf        # Output value definitions
├── 📄 providers.tf      # Provider configurations
├── 📄 backend.tf        # State backend config
├── 📄 versions.tf       # Version constraints
├── 📄 terraform.tfvars  # Variable values
├── 📄 .gitignore        # Git ignore rules
└── 📂 modules/           # Reusable modules
    └── 📂 vpc/
        ├── main.tf
        ├── variables.tf
        └── outputs.tf
📄
main.tf
Resources & Modules
⚙️
variables.tf
Input Variables
📤
outputs.tf
Output Values
🗄️
backend.tf
State Configuration
main.tf - Standard Template 
# =========================================
# TERRAFORM CONFIGURATION
# =========================================
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }

  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "env/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}

# =========================================
# PROVIDER CONFIGURATION
# =========================================
provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = var.project_name
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
    }
  }
}

# =========================================
# LOCAL VALUES
# =========================================
locals {
  name_prefix = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}"
  common_tags = {
    Owner      = var.owner
    CostCenter = var.cost_center
  }
}

# =========================================
# DATA SOURCES
# =========================================
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}

# =========================================
# RESOURCES
# =========================================
# Add your resources here...
variables.tf - Standard Template 
# =========================================
# REQUIRED VARIABLES
# =========================================
variable "project_name" {
  description = "Name of the project"
  type        = string

  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z0-9-]+$", var.project_name))
    error_message = "Project name must be lowercase alphanumeric with hyphens."
  }
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Environment name"
  type        = string

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

# =========================================
# OPTIONAL VARIABLES WITH DEFAULTS
# =========================================
variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

variable "owner" {
  description = "Resource owner"
  type        = string
  default     = "DevOps Team"
}

variable "cost_center" {
  description = "Cost center for billing"
  type        = string
  default     = "Engineering"
}

# =========================================
# COMPLEX TYPE VARIABLES
# =========================================
variable "vpc_config" {
  description = "VPC configuration"
  type = object({
    cidr_block           = string
    enable_dns_hostnames = bool
    enable_dns_support   = bool
  })
  default = {
    cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
    enable_dns_hostnames = true
    enable_dns_support   = true
  }
}

variable "tags" {
  description = "Additional tags"
  type        = map(string)
  default     = {}
}
outputs.tf - Standard Template 
# =========================================
# OUTPUTS
# =========================================

output "vpc_id" {
  description = "ID of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  description = "IDs of public subnets"
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].id
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  description = "IDs of private subnets"
  value       = aws_subnet.private[*].id
}

# Sensitive output
output "database_password" {
  description = "Database master password"
  value       = random_password.db.result
  sensitive   = true
}

# Formatted output
output "connection_string" {
  description = "Database connection string"
  value       = "postgresql://${var.db_username}@${aws_db_instance.main.endpoint}/${var.db_name}"
  sensitive   = true
}

🗄️ Backend Configuration Templates

S3 Backend (Recommended for AWS) 
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "project/env/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"  # For state locking
    
    # Optional: Use specific profile
    # profile        = "production"
    
    # Optional: Assume role for access
    # role_arn       = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}
Terraform Cloud Backend 
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "my-organization"
    
    workspaces {
      name = "my-project-prod"
      # OR use tags for multiple workspaces
      # tags = ["project:myapp", "env:production"]
    }
  }
}
Local Backend (Development) 
# Default - no configuration needed
# State stored in terraform.tfstate

# Explicit local backend with custom path
terraform {
  backend "local" {
    path = "state/terraform.tfstate"
  }
}

17.6 Comparison Tables

⚖️ IaC Tools Comparison

🟣
Terraform
Multi-Cloud
🟠
CloudFormation
AWS-Only
🔵
Pulumi
Real Code
🔴
Ansible
Config Mgmt

Terraform vs CloudFormation

Feature 🟣 Terraform 🟠 CloudFormation
Language HCL (HashiCorp Config Language) JSON / YAML
Multi-Cloud ✓ AWS, Azure, GCP, etc. ✗ AWS only
State Management External (S3, Terraform Cloud) Managed by AWS
Drift Detection ✓ terraform plan ⚠ Limited
Modularity ✓ Excellent (Registry) ⚠ Nested stacks
Learning Curve Moderate Moderate (AWS knowledge)
IDE Support ✓ Excellent ✓ Good
Cost Free (OSS) / Paid (Cloud) Free (AWS charges)

Terraform vs Pulumi

Feature 🟣 Terraform 🔵 Pulumi
Language HCL (declarative DSL) Python, TypeScript, Go, C#
Real Programming ⚠ Limited (functions only) ✓ Full language features
Testing terraform test (limited) ✓ Standard unit tests
Community/Ecosystem ✓ Massive (established) ⚠ Growing
Terraform Compatibility N/A ✓ Can use TF providers
Learning Curve Learn HCL Use existing language skills

Terraform vs Ansible

Feature 🟣 Terraform 🔴 Ansible
Primary Use Infrastructure provisioning Configuration management
Approach Declarative (what) Procedural (how) / Declarative
State Management ✓ State file tracks resources ⚠ Stateless (checks each run)
Agent Required No (API-based) No (SSH/WinRM)
Language HCL YAML
Idempotency ✓ Built-in ⚠ Module dependent
Best For Cloud infrastructure (AWS, Azure) Server config, app deployment
Combined Use ✅ Terraform provisions → Ansible configures

Terraform Editions Comparison

Feature OSS (Free) Cloud (Free Tier) Cloud (Team/Business)
State Management Self-managed ✓ Hosted ✓ Hosted + versioning
Remote Runs ✗ ✓ 500/mo ✓ Unlimited
Team Collaboration ✗ ⚠ Limited (5 users) ✓ Full
Private Registry ✗ ✓ ✓
Sentinel Policies ✗ ✗ ✓
SSO/SAML ✗ ✗ ✓
Cost Free Free $20/user/mo+

🎯 Which Tool Should You Choose?

🌐
Multi-cloud or AWS+Azure+GCP?
→ Terraform - Best multi-cloud support
☁️
AWS-only with native integration?
→ CloudFormation - Native AWS, no state management
💻
Team prefers Python/TypeScript/Go?
→ Pulumi - Real programming languages
🔧
Need server configuration + deployment?
→ Terraform + Ansible - Provision infra, then configure

17.7 Cost Reference Guide

💰 AWS Cost Estimation Reference

Quick reference for common AWS service pricing. Prices are approximate and vary by region. Use AWS Pricing Calculator for accurate estimates.

🖥️ EC2 Instance Pricing (us-east-1, On-Demand)

Instance Type vCPU Memory Hourly Monthly (~730h) Use Case
t3.micro 2 1 GB $0.0104 ~$7.60 Dev/Test
t3.small 2 2 GB $0.0208 ~$15 Light workloads
t3.medium 2 4 GB $0.0416 ~$30 Small Django app
t3.large 2 8 GB $0.0832 ~$61 Medium workloads
m6i.large 2 8 GB $0.096 ~$70 Production baseline
m6i.xlarge 4 16 GB $0.192 ~$140 Production

🗄️ RDS PostgreSQL Pricing (Single-AZ)

Instance Class vCPU Memory Monthly + Multi-AZ Use Case
db.t3.micro 2 1 GB ~$13 ~$26 Dev/Test
db.t3.small 2 2 GB ~$26 ~$52 Small apps
db.t3.medium 2 4 GB ~$52 ~$104 Staging
db.r6g.large 2 16 GB ~$145 ~$290 Production
db.r6g.xlarge 4 32 GB ~$290 ~$580 High traffic

☁️ Common Services Monthly Cost

⚖️ ALB
Base: ~$16/mo
+ LCU: $0.008/LCU-hour
Typical: $20-50/mo
🌐 NAT Gateway
Per AZ: ~$32/mo
+ Data: $0.045/GB
Consider: NAT Instance
📦 S3
Storage: $0.023/GB
Requests: $0.0004/1K GET
100GB: ~$2.30/mo
🚀 CloudFront
First 10TB: $0.085/GB
Requests: $0.01/10K
1TB: ~$85/mo
⚡ ElastiCache Redis
cache.t3.micro: ~$12/mo
cache.r6g.large: ~$109/mo
+ data transfer
🔐 Secrets Manager
Per secret: $0.40/mo
API calls: $0.05/10K
10 secrets: ~$4/mo

📡 Data Transfer Costs

Transfer Type Cost Notes
Data IN (to AWS) Free All incoming data is free
Data OUT (first 100GB/mo) Free Free tier included
Data OUT (10TB+) $0.09/GB Internet egress
Same Region (AZ to AZ) $0.01/GB Cross-AZ traffic
Same AZ (private IP) Free Use private IPs!
Cross-Region $0.02/GB Region to region
NAT Gateway $0.045/GB + hourly charge
CloudFront $0.085/GB Often cheaper than direct

☁️ Terraform Cloud Pricing

Plan Price Includes
Free $0 500 runs/mo, 5 users, remote state, VCS integration
Standard $0.00014/hr per resource Teams, RBAC, run tasks, policy enforcement
Plus Contact Sales SSO, audit logs, drift detection, concurrent runs
Enterprise Contact Sales Self-hosted, air-gapped, custom SLAs

💡 Cost Optimization Tips

✓ Reserved Instances: Save 30-60% with 1-3 year commitments
✓ Spot Instances: Save 70-90% for fault-tolerant workloads
✓ Right-sizing: Use CloudWatch to identify underutilized resources
✓ S3 Lifecycle: Move old data to Glacier (90% cheaper)
✓ Dev/Staging: Schedule shutdown during off-hours
✓ Infracost: Use for PR cost estimates before apply

📊 Sample Django App Monthly Cost (Production)

2x t3.medium EC2 (Web)
$60
RDS db.t3.small (Multi-AZ)
$52
ElastiCache cache.t3.micro
$12
ALB + NAT Gateway
$48
S3 + CloudFront (50GB)
$15
Route53 + ACM + Secrets
$5
💰 Estimated Monthly Total
~$192/mo
💡 Dev/Staging: ~$50/mo (single instances, no Multi-AZ) | With Reserved: ~$130/mo (1-year commitment)

18. Integrations & Tools

🔗

Integrations & Developer Tools

Supercharge Your Terraform Workflow

Connect Terraform with version control, IDEs, documentation generators, visualization tools, and cost management platforms for a complete infrastructure-as-code ecosystem.

📂 Version Control 💻 IDE Setup 📝 Auto-Docs 🗺️ Visualization 💰 Cost Tools
📂
Version Control
💻
IDE Integration
📝
Documentation
🗺️
Visualization
🔍
Drift Detection
💰
Cost Management

18.1 Version Control

📂 Git Workflow for Terraform

feature/*
Develop
→
PR + Plan
Review
→
main
Merge
→
Apply
Deploy
.gitignore - Terraform Projects 
# Local .terraform directories
**/.terraform/*

# .tfstate files
*.tfstate
*.tfstate.*

# Crash log files
crash.log
crash.*.log

# Exclude all .tfvars files (may contain secrets)
*.tfvars
*.tfvars.json

# Ignore override files
override.tf
override.tf.json
*_override.tf
*_override.tf.json

# Ignore CLI configuration files
.terraformrc
terraform.rc

# Ignore lock file for modules (keep for root)
# .terraform.lock.hcl

# Plan output files
*.tfplan
tfplan
plan.out

# Sensitive variable files
secrets.tf
*.secret.tf

🌿 Branch Strategy

main Production

Protected branch. All changes require PR + approval. Auto-applies on merge.

develop Staging

Integration branch for testing. Auto-applies to staging environment.

feature/* Development

Short-lived branches for new features. Plan runs on PR creation.

📝 Commit Message Standards

<type>(scope): description
feat
New resource
fix
Bug fix
refactor
Restructure
docs
Documentation
chore
Maintenance
security
Security fix
Examples:
feat(vpc): add NAT gateway for private subnets
fix(rds): correct security group ingress rules
refactor(modules): split networking into submodules

✅ Terraform PR Review Checklist

🔒 Security
  • No hardcoded secrets/credentials
  • IAM permissions follow least privilege
  • Security groups properly restricted
  • Encryption enabled where applicable
📋 Plan Output
  • Review all resource changes
  • No unexpected destroys/replaces
  • Verify resource naming convention
  • Check for drift in unchanged resources
💰 Cost Impact
  • Infracost diff reviewed
  • Instance sizes appropriate
  • No unnecessary resources
  • Reserved capacity considered
📦 Code Quality
  • terraform fmt applied
  • terraform validate passes
  • tflint checks pass
  • Variables properly documented

18.2 IDE Integration

💻 Terraform IDE Support

🔵
VS Code
Recommended
🟠
JetBrains
IntelliJ/PyCharm
🟢
Vim/Neovim
LSP Support
🟣
Emacs
terraform-mode
💻

VS Code

  • HashiCorp Terraform extension
  • Syntax highlighting
  • Auto-completion
  • Format on save
  • Go to definition
🧠

JetBrains

  • Terraform plugin
  • HCL language support
  • Refactoring tools
  • Resource navigation
📝

Vim/Neovim

  • vim-terraform plugin
  • ALE for linting
  • CoC for completion
  • terraform-ls LSP
.vscode/settings.json 
{
  "[terraform]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true,
    "editor.tabSize": 2
  },
  "[terraform-vars]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true
  },
  "terraform.languageServer.enable": true,
  "terraform.experimentalFeatures.validateOnSave": true,
  "terraform.codelens.referenceCount": true
}
~/.config/nvim/lua/lsp/terraform.lua - Neovim LSP 
-- Neovim Terraform LSP Configuration
-- Requires nvim-lspconfig and terraform-ls installed

local lspconfig = require('lspconfig')

lspconfig.terraformls.setup{
  cmd = { "terraform-ls", "serve" },
  filetypes = { "terraform", "terraform-vars", "hcl" },
  root_dir = lspconfig.util.root_pattern(".terraform", ".git"),
  settings = {
    terraform = {
      experimentalFeatures = {
        validateOnSave = true,
        prefillRequiredFields = true
      }
    }
  },
  on_attach = function(client, bufnr)
    -- Format on save
    vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd("BufWritePre", {
      buffer = bufnr,
      callback = function()
        vim.lsp.buf.format({ async = false })
      end
    })
  end
}

-- Optional: tflint integration
lspconfig.tflint.setup{
  cmd = { "tflint", "--langserver" },
  filetypes = { "terraform" }
}
~/.emacs.d/init.el - Emacs Terraform Mode 
;; Emacs Terraform Configuration
;; Install: M-x package-install RET terraform-mode

;; Package setup
(require 'package)
(add-to-list 'package-archives '("melpa" . "https://melpa.org/packages/"))
(package-initialize)

;; Install terraform-mode if not present
(unless (package-installed-p 'terraform-mode)
  (package-refresh-contents)
  (package-install 'terraform-mode))

;; Terraform mode configuration
(use-package terraform-mode
  :ensure t
  :mode ("\\.tf\\'" "\\.tfvars\\'")
  :hook ((terraform-mode . terraform-format-on-save-mode)
         (terraform-mode . lsp-deferred))
  :config
  (setq terraform-indent-level 2)
  (setq terraform-format-on-save t))

;; LSP support for terraform-ls
(use-package lsp-mode
  :ensure t
  :commands lsp
  :hook (terraform-mode . lsp-deferred)
  :config
  (setq lsp-terraform-server "terraform-ls")
  (setq lsp-terraform-ls-enable-show-reference t))

;; Company mode for completion
(use-package company-terraform
  :ensure t
  :after (company terraform-mode)
  :config
  (company-terraform-init))

18.3 Documentation Tools

📚 Auto-Documentation Pipeline

📄
.tf Files
→
⚙️
terraform-docs
→
📋
README.md
→
✅
Git Commit
📊
Markdown Table

Variables, outputs, and resources in clean tables

🔄
Auto-Inject

Insert docs between markers in existing files

🪝
Pre-commit

Auto-generate on git commit hooks

Terminal - terraform-docs 
# Install terraform-docs
brew install terraform-docs  # macOS
choco install terraform-docs # Windows

# Generate README for module
terraform-docs markdown table ./modules/vpc > ./modules/vpc/README.md

# Generate with config
cat << EOF > .terraform-docs.yml
formatter: markdown table
output:
  file: README.md
  mode: inject
sort:
  enabled: true
  by: required
settings:
  hide-empty: true
  indent: 2
EOF

terraform-docs ./modules/vpc

# Pre-commit hook for auto-docs
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/terraform-docs/terraform-docs
    rev: v0.16.0
    hooks:
      - id: terraform-docs-go
        args: ["markdown", "table", "--output-file", "README.md"]

📊 Diagram Generation

🖼️
Mermaid

Markdown-based diagrams in docs

📐
PlantUML

Architecture & sequence diagrams

🔄
Diagrams (Python)

Code-generated cloud diagrams

diagrams/architecture.py - Python Diagrams Library 
# pip install diagrams
from diagrams import Diagram, Cluster, Edge
from diagrams.aws.compute import ECS, Fargate
from diagrams.aws.database import RDS, ElastiCache
from diagrams.aws.network import ALB, CloudFront, Route53
from diagrams.aws.storage import S3

# Generate architecture diagram from Terraform structure
with Diagram("Django on AWS (Terraform)", show=False, direction="TB"):
    
    dns = Route53("Route 53")
    cdn = CloudFront("CloudFront")
    
    with Cluster("VPC"):
        lb = ALB("ALB")
        
        with Cluster("ECS Cluster"):
            ecs = [Fargate("Django 1"),
                   Fargate("Django 2")]
        
        with Cluster("Data Layer"):
            db = RDS("PostgreSQL")
            cache = ElastiCache("Redis")
    
    static = S3("Static Assets")
    
    dns >> cdn >> lb >> ecs
    ecs >> db
    ecs >> cache
    cdn >> static
README.md - Mermaid Diagram in Docs 
## Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "AWS Cloud"
        R53[Route 53] --> CF[CloudFront]
        CF --> ALB[Load Balancer]
        CF --> S3[S3 Static]
        
        subgraph "VPC"
            ALB --> ECS1[Fargate Task 1]
            ALB --> ECS2[Fargate Task 2]
            ECS1 --> RDS[(PostgreSQL)]
            ECS2 --> RDS
            ECS1 --> Redis[(ElastiCache)]
            ECS2 --> Redis
        end
    end
    
    User((User)) --> R53
```

## Module Dependencies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root[Root Module] --> vpc[VPC Module]
    root --> ecs[ECS Module]
    root --> rds[RDS Module]
    vpc --> ecs
    vpc --> rds
    ecs --> alb[ALB Module]
```

private_subnet_idsList of private subnet IDs

18.4 Infrastructure Visualization

🗺️ Terraform Visualization Tools

📈
terraform graph
Built-in • DOT format
FREE
🚀
Rover
Interactive • Docker
FREE
🗺️
Inframap
State-based • Clean
FREE
💥
Blast-Radius
Interactive • Web UI
FREE
Terminal - Visualization Commands 
# Built-in graph (requires Graphviz)
terraform graph | dot -Tpng > infrastructure.png
terraform graph | dot -Tsvg > infrastructure.svg

# Filtered graph
terraform graph -type=plan | grep -v "provider" | dot -Tpng > plan.png

# Using Rover (interactive)
# https://github.com/im2nguyen/rover
docker run --rm -it -p 9000:9000 \
  -v $(pwd):/src \
  im2nguyen/rover

# Using Inframap
# https://github.com/cycloidio/inframap
inframap generate ./terraform --raw | dot -Tpng > infra.png

# Using Blast-Radius (interactive)
# https://github.com/28mm/blast-radius
blast-radius --serve ./terraform

Feature terraform graph Rover Inframap Blast-Radius

Best For Quick debug Full exploration Clean diagrams Impact analysis

digraph {
    compound = "true"

        "[root] aws_vpc.main" [label = "aws_vpc.main", shape = "box"]
        "[root] aws_subnet.private" [label = "aws_subnet.private", shape = "box"]
        "[root] aws_subnet.public" [label = "aws_subnet.public", shape = "box"]
        "[root] aws_db_instance.main" [label = "aws_db_instance.main", shape = "box"]
        "[root] aws_ecs_cluster.main" [label = "aws_ecs_cluster.main", shape = "box"]
        
        # Dependencies
        "[root] aws_subnet.private" -> "[root] aws_vpc.main"
        "[root] aws_subnet.public" -> "[root] aws_vpc.main"
        "[root] aws_db_instance.main" -> "[root] aws_subnet.private"
        "[root] aws_ecs_cluster.main" -> "[root] aws_subnet.private"
    }
}

18.5 Drift Detection

🔄 Infrastructure Drift Detection

📋
State File
≠
☁️
Cloud Reality
=
⚠️
DRIFT!

Detect when infrastructure differs from Terraform state due to manual changes

🏢
TF Cloud

Built-in drift detection with scheduled runs

ENTERPRISE
🔍
driftctl

Open-source deep drift analysis

FREE
⏰
Scheduled Plan

CI/CD cron jobs with -refresh-only

DIY
Terminal - Drift Detection Commands 
# Built-in drift detection with refresh-only
terraform plan -refresh-only

# Check with detailed exit code
# Exit 0 = No drift, Exit 2 = Drift detected
terraform plan -refresh-only -detailed-exitcode

# Using driftctl (open-source)
# https://github.com/snyk/driftctl
driftctl scan

# driftctl with specific provider
driftctl scan --from tfstate://terraform.tfstate

# Compare state to actual resources
driftctl scan --to aws+tf --from tfstate+s3://bucket/state.tfstate
.github/workflows/drift.yml - Scheduled Drift Check 
# Scheduled drift detection workflow
name: Drift Detection

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 8 * * *'  # Daily at 8 AM UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  drift-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        environment: [staging, production]

    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
      issues: write

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Configure AWS
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Terraform Init
        working-directory: terraform/environments/${{ matrix.environment }}
        run: terraform init

      - name: Check for Drift
        id: drift
        working-directory: terraform/environments/${{ matrix.environment }}
        run: |
          terraform plan -refresh-only -detailed-exitcode -no-color 2>&1 | tee plan.txt
          echo "exitcode=$?" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Create Issue on Drift
        if: steps.drift.outputs.exitcode == '2'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const plan = fs.readFileSync('terraform/environments/${{ matrix.environment }}/plan.txt', 'utf8');

            github.rest.issues.create({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              title: `🔄 Drift Detected: ${{ matrix.environment }}`,
              body: `Infrastructure drift detected in **${{ matrix.environment }}**.\n\n\`\`\`\n${plan.slice(0, 60000)}\n\`\`\``,
              labels: ['drift', '${{ matrix.environment }}']
            });

🔍 Types of Infrastructure Drift

🚨
Critical Drift
  • Security group rules modified
  • IAM policies changed
  • Encryption settings disabled
  • Public access enabled
Immediate Action Required
⚠️
Configuration Drift
  • Instance types changed
  • Scaling parameters modified
  • Resource tags altered
  • Timeout values adjusted
Review & Reconcile
ℹ️
Cosmetic Drift
  • Description fields updated
  • Non-functional tags added
  • Display names changed
  • Metadata modifications
Low Priority
🔄 Drift Response Workflow
Detect
→
Classify
→
Decide
→
Apply / Import

📈

AWS POST-APPLY
Terminal - Infracost 
# Install Infracost
brew install infracost  # macOS
choco install infracost # Windows

# Register for API key
infracost auth login

# Generate cost estimate
infracost breakdown --path ./terraform

# Compare costs (PR workflow)
infracost diff \
  --path ./terraform \
  --compare-to infracost-base.json

# Output formats
infracost breakdown --path . --format html > cost-report.html
infracost breakdown --path . --format json > cost.json

# GitHub Actions integration
- name: Infracost
  uses: infracost/actions/comment@v1
  with:
    path: terraform/
    github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
Infracost Output
Project: anihpj-terraform
📦 aws_db_instance.main RDS db.r6g.large $157.50/mo 📦 aws_elasticache_replication_group Redis cache.r6g.large $292.00/mo 📦 aws_ecs_service.django Fargate 2vCPU 4GB $32.12/mo 📦 aws_cloudfront_distribution 1TB data transfer $85.00/mo
TOTAL (14 resources) $566.62/month

🏢 Terraform Cloud Cost Estimation

Built-in cost estimation in Terraform Cloud (Team & Governance tier)

  • Automatic cost estimation on every plan
  • Monthly cost projections displayed in UI
  • Cost policies with Sentinel (require approval above threshold)
  • Supports AWS, Azure, and GCP resources
sentinel/cost-policy.sentinel - Cost Threshold Policy 
# Sentinel policy for Terraform Cloud cost estimation
# Requires Team & Governance tier

import "tfrun"
import "decimal"

# Maximum allowed monthly cost increase
max_monthly_increase = decimal.new(500)

# Get the cost estimate from the run
cost_estimate = tfrun.cost_estimate

# Check if cost estimation is available
cost_available = rule {
    cost_estimate is not null and
    cost_estimate.delta_monthly_cost is not null
}

# Validate cost increase is within threshold
cost_within_limit = rule when cost_available {
    decimal.new(cost_estimate.delta_monthly_cost).lte(max_monthly_increase)
}

# Main rule - soft-mandatory (can be overridden)
main = rule {
    cost_within_limit
}

# Print helpful message
print("Monthly cost change: $", cost_estimate.delta_monthly_cost)
print("Maximum allowed: $", max_monthly_increase)

☁️ AWS Cost Explorer Integration

📍 Cost Allocation Tags
Track costs per environment/project
📊 Budgets & Alerts
Automated notifications on spend
📈 Anomaly Detection
ML-powered spend monitoring
cost-explorer.tf - AWS Cost Management Resources 
# ============================================
# AWS Cost Explorer Integration with Terraform
# ============================================

# Cost Allocation Tags (applied to all resources)
locals {
  cost_tags = {
    Project     = var.project_name
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    CostCenter  = var.cost_center
    Owner       = var.owner_email
  }
}

# Default tags for all resources (AWS Provider 3.38+)
provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
  
  default_tags {
    tags = local.cost_tags
  }
}

# AWS Budget with Alerts
resource "aws_budgets_budget" "monthly" {
  name              = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}-monthly"
  budget_type       = "COST"
  limit_amount      = var.monthly_budget_limit
  limit_unit        = "USD"
  time_unit         = "MONTHLY"
  time_period_start = formatdate("YYYY-MM-01_00:00", timestamp())

  cost_filter {
    name   = "TagKeyValue"
    values = ["user:Project$${var.project_name}"]
  }

  # 80% threshold alert
  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 80
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = var.budget_alert_emails
  }

  # 100% threshold alert
  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 100
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = var.budget_alert_emails
  }

  # Forecasted overspend alert
  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 100
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "FORECASTED"
    subscriber_email_addresses = var.budget_alert_emails
  }
}

# Cost Anomaly Detection
resource "aws_ce_anomaly_monitor" "project" {
  name              = "${var.project_name}-anomaly-monitor"
  monitor_type      = "DIMENSIONAL"
  monitor_dimension = "SERVICE"
}

resource "aws_ce_anomaly_subscription" "alerts" {
  name      = "${var.project_name}-anomaly-alerts"
  frequency = "DAILY"

  monitor_arn_list = [
    aws_ce_anomaly_monitor.project.arn
  ]

  subscriber {
    type    = "EMAIL"
    address = var.owner_email
  }

  # Alert when anomaly impact exceeds $50
  threshold_expression {
    dimension {
      key           = "ANOMALY_TOTAL_IMPACT_ABSOLUTE"
      values        = ["50"]
      match_options = ["GREATER_THAN_OR_EQUAL"]
    }
  }
}

# Variables
variable "monthly_budget_limit" {
  description = "Monthly budget limit in USD"
  type        = string
  default     = "1000"
}

variable "budget_alert_emails" {
  description = "Email addresses for budget alerts"
  type        = list(string)
}

variable "cost_center" {
  description = "Cost center for billing"
  type        = string
}

variable "owner_email" {
  description = "Owner email for notifications"
  type        = string
}

💡 Cost Optimization Tips

📊
Right-Size Resources

Use aws_instance with appropriate sizes. Monitor utilization before upgrading.

💰
Reserved Instances

Use aws_ec2_capacity_reservation for predictable workloads. Up to 72% savings.

⚡
Spot Instances

Use aws_spot_instance_request for fault-tolerant workloads. Up to 90% savings.

🗑️
Cleanup Unused

Remove orphaned EBS volumes, old snapshots, and unused Elastic IPs.

🏷️
Tag Everything

Use cost allocation tags consistently. Enable in AWS Cost Explorer for tracking.

⏰
Schedule Resources

Stop dev/test resources off-hours. Use aws_autoscaling_schedule for automation.

📋 Monthly Cost Review Checklist

Appendix A: Terraform Version History

A.1 Major Version Changes

Version Release Key Features
1.8 2024 Provider-defined functions, improved error messages
1.7 2024 Removed state block, test mocking improvements
1.6 2023 terraform test GA, S3 native state locking
1.5 2023 import blocks, check blocks, -generate-config-out
1.4 2023 terraform_data resource, null_resource replacement
1.3 2022 moved blocks, optional object attributes
1.2 2022 precondition/postcondition, replace_triggered_by
1.1 2021 -refresh-only apply, improved error messages
1.0 2021 Stable release, breaking change freeze
0.15 2021 Remote state data source improvements
0.14 2020 sensitive variables, provider lock files
0.13 2020 count/for_each on modules, depends_on for modules
0.12 2019 HCL2, first-class expressions, for loops

A.2 Upgrade Guides

Terminal - Safe Upgrade Process 
# Step 1: Check current version
terraform version

# Step 2: Read upgrade guide
# https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/upgrade-guides

# Step 3: Backup state
terraform state pull > backup.tfstate

# Step 4: Upgrade Terraform binary
tfenv install 1.8.0
tfenv use 1.8.0

# Step 5: Update version constraint
# required_version = ">= 1.8.0"

# Step 6: Re-initialize
terraform init -upgrade

# Step 7: Plan (no changes expected)
terraform plan

# Step 8: Run any required migrations
# (version-specific commands if needed)

# Step 9: Verify state
terraform state list

Appendix B: AWS Provider Version History

B.1 Major Version Breaking Changes

Version Breaking Changes Migration Notes
v5.x
  • Default tags propagation changes
  • S3 bucket argument deprecations
  • EC2 instance type validation
 Use separate S3 bucket resources for lifecycle, versioning, etc.
v4.x
  • S3 bucket split into multiple resources
  • Provider default_tags support
 Migrate aws_s3_bucket to v4 structure
v3.x
  • Terraform 0.12+ required
  • Region validation
 Update HCL syntax from 0.11
S3 Bucket v3 → v4/v5 Migration 
# =========================================
# OLD (v3.x) - All-in-one S3 bucket
# =========================================
# resource "aws_s3_bucket" "static" {
#   bucket = "anihpj-static"
#   acl    = "private"
#
#   versioning {
#     enabled = true
#   }
#
#   lifecycle_rule {
#     enabled = true
#     expiration { days = 90 }
#   }
#
#   server_side_encryption_configuration {
#     rule {
#       apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
#         sse_algorithm = "AES256"
#       }
#     }
#   }
# }

# =========================================
# NEW (v4.x/v5.x) - Separate resources
# =========================================
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "static" {
  bucket = "anihpj-static"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id

  rule {
    id     = "expire-old"
    status = "Enabled"

    expiration {
      days = 90
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm = "AES256"
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id

  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}

Appendix C: Glossary

C.1 Terraform Terminology

Term Definition
Resource A single infrastructure object (e.g., aws_instance, aws_s3_bucket)
Data Source Read-only query to fetch information from providers
Provider Plugin that manages resources for a service (AWS, Azure, GCP)
Module Reusable container for multiple resources
State JSON file mapping configuration to real resources
Backend Where state is stored (local, S3, Terraform Cloud)
Workspace Isolated state instance within same configuration
Plan Preview of changes Terraform will make
Apply Execute planned changes to infrastructure
Drift Difference between state and actual infrastructure
Taint Mark resource for recreation (deprecated, use -replace)
Provisioner Scripts that run during resource creation/destruction
Local Values Named expressions for reuse within a module (locals)
Output Values Return values from a module or root configuration
Input Variables Parameters that customize module behavior

C.2 AWS Terminology

Term Definition
VPC Virtual Private Cloud - isolated network in AWS
Subnet IP address range within a VPC
Security Group Virtual firewall for instances
IAM Identity and Access Management
ARN Amazon Resource Name - unique identifier
Region Geographic area with multiple AZs (e.g., us-east-1)
AZ Availability Zone - isolated data center
ECS Elastic Container Service
RDS Relational Database Service
ALB Application Load Balancer

D.3 HashiCorp Terraform Associate Certification

🎓
Exam Objectives
  1. Understand Infrastructure as Code concepts
  2. Understand Terraform's purpose (vs other IaC)
  3. Understand Terraform basics
  4. Use the Terraform CLI
  5. Interact with Terraform modules
  6. Navigate Terraform workflow
  7. Implement and maintain state
  8. Read, generate, and modify configuration
  9. Understand Terraform Cloud and Enterprise

Appendix E: Quick Reference Card

Terraform Quick Reference - Print This! 
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║           TERRAFORM QUICK REFERENCE - anihpj Project              ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ DAILY COMMANDS                                                     ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ terraform init          │ Initialize working directory             ║
║ terraform plan          │ Preview changes                          ║
║ terraform apply         │ Apply changes                            ║
║ terraform destroy       │ Destroy infrastructure                   ║
║ terraform fmt           │ Format code                              ║
║ terraform validate      │ Validate configuration                   ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ STATE COMMANDS                                                     ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ terraform state list    │ List resources in state                  ║
║ terraform state show X  │ Show resource details                    ║
║ terraform state mv A B  │ Move/rename resource                     ║
║ terraform state rm X    │ Remove from state                        ║
║ terraform import X ID   │ Import existing resource                 ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ USEFUL FLAGS                                                       ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ -auto-approve           │ Skip confirmation prompt                 ║
║ -target=RESOURCE        │ Target specific resource                 ║
║ -var="key=value"        │ Set variable value                       ║
║ -var-file=FILE          │ Load variables from file                 ║
║ -out=FILE               │ Save plan to file                        ║
║ -replace=RESOURCE       │ Force resource recreation                ║
║ -refresh-only           │ Only refresh state                       ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ DEBUG                                                              ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ export TF_LOG=DEBUG     │ Enable debug logging                     ║
║ terraform console       │ Interactive console                      ║
║ terraform graph         │ Generate dependency graph                ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ ANIHPJ DJANGO PROJECT COMMANDS                                     ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ cd terraform/environments/dev                                      ║
║ terraform init                                                     ║
║ terraform plan -var-file=../../vars/dev.tfvars                    ║
║ terraform apply -var-file=../../vars/dev.tfvars                   ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ COMMON DJANGO + AWS RESOURCES                                      ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ aws_ecs_service.django      │ Django web service                   ║
║ aws_ecs_service.celery      │ Celery workers                       ║
║ aws_db_instance.main        │ PostgreSQL (JobPost models)          ║
║ aws_elasticache_cluster     │ Redis (cache/Celery)                 ║
║ aws_s3_bucket.static        │ Static files                         ║
║ aws_s3_bucket.media         │ Media uploads                        ║
║ aws_cloudfront_distribution │ CDN                                  ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Appendix F: Troubleshooting Decision Tree

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TERRAFORM TROUBLESHOOTING                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
                    ┌─────────────────┐
                    │  terraform init │
                    │     fails?      │
                    └────────┬────────┘
                             │
               ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐
               ▼                           ▼
        ┌──────────┐                ┌──────────────┐
        │   Yes    │                │     No       │
        └────┬─────┘                └──────┬───────┘
             │                             │
             ▼                             ▼
    ┌────────────────┐            ┌─────────────────┐
    │ Check:         │            │  terraform plan │
    │ • Backend creds│            │     fails?      │
    │ • Network/proxy│            └────────┬────────┘
    │ • Provider ver │                     │
    └────────────────┘       ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐
                             ▼                           ▼
                      ┌──────────┐                ┌──────────────┐
                      │   Yes    │                │     No       │
                      └────┬─────┘                └──────┬───────┘
                           │                             │
                           ▼                             ▼
              ┌─────────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────────┐
              │ Error type?         │         │  terraform apply    │
              └─────────┬───────────┘         │     fails?          │
                        │                     └──────────┬──────────┘
        ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐                │
        ▼               ▼               ▼      ┌─────────┴─────────┐
   ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌─────────┐  ▼                   ▼
   │ Syntax  │    │ Provider │    │  State  │┌──────────┐   ┌──────────────┐
   │  Error  │    │   Error  │    │  Error  ││   Yes    │   │     No       │
   └────┬────┘    └────┬─────┘    └────┬────┘└────┬─────┘   └──────┬───────┘
        │              │               │          │                │
        ▼              ▼               ▼          ▼                ▼
   ┌─────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐ ┌────────────┐  ┌───────────┐
   │terraform│   │• AWS creds│   │• Lock ID │ │Check:      │  │✅ Success! │
   │validate │   │• Region   │   │• S3 perms│ │• API limits│  └───────────┘
   │terraform│   │• Provider │   │• DynamoDB│ │• IAM perms │
   │fmt      │   │  version  │   │  perms   │ │• Quotas    │
   └─────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘ │• Dependencies
                                             └────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      QUICK FIXES                                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  🔒 State Lock Error                                                │
│     terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID                                  │
│                                                                     │
│  🔄 Drift Detected                                                  │
│     terraform apply -refresh-only                                   │
│                                                                     │
│  💥 Resource Stuck                                                  │
│     terraform apply -replace=RESOURCE_ADDRESS                       │
│                                                                     │
│  🚫 Provider Error                                                  │
│     rm -rf .terraform && terraform init -upgrade                    │
│                                                                     │
│  📝 State Mismatch                                                  │
│     terraform state rm RESOURCE_ADDRESS                             │
│     terraform import RESOURCE_ADDRESS AWS_ID                        │
│                                                                     │
│  🐛 Debug Mode                                                      │
│     export TF_LOG=DEBUG && terraform plan                           │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Quick Start Paths

🚀

🎯 Choose Your Learning Path

Select the path that matches your background for the fastest route to Terraform mastery

📊 3 Paths • 250+ Topics • All skill levels welcome
🌱

Complete Beginner

New to IaC & Terraform

4-6 weeks
☁️

AWS Developer

Know AWS, learning Terraform

2-3 weeks
🔧

DevOps Engineer

CI/CD & automation focus

1-2 weeks

📊 Skill Prerequisites at a Glance

Skill Area 🌱 Path 1 ☁️ Path 2 🔧 Path 3
Command Line / Terminal None Basic Advanced
AWS Knowledge None Strong Moderate
Infrastructure as Code None CFN/CDK Other IaC
Git / Version Control None Basic Advanced
CI/CD Pipelines None None Required

⏱️ Time Investment Comparison

🌱 Path 1: Beginner 4-6 weeks
📚 Heavy Learning ~60 hours
☁️ Path 2: AWS Dev 2-3 weeks
🔄 Skill Transfer ~30 hours
🔧 Path 3: DevOps 1-2 weeks
⚡ Fast Track ~20 hours

Path 1: Complete Beginner

🗺️ Your 6-Week Learning Journey

Week 1
Fundamentals
→
Week 2
HCL & Basics
→
Week 3
Variables
→
Week 4
State
→
Week 5-6
AWS + Django 🎉
🌱
Your Journey (4-6 weeks)
  1. Week 1: What is Terraform? → IaC Benefits → Installation
  2. Week 2: HCL Basics → First Project → Plan/Apply/Destroy
  3. Week 3: Variables → Outputs → Data Sources
  4. Week 4: State Management → Modules Basics
  5. Week 5-6: AWS Provider → Django Patterns
📋 Path 1 Checklist (per Topic.txt)
✓ Terraform Fundamentals (1)
✓ CLI & Commands (2)
✓ AWS Provider Basics (3.1-3.5)
✓ Variables & Outputs (6)
✓ First Django Project (16.1)
First Commands to Learn 
# Start here!
terraform version     # Verify installation
terraform init        # Initialize project
terraform plan        # Preview changes
terraform apply       # Create resources
terraform destroy     # Clean up

# Practice project: Create an S3 bucket
mkdir my-first-terraform && cd my-first-terraform
# Create main.tf with S3 bucket resource
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply

🏆 Success Milestones - How to Know You're Ready

✅
Create S3 bucket without looking at docs
✅
Explain plan vs apply to a colleague
✅
Use variables and outputs in a module
✅
Deploy Django app to EC2 with Terraform

⚠️ Common Beginner Pitfalls to Avoid

✗
Committing .tfstate to Git
Contains secrets! Add to .gitignore
✗
Hardcoding credentials
Use env vars or AWS profiles instead
✗
Skipping terraform plan
Always preview before apply!
✗
Manual changes after apply
Creates drift - all changes via Terraform

Path 2: AWS Developer Adding Terraform

🧠 AWS → Terraform Mental Model

🖱️
Console Click
↓
resource block
📋
CloudFormation
↓
HCL config
📊
Stack State
↓
.tfstate file
📁
Nested Stacks
↓
modules
☁️
Your Journey (2-3 weeks)
  1. Day 1-2: What is Terraform? → Core Concepts → Quick Setup
  2. Day 3-5: AWS Provider Deep Dive → Authentication
  3. Week 2: State Management → Import → Convert existing
  4. Week 3: Modules → CI/CD → Django Patterns
📋 Path 2 Checklist (per Topic.txt)
✓ Core Concepts (1.2)
✓ AWS Provider Deep Dive (3)
✓ State Management (5)
✓ Modules (4)
✓ CI/CD (11)
You Know (AWS) Terraform Equivalent Quick Link
AWS Console terraform apply Plan/Apply/Destroy
CloudFormation HCL Configuration Comparison
IAM Roles aws_iam_role IAM Resources
VPC Console VPC Module VPC Section
Tags default_tags + tags Tagging

🔄 AWS Console → Terraform Quick Translation

AWS Console Action
Create → EC2 → Launch Instance
Terraform Equivalent
resource "aws_instance" "web" { }
AWS Console Action
S3 → Create Bucket → Configure
Terraform Equivalent
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" { }
AWS Console Action
RDS → Create Database → Multi-AZ
Terraform Equivalent
resource "aws_db_instance" "db" { }

🏆 Success Milestones - You're Production Ready When...

🔄
Import existing AWS resources into state
📦
Create reusable VPC module
🔒
Configure remote state with S3 + DynamoDB
🚀
Deploy multi-tier app with ALB + ASG

⚠️ AWS Developer Pitfalls to Avoid

✗
Mixing Console + Terraform
Creates drift - pick one method per resource
✗
Ignoring resource dependencies
Use depends_on when implicit deps fail
✗
Not using data sources
Reference existing resources, don't recreate
✗
Forgetting lifecycle rules
Use create_before_destroy for zero-downtime

Path 3: DevOps Engineer

🎯 DevOps Focus Areas

⚡
CI/CD
GitHub Actions
🔒
Security
tfsec, Sentinel
🧪
Testing
terratest, native
📦
Modules
Registry, Reuse
🔄
Drift
Detection, Fix
💰
Cost
Infracost
🔧
Your Journey (1-2 weeks)
  1. Day 1: Setup → CI/CD & Automation (Section 11)
  2. Day 2-3: Security & Compliance (Section 10) → OIDC
  3. Day 4-5: Testing (Section 12) → Module Design
  4. Week 2: Advanced Topics (Section 13) → Best Practices (Section 14)
📋 Path 3 Checklist (per Topic.txt)
✓ CI/CD & Automation (11)
✓ Security & Compliance (10)
✓ Testing (12)
✓ Advanced Topics (13)
✓ Best Practices (14)
DevOps Priority Commands 
# CI/CD Commands
terraform fmt -check -diff        # Lint in CI
terraform validate                # Validate syntax
terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan   # Save plan for apply
terraform apply plan.tfplan       # Apply saved plan

# State management
terraform state list              # Audit resources
terraform plan -refresh-only      # Detect drift
terraform import ADDR ID          # Import existing

# Security scanning
tfsec .                           # Security scan
checkov -d .                      # Policy checks
infracost breakdown --path .      # Cost estimation

🔄 Terraform CI/CD Pipeline Flow

📝
PR Created
→
✨
fmt + validate
→
🔒
tfsec scan
→
📋
plan output
→
💰
cost estimate
→
👀
Review
→
🚀
apply

🏆 DevOps Excellence Milestones

⚡
PR triggers auto-plan with cost diff
🔒
OIDC auth (no stored credentials)
🧪
Automated tests catch breaking changes
📊
Drift detected and alerted automatically

⚠️ DevOps Pipeline Pitfalls to Avoid

✗
Auto-apply without approval
Always require human review for prod
✗
Storing secrets in CI variables
Use OIDC or secrets manager
✗
No state locking in pipelines
Concurrent runs corrupt state
✗
Ignoring plan diff in reviews
The plan IS the code review - read it!

🤔 Not Sure Which Path? Answer These Questions

Have you used Infrastructure as Code before?
No → Start with Path 1
CloudFormation → Try Path 2
Ansible/Pulumi → Jump to Path 3
What's your primary goal?
Learn basics → Path 1
Manage AWS → Path 2
Automate CI/CD → Path 3
How much time do you have?
4-6 weeks → Path 1 (thorough)
2-3 weeks → Path 2 (focused)
1-2 weeks → Path 3 (fast-track)
💡 Most Django developers start with
Path 2: AWS Developer → Terraform

🎓 What You'll Be Able to Do After Each Path

🌱

After Path 1

  • Understand IaC concepts & benefits
  • Write basic Terraform configurations
  • Manage simple AWS resources
  • Use variables, outputs, and data sources
  • Deploy a Django app to AWS
  • Ready for Path 2 or 3 deep-dive
☁️

After Path 2

  • Translate AWS knowledge to Terraform
  • Import existing infrastructure
  • Create reusable modules
  • Manage state with S3 backend
  • Deploy production-ready Django apps
  • Ready for team collaboration
🔧

After Path 3

  • Build automated CI/CD pipelines
  • Implement security scanning
  • Write and run Terraform tests
  • Set up drift detection
  • Optimize costs with Infracost
  • Lead infrastructure projects

💡 Pro Tips for Faster Learning

🖥️
Use VS Code with TF Extension
Syntax highlighting, autocomplete, and inline docs make learning 3x faster
🧪
Practice with Free Tier
S3, DynamoDB, Lambda - practice real deployments without spending money
📖
Read terraform plan Output
Understanding plan output teaches you more than any tutorial
🗑️
Always terraform destroy
Clean up after practice - avoid surprise bills and learn the full lifecycle
🔄
Break Things on Purpose
Make errors, fix them - debugging teaches you how Terraform really works
📚
Keep This Guide Open
Use the search (Ctrl+F) - every answer is here. Bookmark it!

🌱 Beginners Bookmark

☁️ AWS Devs Bookmark

🔧 DevOps Bookmark

Completed your initial path? Here's how to level up:

🌱 → ☁️
Path 1 → Path 2
After basics, go deep on AWS specifics and modules
☁️ → 🔧
Path 2 → Path 3
Ready for AWS? Add CI/CD and team automation
🌱 → 🔧
Path 1 → Path 3
Skip AWS deep-dive, go straight to DevOps

HashiCorp Certified: Terraform Associate (003)

Master the 9 exam objectives using this documentation as your complete study guide

57
Questions
60
Minutes
70%
Pass Score
$70
USD Cost
2
Years Valid

Exam Format & What to Expect

📝

Question Types

Multiple Choice ~70%
Multiple Select ~20%
True/False ~10%
🖥️

Exam Delivery

  • Online proctored - take from home
  • PSI platform - browser-based
  • ID verification - government ID required
  • Webcam & mic - must be enabled
  • Clean desk - no notes allowed
📊

Scoring

70%
Minimum Passing Score
Need correct: ~40 of 57 questions
Can miss: ~17 questions

📈 Topic Weight Distribution (Approximate)

State Management ~18%
Modules ~15%
Core Workflow (init/plan/apply) ~15%
Variables & Outputs ~12%
IaC Concepts ~10%
Terraform Cloud/Enterprise ~10%
CLI Commands ~10%
Configuration Language ~10%

Exam Objectives Mapped to Sections

1

Understand Infrastructure as Code (IaC) concepts

Benefits, IaC patterns, idempotency, declarative vs imperative

Section 1.1-1.2
⏱️ ~2 hours study
2

Understand the purpose of Terraform

Multi-cloud, provider ecosystem, Terraform vs alternatives

Section 1.3
⏱️ ~1 hour study
3

Understand Terraform basics

Providers, resources, data sources, terraform settings

Section 1.4-1.5
⏱️ ~3 hours study
4

Use Terraform outside core workflow

terraform import, taint, console, graph, output

Section 2
⏱️ ~4 hours study
5

Interact with Terraform modules

Module sources, inputs/outputs, versioning, registry

Section 4
⏱️ ~5 hours study
6

Use the core Terraform workflow

Write → Plan → Apply cycle, terraform.tfvars, workspaces

Section 2.1
⏱️ ~3 hours study
7

Implement and maintain state ⭐ HIGH WEIGHT

Backends, locking, state commands, sensitive data, remote state

Section 5
⏱️ ~6 hours study
8

Read, generate, and modify configuration

Variables, locals, outputs, expressions, functions, dynamic blocks

Section 6-7
⏱️ ~4 hours study
9

Understand Terraform Cloud/Enterprise

Remote operations, workspaces, VCS integration, Sentinel

Section 5.4
⏱️ ~3 hours study
📚 Total Recommended Study Time
~31 hours
Spread over 2-4 weeks for best retention

~10 hours

~10 hours

  • Practice exams

~3 hours + practice

Practice Questions & Sample Exam

📝

High-Weight Topics

  • State locking and backends
  • terraform init, plan, apply
  • Variables and outputs
  • Module sources and versions
⚡

Frequently Tested

  • Resource dependencies
  • Data sources vs resources
  • count vs for_each
  • Sensitive values
🔐

Security Topics

  • Sensitive variables
  • State encryption
  • Provider authentication
  • Secrets management

📋 Sample Practice Questions

Q1 State Management

Which command is used to download provider plugins and initialize backend configuration?

💡 Explanation: terraform init initializes the working directory, downloads providers, and configures the backend.
Q2 Modules

Which of the following is a valid module source? (Select TWO)

💡 Explanation: Valid sources include local paths (./), registry (namespace/name/provider), GitHub, S3, and GCS.
Q3 State

True or False: The terraform state file can contain sensitive information and should be secured.

💡 Explanation: State files contain resource attributes, potentially including passwords, keys, and other sensitive data. Always use encrypted backends!
Q4 Configuration

What is the difference between count and for_each?

💡 Explanation: for_each is preferred because removing items doesn't shift indexes, preventing unnecessary recreations.
💡
Exam-Taking Strategies
  • Read ALL answer choices before selecting—many are "almost correct"
  • Flag difficult questions and return to them later
  • Watch for keywords: "BEST", "MOST", "ALWAYS", "NEVER"
  • Eliminate obviously wrong answers first
  • Trust your first instinct unless you're certain it's wrong

📝 More Practice Questions by Objective

Objective 5: Modules

Q5: What is required to use a module from the Terraform Registry?

A) Download and extract manually
B) ✓ Specify source and version in module block
C) Copy module files to local directory
D) Install using terraform get only
💡 Registry modules need source (namespace/name/provider) and version constraint.
Objective 6: Workflow

Q6: What does terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan do?

A) Creates a backup of the state file
B) ✓ Saves the plan to a file for later apply
C) Outputs the plan to standard output only
D) Creates a new workspace
💡 Saved plans ensure exact changes are applied, especially in CI/CD.
Objective 7: State (HIGH WEIGHT)

Q7: Which backend supports state locking by default?

A) Local backend
B) ✓ S3 with DynamoDB
C) HTTP backend
D) Consul without ACLs
💡 S3 stores state, DynamoDB provides locking. Always use both together!
Objective 7: State (continued)

Q8: How do you move a resource to a different state address?

A) terraform state copy
B) ✓ terraform state mv
C) terraform state rename
D) Edit the state file manually
💡 Never edit state manually! Use state mv for refactoring.
Objective 8: Configuration

Q9: What is the difference between variable and local?

A) They are identical
B) ✓ Variables are inputs, locals are computed values
C) Locals can be overridden at runtime
D) Variables cannot have default values
💡 Variables = user input, Locals = internal calculations/reused expressions.
Objective 9: Terraform Cloud

Q10: What execution mode runs Terraform on your local machine but stores state remotely?

A) Remote execution
B) ✓ Local execution with remote state
C) Agent execution
D) Hybrid execution
💡 TFC supports local (run locally, state remote) and remote (run in TFC) modes.

📚 Total: 10 sample questions covering key objectives

Practice More on HashiCorp Learn →

📋 Extended Question Bank (Questions 11-30)

Q11: State Management

Which command manually triggers state reconciliation with real resources?

A) terraform sync
B) terraform state sync
C) ✓ terraform apply -refresh-only
D) terraform refresh (deprecated)

Q12: Providers

How do you use the same provider with different configurations?

A) Duplicate provider blocks
B) ✓ Provider alias
C) Multiple terraform blocks
D) Provider inheritance

Q13: Modules

Where is a module's version constraint specified?

A) Inside the module
B) ✓ In the module block's version argument
C) In required_providers
D) In terraform.lock.hcl

Q14: Variables

Which variable type preserves insertion order?

A) set
B) ✓ list
C) map
D) object

Q15: Expressions

What does coalesce("", "default") return?

A) ""
B) ✓ "default"
C) null
D) Error

Q16: State

What happens to resources removed from config but still in state?

A) Kept as-is
B) ✓ Destroyed on next apply
C) Moved to import block
D) Warning only

Q17: Backends

Which backend supports state locking by default?

A) Local
B) HTTP
C) ✓ S3 (with DynamoDB)
D) GCS (without locking)

Q18: CLI

What does terraform init -upgrade do?

A) Upgrades Terraform version
B) ✓ Updates providers to latest allowed versions
C) Upgrades state file format
D) Updates modules only

Q19: Provisioners

When does local-exec run by default?

A) Before resource creation
B) ✓ After resource creation
C) During plan
D) On destroy only

Q20: Data Sources

When is a data source read?

A) Only during init
B) ✓ During plan and apply
C) Only during apply
D) Only when explicitly called

Q21: Lifecycle

What does prevent_destroy = true do?

A) Makes resource immutable
B) ✓ Errors if destroy is planned for this resource
C) Skips resource in destroy
D) Requires confirmation for destroy

Q22: TF Cloud

What's the difference between workspace variables and environment variables in TFC?

A) They are the same
B) ✓ Terraform vars = TF_VAR, Env vars = shell environment
C) Workspace vars are encrypted
D) Env vars can't be sensitive

Q23: State

How do you import existing infrastructure without writing config first? (TF 1.5+)

A) terraform import only
B) ✓ import block with terraform plan -generate-config-out
C) Manual state editing
D) terraform state pull

Q24: Workspaces

How do you reference current workspace name in config?

A) var.workspace
B) ✓ terraform.workspace
C) local.workspace
D) TF_WORKSPACE

Q25: Functions

What does try(var.obj.attr, "default") do?

A) Throws error if attr missing
B) ✓ Returns "default" if attr fails
C) Creates attr with default
D) Validates attr type

Q26: Outputs

How do you access a child module's output?

A) output.module_name.value
B) ✓ module.module_name.output_name
C) var.module_name.output
D) data.module.output

Q27: Meta-Arguments

Which meta-argument creates resources before destroying old ones?

A) lifecycle { prevent_destroy }
B) ✓ lifecycle { create_before_destroy }
C) depends_on
D) replace_triggered_by

Q28: Version Constraints

What does ~> 2.1 allow?

A) Only 2.1.x
B) 2.x.x
C) ✓ >= 2.1.0, < 3.0.0
D) Exact 2.1.0

Q29: State Locking

What flag bypasses state locking?

A) -no-lock
B) ✓ -lock=false
C) -skip-lock
D) -force

Q30: Sensitive Data

How do you mark output as sensitive?

A) sensitive_output block
B) ✓ output { sensitive = true }
C) redact = true
D) mask = true

🏆 Advanced Question Bank (Questions 31-50)

Q31: moved Block

What does a moved block do?

A) Moves files
B) ✓ Refactors resource addresses in state
C) Moves providers
D) Migrates backends

Q32: Validation

Where do you define custom variable validation?

A) In locals
B) ✓ validation block inside variable
C) In precondition
D) In check block

Q33: Preconditions

What's the difference between precondition and postcondition?

A) Same, different syntax
B) ✓ Pre = before apply, Post = after apply
C) Pre for inputs, Post for outputs
D) Pre = plan, Post = destroy

Q34: Resource Targeting

What flag applies changes to only one resource?

A) -resource
B) ✓ -target=resource.name
C) -only
D) -select

Q35: Provider Requirements

Where do you specify provider source and version?

A) provider block
B) ✓ terraform { required_providers {} }
C) versions.tf
D) .terraform.lock.hcl

Q36: Dynamic Blocks

What does a dynamic block generate?

A) Dynamic resources
B) ✓ Repeated nested blocks within a resource
C) Dynamic variables
D) Dynamic outputs

Q37: Taint (Deprecated)

What replaces terraform taint?

A) terraform destroy
B) ✓ terraform apply -replace=resource
C) terraform refresh
D) terraform state rm

Q38: Graph

What does terraform graph output?

A) JSON
B) YAML
C) ✓ DOT format (GraphViz)
D) PNG

Q39: Module Sources

Which is NOT a valid module source?

A) Local path (./module)
B) GitHub URL
C) Registry (hashicorp/consul/aws)
D) ✓ Direct provider reference

Q40: Output Dependencies

Why might you add depends_on to an output?

A) Never needed
B) ✓ Ensure resource completes before output exposed
C) Required for sensitive outputs
D) For module outputs only

Q41: check Block

What is a check block used for?

A) Syntax validation
B) ✓ Health checks/assertions on infrastructure
C) Provider testing
D) Module verification

Q42: .tf vs .tf.json

Can you mix HCL and JSON configs in same directory?

A) No, choose one
B) ✓ Yes, Terraform loads both
C) Only in modules
D) Requires flag

Q43: null_resource

When would you use null_resource?

A) Create empty resources
B) ✓ Run provisioners without managing real resource
C) Placeholder only
D) Testing

Q44: terraform_remote_state

What type is terraform_remote_state?

A) Resource
B) ✓ Data source
C) Provider
D) Module

Q45: count.index

What is count.index for the third instance?

A) 3
B) ✓ 2 (zero-indexed)
C) 1
D) "third"

Q46: each.key/each.value

With for_each on a set, what is each.key?

A) Index number
B) ✓ Same as each.value (the set element)
C) Undefined
D) Error

Q47: Sensitive Outputs

Can you use sensitive values in non-sensitive outputs?

A) Yes, no restrictions
B) ✓ No, output must also be marked sensitive
C) Only with nonsensitive() function
D) In modules only

Q48: Backend Migration

What command do you run after changing backend config?

A) terraform backend init
B) ✓ terraform init -migrate-state
C) terraform state migrate
D) terraform apply

Q49: Plan Output

What does terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan create?

A) Readable text file
B) ✓ Binary plan file for exact apply
C) JSON output
D) State backup

Q50: terraform fmt

What does terraform fmt -check do?

A) Formats and saves
B) ✓ Returns non-zero exit code if files need formatting
C) Shows diff only
D) Validates syntax

Exam Day Preparation

📋

Before the Exam

⏱️

During the Exam

  • Time management: ~1 min per question
  • Flag & skip: Don't get stuck on hard questions
  • Read carefully: Watch for "NOT", "EXCEPT"
  • Eliminate wrong: Narrow down to 2 choices
  • Trust yourself: First instinct is often correct
  • Review flagged: Use remaining time wisely

⏱️ Time Management Strategy

0-40
minutes
First Pass
Answer easy Qs, flag hard ones
→
40-55
minutes
Second Pass
Return to flagged Qs
→
55-60
minutes
Final Review
Double-check answers

⚠️ Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid

❌
Confusing count vs for_each

count = integer index, for_each = map/set keys

❌
Thinking refresh updates resources

Refresh only updates state, not infrastructure

❌
Forgetting -migrate-state

Required when changing backend config

❌
Mixing up data vs resource

Data reads existing, resource manages lifecycle

❌
Wrong variable precedence

-var > TF_VAR_ > .auto.tfvars > terraform.tfvars

❌
Local vs remote execution in TFC

Local = runs on your machine, Remote = runs in TFC

📋 Quick Reference Cheat Sheet

📁 File Extensions
.tf - HCL configuration
.tf.json - JSON configuration
.tfvars - Variable values
.tfstate - State file
.terraform.lock.hcl - Dependency lock
🧱 Block Types
terraform - Settings, required_providers
provider - Provider configuration
resource - Managed infrastructure
data - Read-only queries
variable/output/locals - Values
module - Call child modules
🔢 Version Constraints
= - Exact version
!= - Exclude version
>, >=, <, <= - Comparisons
~> 2.0 - >= 2.0, < 3.0
~> 2.1.0 - >= 2.1.0, < 2.2.0
⚙️ Meta-Arguments
count - Create N instances
for_each - Iterate map/set
depends_on - Explicit dependencies
provider - Select provider alias
lifecycle - Custom behaviors

Enterprise
