Template 1: Startup MVP Stack

🚀

Early-Stage Startup, Cost-Optimized Django App

Perfect for MVPs, proof of concepts, and small production apps

$50-100
/month estimated

🏗️ Architecture Diagram

🌐
VPC

2 Availability Zones

💻
EC2

t3.small + ASG

🗃️
RDS

db.t3.micro PostgreSQL

📦
S3

Static files

⚡
CloudFront

CDN Distribution

🔗
Route 53

DNS Management

💰 Monthly Cost Breakdown (Estimated)

EC2 t3.small
~$15
RDS db.t3.micro
~$15
S3 + Transfer
~$5
CloudFront
~$10
Route 53
~$1
Total
~$50-100
📄 main.tf - Startup MVP Stack Terraform 
# ===========================================
# Startup MVP Stack - Cost-Optimized Django
# Estimated: $50-100/month
# ===========================================

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.0"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
}

# Variables
variable "aws_region" {
  default = "us-east-1"
}

variable "project_name" {
  default = "startup-mvp"
}

variable "environment" {
  default = "production"
}

# VPC with 2 AZs
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"

  name = "${var.project_name}-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs             = ["${var.aws_region}a", "${var.aws_region}b"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24"]

  enable_nat_gateway = true
  single_nat_gateway = true  # Cost optimization

  tags = {
    Project     = var.project_name
    Environment = var.environment
  }
}

# Security Group for EC2
resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name_prefix = "${var.project_name}-web-"
  vpc_id      = module.vpc.vpc_id

  ingress {
    from_port   = 80
    to_port     = 80
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  ingress {
    from_port   = 443
    to_port     = 443
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
}

# EC2 Instance (Django App)
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = "t3.small"
  subnet_id     = module.vpc.public_subnets[0]

  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web.id]

  user_data = <<-EOF
    #!/bin/bash
    yum update -y
    yum install -y docker
    systemctl start docker
    systemctl enable docker
  EOF

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-web"
  }
}

# RDS PostgreSQL
resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
  identifier     = "${var.project_name}-db"
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "15"
  instance_class = "db.t3.micro"

  allocated_storage     = 20
  max_allocated_storage = 100
  storage_encrypted     = true

  db_name  = "djangodb"
  username = "django_admin"
  password = var.db_password  # Use secrets manager!

  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds.id]
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name

  backup_retention_period = 7
  skip_final_snapshot     = false

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-postgres"
  }
}

# S3 Bucket for Static Files
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "static" {
  bucket = "${var.project_name}-static-${random_id.bucket.hex}"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id

  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}

# CloudFront Distribution
resource "aws_cloudfront_distribution" "cdn" {
  enabled             = true
  default_root_object = "index.html"

  origin {
    domain_name = aws_s3_bucket.static.bucket_regional_domain_name
    origin_id   = "S3Origin"

    s3_origin_config {
      origin_access_identity = aws_cloudfront_origin_access_identity.oai.cloudfront_access_identity_path
    }
  }

  default_cache_behavior {
    allowed_methods        = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    cached_methods         = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    target_origin_id       = "S3Origin"
    viewer_protocol_policy = "redirect-to-https"

    forwarded_values {
      query_string = false
      cookies {
        forward = "none"
      }
    }
  }

  restrictions {
    geo_restriction {
      restriction_type = "none"
    }
  }

  viewer_certificate {
    cloudfront_default_certificate = true
  }
}
📍 Template Location: Section 16.1 (Simplified) VPC + EC2 + RDS + S3 + CloudFront + Route 53

Template 2: Production SaaS Platform

🏢

B2B SaaS with High Availability

Production-grade infrastructure for growing SaaS businesses

/month estimated

ElastiCache

✨ Key Features & Benefits
High Availability 3 AZ deployment, auto-failover Aurora, ECS service auto-recovery
Auto-Scaling ECS Fargate scales 0-100 tasks, Aurora Serverless scales compute
Security WAF rules, Shield DDoS protection, Cognito MFA, VPC isolation
Performance CloudFront edge caching, Redis session/cache, optimized DB queries

Template 3: Enterprise Multi-Region

🌐

Global Enterprise with Compliance Requirements

Multi-region deployment for global reach and disaster recovery

$2000+
/month estimated

🌍 Multi-Region Architecture

🏢 Enterprise Components
Component US-EAST (Primary) EU-WEST (DR) Purpose
VPC 3 AZs, Transit Gateway 3 AZs, Transit Gateway Network isolation
Aurora Global Primary Writer Read Replica (< 1s lag) Global database
S3 Source bucket Cross-region replication Data durability
Route 53 Geo-routing + health checks + failover Global DNS
🔒 SOC 2
🇪🇺 GDPR
🏥 HIPAA
💳 PCI-DSS
☁️ ISO 27001
📍 Template Location: Advanced Section (Custom) Multi-Region VPC + Route 53 Geo + Aurora Global + Transit Gateway

Template 4: Microservices Architecture

🔄

Decomposed Django Monolith to Microservices

Event-driven architecture with Kubernetes and service mesh

$1500+
/month estimated

🔄 Microservices Architecture

👤 User Service
  • Authentication & Authorization
  • User profiles & preferences
  • Django REST + JWT
📦 Order Service
  • Order processing & tracking
  • Inventory management
  • Event sourcing pattern
🛍️ Product Service
  • Product catalog & search
  • Pricing & promotions
  • ElasticSearch integration
💳 Payment Service
  • Payment processing
  • Stripe/PayPal integration
  • PCI compliance

🔧 Key Microservices Patterns Used

🔀
Service Mesh

AWS App Mesh for service-to-service communication

📨
Event Sourcing

EventBridge for async event-driven communication

🚪
API Gateway

Single entry point with rate limiting & auth

🔄
CQRS

Separate read/write paths for scalability

⚡
Lambda@Edge

Edge functions for auth, redirects, A/B testing

🔁
Step Functions

Workflow orchestration for complex processes

🗄️
DynamoDB Sessions

High-speed session storage across services

🌊
Kinesis Streaming

Real-time data streaming for analytics

📍 Template Location: Section 16.1 + Section 3.13 EKS + EventBridge + Step Functions

Variable
usage-based

Athena

EMR

SageMaker

QuickSight

🎯 Which Template Should You Choose?

Template Best For Team Size Complexity Cost
🚀 Startup MVP MVPs, side projects, small apps 1-3 devs Low $50-100/mo
🏢 SaaS Platform B2B SaaS, production apps 5-15 devs Medium $500-1000/mo
🌐 Enterprise Global apps, compliance needs 20+ devs High $2000+/mo
🔄 Microservices Large monolith decomposition 10-30 devs Very High $1500+/mo
📊 Data Platform Analytics, ML, BI Data team Medium-High Variable

🧭 Template Decision Flowchart

✅ Template Implementation Checklist

1 Pre-Deployment
  • ☐ AWS account configured with billing alerts
  • ☐ IAM user with admin permissions created
  • ☐ AWS CLI installed and configured
  • ☐ Terraform v1.0+ installed
  • ☐ S3 backend bucket created for state
2 Customization
  • ☐ Copy template to your project directory
  • ☐ Update variables.tf with your values
  • ☐ Modify instance types for your needs
  • ☐ Configure domain names and certificates
  • ☐ Review security group rules
3 Deployment
  • ☐ Run terraform init successfully
  • ☐ Review terraform plan output
  • ☐ Apply to staging environment first
  • ☐ Verify all resources created
  • ☐ Deploy Django application
4 Post-Deployment
  • ☐ Configure monitoring dashboards
  • ☐ Set up alerting for critical metrics
  • ☐ Document any customizations made
  • ☐ Set up CI/CD pipeline
  • ☐ Schedule regular cost reviews

⚡ Quick Start Commands

1. Initialize your template:
# Clone and setup
mkdir my-django-infra && cd my-django-infra
cp -r /path/to/template/* .
terraform init
2. Configure your variables:
# Create terraform.tfvars
cat > terraform.tfvars << EOF
project_name = "my-django-app"
environment  = "production"
aws_region   = "us-east-1"
EOF
3. Plan and verify:
# Review what will be created
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Check estimated costs
terraform show -json tfplan | jq '.resource_changes'
4. Deploy infrastructure:
# Apply the configuration
terraform apply tfplan

# Get outputs (endpoints, IPs, etc.)
terraform output

📈 Template Upgrade Path

🎉

Ready to Deploy Your Django Infrastructure?

Choose a template above, customize it for your needs, and have production-ready infrastructure running in minutes!

🚀 Start with MVP Template 📚 Learn Terraform Basics First

Comparison Matrices

🔍

Side-by-Side Comparisons

Make informed decisions with comprehensive comparison tables for tools, strategies, and services

⚔️
Terraform vs Others
🚀
Deployment Strategies
🗄️
Database Options

Terraform vs Alternatives

⚔️

Infrastructure as Code Tool Comparison

Compare Terraform with CloudFormation, Pulumi, and Ansible

🟣
Terraform
HashiCorp
⭐ Our Choice
🟠
CloudFormation
AWS Native
AWS Only
🔵
Pulumi
Real Languages
Developer-Friendly
🔴
Ansible
Red Hat
Config Management
Feature 🟣 Terraform 🟠 CloudFormation 🔵 Pulumi 🔴 Ansible
Cloud Support Multi-cloud AWS only Multi-cloud Multi-cloud
Language HCL YAML/JSON Python/TS/Go YAML
State Management ✅ Yes (S3/Cloud) ✅ AWS-managed ✅ Yes (Cloud) ❌ No (Idempotent)
Provider Ecosystem 3000+ N/A (AWS) 100+ Modules
Learning Curve Moderate Easy Steep Easy
Community Size ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very Large ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Large ⭐⭐⭐ Growing ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very Large
Enterprise Support ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes
Cost Free / Paid Free Free / Paid Free / Paid
Best For Infrastructure AWS-only Developers Config Mgmt
✅ Choose Terraform When
  • Multi-cloud or hybrid strategy
  • Need large provider ecosystem
  • Team knows HCL or willing to learn
  • Want portable infrastructure code
🟠 Choose CloudFormation When
  • 100% AWS environment
  • Want native AWS integration
  • Prefer AWS-managed state
  • Need StackSets for multi-account
🔵 Choose Pulumi When
  • Team prefers Python/TypeScript
  • Need complex logic in IaC
  • Want testing with real languages
  • Prefer familiar IDE tooling
🔴 Choose Ansible When
  • Focus on configuration management
  • Need to manage existing servers
  • Want agentless architecture
  • Combine with Terraform (IaC + Config)

🧭 IaC Tool Selection Flowchart

📝 Syntax Comparison: Create an S3 Bucket

🟣 Terraform (HCL) ⭐ Recommended 
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "media" {
  bucket = "myapp-media-bucket"
  
  tags = {
    Environment = "production"
    Project     = "django-app"
  }
}
🟠 CloudFormation (YAML) 
Resources:
  MediaBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      BucketName: myapp-media-bucket
      Tags:
        - Key: Environment
          Value: production
        - Key: Project
          Value: django-app
🔵 Pulumi (Python) 
import pulumi_aws as aws

media_bucket = aws.s3.Bucket(
    "media",
    bucket="myapp-media-bucket",
    tags={
        "Environment": "production",
        "Project": "django-app",
    }
)
🔴 Ansible (YAML) 
- name: Create S3 bucket
  amazon.aws.s3_bucket:
    name: myapp-media-bucket
    state: present
    tags:
      Environment: production
      Project: django-app

Deployment Strategies Comparison

🚀

Deployment Strategy Selection Guide

Compare deployment methods to find the best fit for your Django application

📊 Deployment Strategy Visual Guide

Strategy Downtime Risk Rollback Complexity Cost
🔄 Recreate High High Fast Low Low
📈 Rolling None Low Slow Medium Low
🔵🟢 Blue/Green None Low Instant High High
🐦 Canary None Very Low Medium Very High High
🧪 A/B Testing None Low Medium Very High High
🔄 Recreate - Best For
  • Development environments
  • Small apps with low traffic
  • When downtime is acceptable
  • Database schema migrations
📈 Rolling - Best For
  • Most production Django apps
  • Cost-conscious deployments
  • Stateless applications
  • ECS/Kubernetes default
🔵🟢 Blue/Green - Best For
  • Mission-critical applications
  • When instant rollback needed
  • Major version releases
  • Compliance requirements
🐦 Canary - Best For
  • High-traffic applications
  • Risk-averse deployments
  • Testing in production
  • Gradual feature rollout
🔵🟢

Blue/Green Deployment with Terraform + ALB

Instant rollback using target group switching

# Blue/Green deployment using ALB target groups
resource "aws_lb_target_group" "blue" {
  name     = "django-blue"
  port     = 8000
  protocol = "HTTP"
  vpc_id   = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  health_check {
    path                = "/health/"
    healthy_threshold   = 2
    unhealthy_threshold = 3
  }
}

resource "aws_lb_target_group" "green" {
  name     = "django-green"
  port     = 8000
  protocol = "HTTP"
  vpc_id   = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  health_check {
    path                = "/health/"
    healthy_threshold   = 2
    unhealthy_threshold = 3
  }
}

# Switch traffic instantly by changing this variable
variable "active_color" {
  default = "blue"  # Change to "green" to switch
}

resource "aws_lb_listener_rule" "app" {
  listener_arn = aws_lb_listener.https.arn
  priority     = 100

  action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = var.active_color == "blue" ? 
      aws_lb_target_group.blue.arn : 
      aws_lb_target_group.green.arn
  }

  condition {
    path_pattern { values = ["/*"] }
  }
}

# 🔄 Rollback: Simply change active_color = "blue" and apply

AWS Database Options for Django

🗄️

Database Selection for Django Applications

Choose the right AWS database service based on your Django app requirements

🐘
RDS PostgreSQL
General Purpose
⭐ Recommended
⚡
Aurora PostgreSQL
High Performance
5x faster
🔄
Aurora Serverless
Auto-scaling
Pay per use
⚙️
DynamoDB
NoSQL
Sessions/Cache
🍃
DocumentDB
MongoDB-like
Document store
🐬
RDS MySQL
Legacy/WordPress
Alternative
Service Use Case Cost Scalability Maintenance Django ORM
🐘 RDS PostgreSQL General purpose Medium Good AWS-managed ⭐ Excellent
⚡ Aurora PostgreSQL High-performance High Excellent AWS-managed ⭐ Excellent
🔄 Aurora Serverless Variable workloads Variable Auto AWS-managed ⭐ Excellent
⚙️ DynamoDB NoSQL / Sessions Low Excellent Fully-managed Poor (custom)
🍃 DocumentDB MongoDB-like Medium Good AWS-managed Poor
🐬 RDS MySQL Legacy apps Medium Good AWS-managed Good

🎯 Quick Decision Guide

🐘 Use RDS PostgreSQL When...
  • Starting a new Django project
  • Need full ORM compatibility
  • Moderate traffic (up to 10K req/min)
  • Budget-conscious but need reliability
⚡ Use Aurora PostgreSQL When...
  • High-traffic production apps
  • Need read replicas for scaling
  • Multi-AZ failover is critical
  • Budget allows for premium DB
🔄 Use Aurora Serverless When...
  • Unpredictable traffic patterns
  • Development/staging environments
  • Want pay-per-use pricing
  • Need auto-scaling without config
⚙️ Use DynamoDB When...
  • Session storage only
  • Caching layer (with DAX)
  • Event logs / analytics data
  • Don't need Django ORM
💡
Django ORM Best Practice

For Django applications, PostgreSQL (RDS or Aurora) is the recommended choice because Django's ORM has the best support for PostgreSQL features including JSONField, ArrayField, full-text search, and advanced indexing. Use DynamoDB only for specific use cases like session storage alongside your primary PostgreSQL database.

🐘

RDS PostgreSQL for Django with Terraform

Production-ready database configuration

# RDS PostgreSQL optimized for Django
resource "aws_db_instance" "django" {
  identifier     = "django-postgres"
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "15.4"
  instance_class = "db.t3.medium"  # Scale up for production
  
  allocated_storage     = 100
  max_allocated_storage = 500  # Auto-scaling storage
  storage_encrypted     = true
  
  db_name  = "django_db"
  username = "django_admin"
  password = var.db_password
  
  # High Availability
  multi_az               = true
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.private.name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds.id]
  
  # Performance & Backup
  backup_retention_period = 7
  backup_window          = "03:00-04:00"
  maintenance_window     = "sun:04:00-sun:05:00"
  
  # Django settings
  parameter_group_name = aws_db_parameter_group.django.name
  skip_final_snapshot  = false
  
  tags = { Name = "Django PostgreSQL" }
}

# Django-optimized PostgreSQL parameters
resource "aws_db_parameter_group" "django" {
  family = "postgres15"
  name   = "django-postgres-params"
  
  parameter {
    name  = "log_statement"
    value = "ddl"  # Log schema changes
  }
  parameter {
    name  = "log_min_duration_statement"
    value = "1000"  # Log slow queries > 1s
  }
}

📈 Database Performance Comparison for Django

📊

Comparison Matrices Complete!

Use these matrices to make informed decisions about your infrastructure tooling, deployment strategies, and database choices.

⚔️ IaC Tools 🚀 Deployments 🗄️ Databases

Cost Estimation Examples

💰

AWS Cost Estimation for Django Apps

Understand monthly AWS costs at different scales - from startup MVP to enterprise deployment

🌱
Startup
~$118/mo
📈
Growth
~$800/mo
🏢
Enterprise
~$8K/mo

📊 Cost Scaling Overview

Small Django App (Startup) - ~$118/month

🌱

MVP / Early Stage Startup

Perfect for 1-1000 daily active users • Simple architecture • Cost-optimized

~$118/mo

🏗️ Startup Architecture

💵 Monthly Cost Breakdown

Service Configuration Cost
● EC2 t3.small × 2 instances $30.00
● RDS PostgreSQL db.t3.micro $15.00
● ALB Application Load Balancer $20.00
● S3 50GB storage $1.00
● CloudFront 100GB transfer $10.00
● Route 53 1 hosted zone $0.50
● NAT Gateway 1 AZ $32.00
● Data Transfer ~100GB egress $10.00
TOTAL MONTHLY COST ~$118.50
📊 Cost Distribution
● NAT: 27%
● EC2: 25%
● ALB+RDS: 30%
● Other: 18%
💡 Cost Optimization Tips
  • Reserved Instances: Save 30-40% on EC2
  • Spot Instances: Up to 90% savings for dev
  • NAT Instance: Replace NAT Gateway (~$5/mo)
  • S3 Intelligent Tiering: Auto-optimize storage
  • Right-sizing: Monitor and adjust instance types

Medium Django App (Growth) - ~$800/month

📈

Growth Stage / Series A

1,000-50,000 DAU • ECS Fargate • Multi-AZ • Caching layer

~$800/mo

🏗️ Growth Architecture

💵 Monthly Cost Breakdown

Service Configuration Cost
● ECS Fargate 4 tasks × 2vCPU/4GB $200.00
● RDS PostgreSQL db.m5.large, Multi-AZ $300.00
● ElastiCache cache.t3.medium (Redis) $60.00
● CloudFront 1TB transfer $85.00
● NAT Gateway 2 AZs $64.00
● ALB Application Load Balancer $20.00
● S3 500GB storage $10.00
● WAF Web Application Firewall $10.00
● Route 53 2 hosted zones $1.00
● Data Transfer ~500GB egress $50.00
TOTAL MONTHLY COST ~$800.00
🚀 Key Upgrades from Startup Tier
EC2 → ECS Fargate
Auto-scaling containers, no server management
Single-AZ → Multi-AZ
High availability with automatic failover
+ ElastiCache
Redis caching for sessions & queries
+ WAF
Web Application Firewall protection

Large Django App (Enterprise) - ~$8,043/month

🏢

Enterprise / High-Traffic

50,000+ DAU • Aurora • EKS Option • DDoS Protection • Global CDN

~$8,043/mo

🏗️ Enterprise Architecture

💵 Monthly Cost Breakdown

Service Configuration Cost
● WAF + Shield Advanced DDoS protection + WAF rules $3,100.00
● ECS Fargate 20 tasks × 4vCPU/8GB $2,000.00
● Aurora PostgreSQL r5.2xlarge cluster (Writer + 2 Readers) $800.00
● CloudFront 10TB transfer $750.00
● Data Transfer ~5TB egress $500.00
● ElastiCache r5.xlarge 3-node cluster $400.00
● EKS (if using) Managed Kubernetes $220.00
● S3 5TB storage $115.00
● NAT Gateway 3 AZs $96.00
● ALB 3 Load Balancers $60.00
● Route 53 5 hosted zones $2.50
TOTAL MONTHLY COST ~$8,043.50
🛡️ Security & Compliance
  • AWS Shield Advanced (DDoS)
  • WAF with managed rules
  • VPC Flow Logs
  • AWS Config for compliance
⚡ High Performance
  • Aurora with read replicas
  • ElastiCache cluster mode
  • Global CloudFront distribution
  • Auto-scaling policies
🔄 High Availability
  • Multi-AZ everything
  • Automatic failover
  • 99.99% SLA target
  • Blue/green deployments
💰

Cost Estimation Complete!

Scale your Django infrastructure from $118/mo startup to $8K/mo enterprise with clear cost breakdowns.

🚀 Migration Guides

Step-by-step guides for migrating to Terraform from other tools or manual infrastructure

🔄

Migration Overview

Safely transition your infrastructure to Terraform

Whether you're moving from AWS CloudFormation, another IaC tool, or manually created console resources, these guides provide battle-tested strategies for migrating your Django infrastructure to Terraform with zero downtime.

☁️ CloudFormation → Terraform

Migrate existing CFN stacks using import blocks, cf2tf, or terraformer tools

14 Steps Tools Included
🖱️ Console → Terraform

Import manually created AWS resources into Terraform management

6 Phases Django-Focused
0
Downtime Target
1-2
Weeks (Typical)
4
Migration Tools
100%
Resource Coverage

CloudFormation to Terraform Migration

☁️

AWS CloudFormation → Terraform

Migrate your existing CloudFormation stacks to Terraform for multi-cloud flexibility

🔄 CloudFormation to Terraform Migration Architecture

🌐
Multi-Cloud

Single tool for AWS, Azure, GCP and 3000+ providers

📦
Modules

Reusable infrastructure components from Registry

👁️
Plan Preview

See changes before applying with terraform plan

🔒
State Mgmt

Track resource state with locking support

📋 Migration Workflow

1. Audit CFN
→
2. Import State
→
3. Write HCL
→
4. Validate
→
5. Delete CFN
📊

CloudFormation vs Terraform Syntax Comparison

Side-by-side syntax mapping for common patterns

Concept CloudFormation (YAML) Terraform (HCL)
VPC MainVPC: Type: AWS::EC2::VPC Properties: CidrBlock: 10.0.0.0/16 EnableDnsHostnames: true resource "aws_vpc" "main" { cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16" enable_dns_hostnames = true }
References VpcId: !Ref MainVPC SubnetId: !GetAtt PublicSubnet.SubnetId vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id subnet_id = aws_subnet.public.id
Parameters / Variables Parameters: Environment: Type: String Default: production # Usage: !Ref Environment variable "environment" { type = string default = "production" } # Usage: var.environment
Outputs Outputs: VpcId: Value: !Ref MainVPC Export: Name: MainVpcId output "vpc_id" { value = aws_vpc.main.id description = "The VPC ID" }
Conditionals Conditions: IsProd: !Equals [!Ref Env, prod] Resources: ProdBucket: Condition: IsProd resource "aws_s3_bucket" "prod" { count = var.environment == "prod" ? 1 : 0 bucket = "prod-bucket" }
Loops # Limited: Use macros # or nested stacks resource "aws_subnet" "public" { for_each = var.availability_zones cidr_block = each.value }
🗺️

AWS Resource Type Mapping

CloudFormation to Terraform resource mapping for Django apps

📦 Category CloudFormation Type Terraform Resource Import ID Format
🌐
Network 		AWS::EC2::VPC aws_vpc vpc-0123456789abcdef
AWS::EC2::Subnet aws_subnet subnet-0123456789abcdef
AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup aws_security_group sg-0123456789abcdef
AWS::EC2::InternetGateway aws_internet_gateway igw-0123456789abcdef
⚡
Compute 		AWS::EC2::Instance aws_instance i-0123456789abcdef
AWS::ECS::Cluster aws_ecs_cluster arn:aws:ecs:region:...:cluster/name
AWS::Lambda::Function aws_lambda_function function_name
🗄️
Database 		AWS::RDS::DBInstance aws_db_instance db-instance-identifier
AWS::RDS::DBSubnetGroup aws_db_subnet_group subnet-group-name
AWS::ElastiCache::CacheCluster aws_elasticache_cluster cluster-id
📁
Storage 		AWS::S3::Bucket aws_s3_bucket bucket-name
AWS::EFS::FileSystem aws_efs_file_system fs-0123456789abcdef
⚖️
LB 		AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::LoadBalancer aws_lb arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:...
AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::TargetGroup aws_lb_target_group arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:...
💡 Tip: Use terraform import aws_resource.name <import_id> to import existing resources
📅

Django Project Migration Timeline

Typical 2-week CFN to Terraform migration schedule

Phase
D1
D2
D3
D4
D5
D6
D7
D8
D9
D10
🔍 Discovery
Audit
📋 Planning
Module Design
📥 Import State
terraform import
📝 Write HCL
Code + Refactor
🧪 Testing
Validate + Plan
🚀 Cutover
Go Live
Audit CFN stacks
Design module structure
Import existing state
Write & refactor HCL
Test & validate
Production cutover
⏱️ Estimate: Small Django app (5-10 resources): 3-5 days | Medium (20-50 resources): 1-2 weeks | Large (50+ resources): 2-4 weeks
🛠️

CloudFormation to Terraform Conversion Tools

Automated tools to accelerate your migration

Tool Description Usage Best For
cf2tf Python-based CFN to Terraform converter. Supports most AWS resources. pip install cf2tf
cf2tf my-stack.yaml -o terraform/ 		Quick Conversion
Former2 Web-based AWS resource scanner. Generates TF from live AWS account. Visit former2.com
Login with AWS credentials
Select resources → Export TF 		Live Discovery
Terraformer CLI tool that generates TF from existing infra. Multi-cloud support. terraformer import aws \
--resources=vpc,ec2 \
--regions=us-east-1 		Bulk Import
terraform import Native Terraform command. Manual but most reliable. terraform import \
aws_vpc.main vpc-abc123 		Precision
💡 Recommendation: Use cf2tf for initial conversion, former2 for discovery, then manual terraform import for validation
⏱️ ~30 min

Step 1: Audit CloudFormation Resources

audit_cfn.sh 
# List all CloudFormation stacks
aws cloudformation list-stacks --stack-status-filter CREATE_COMPLETE UPDATE_COMPLETE

# Get detailed stack resources
aws cloudformation describe-stack-resources --stack-name anihpj-production

# Export stack as JSON for reference
aws cloudformation get-template --stack-name anihpj-production > cfn-template.json

# List all resources with physical IDs (needed for import)
aws cloudformation list-stack-resources --stack-name anihpj-production \
  --query 'StackResourceSummaries[*].[LogicalResourceId,PhysicalResourceId,ResourceType]' \
  --output table
⏱️ ~2-4 hrs

Step 2: Import Resources into Terraform State

import_resources.tf 
# First, create empty resource blocks for import
# main.tf

# VPC Import
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  # Configuration will be filled after import
}

# Subnet Import
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  # Configuration will be filled after import
}

# RDS Import
resource "aws_db_instance" "anihpj" {
  # Configuration will be filled after import
}

# S3 Bucket Import
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "media" {
  # Configuration will be filled after import
}
import_commands.sh 
# Import VPC
terraform import aws_vpc.main vpc-0abc123def456789

# Import Subnets
terraform import aws_subnet.public subnet-0abc123def456789

# Import RDS Instance
terraform import aws_db_instance.anihpj anihpj-production-db

# Import S3 Bucket
terraform import aws_s3_bucket.media anihpj-production-media

# Import Security Group
terraform import aws_security_group.web sg-0abc123def456789

# After import, run plan to see current state
terraform plan
⏱️ ~1-2 days

Step 3: Write Complete HCL Configuration

main.tf (after filling in from state) 
# After running terraform show, fill in the actual values
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-production-vpc"
    Environment = "production"
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    MigratedFrom = "cloudformation"
  }
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block              = "10.0.1.0/24"
  availability_zone       = "us-east-1a"
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-public-subnet"
    Environment = "production"
  }
}

resource "aws_db_instance" "anihpj" {
  identifier             = "anihpj-production-db"
  engine                 = "postgres"
  engine_version         = "14.9"
  instance_class         = "db.t3.medium"
  allocated_storage      = 100
  db_name                = "anihpj"
  username               = "anihpj_admin"
  password               = var.db_password
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.database.id]
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
  skip_final_snapshot    = false
  
  # IMPORTANT: Prevent recreation during migration
  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
    ignore_changes  = [password]
  }
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-production-db"
    Environment = "production"
  }
}
⏱️ ~1-2 hrs

Step 4: Validate No Changes

validate_migration.sh 
# Run terraform plan - should show NO changes
terraform plan

# Expected output:
# No changes. Your infrastructure matches the configuration.

# If there are differences, update your HCL to match
# Use lifecycle ignore_changes for dynamic values

# After validation, apply to ensure state is clean
terraform apply
✅

Migration Validation Checklist

Verify migration success with these steps

🔍 terraform plan -refresh-only
terraform plan -refresh-only

Detects drift without proposing changes. Should return "No changes".

🖥️ AWS Console Comparison
  • Compare resource counts
  • Verify tags match
  • Check security group rules
  • Validate IAM policies
🧪 Isolated Environment Test
  • Clone TF config to test workspace
  • Run terraform apply
  • Deploy Django app to test env
  • Run integration tests
validate_migration.sh 
# Full validation script for Django migration
echo "=== Migration Validation ==="

# 1. Check for drift
echo "Checking for state drift..."
terraform plan -refresh-only -detailed-exitcode
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "✅ No drift detected"
elif [ $? -eq 2 ]; then
  echo "⚠️ Drift detected - review changes"
  exit 1
fi

# 2. Validate state matches resources
echo "Validating resource state..."
terraform state list | while read resource; do
  echo "Checking: $resource"
  terraform state show "$resource" > /dev/null 2>&1 || echo "❌ Failed: $resource"
done

# 3. Test Django connectivity
echo "Testing Django app connectivity..."
DJANGO_URL=$(terraform output -raw app_url)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "$DJANGO_URL/health/" | grep -q "200" && echo "✅ Django healthy" || echo "❌ Django unhealthy"

# 4. Verify database connection
echo "Verifying RDS endpoint..."
RDS_ENDPOINT=$(terraform output -raw db_endpoint)
nc -zv ${RDS_ENDPOINT%:*} ${RDS_ENDPOINT#*:} && echo "✅ RDS reachable" || echo "❌ RDS unreachable"

echo "=== Validation Complete ==="
⏱️ ~30 min

⚠️ Step 5: Delete CloudFormation Stack (Carefully!)

delete_cfn_stack.sh 
# IMPORTANT: Before deleting, set DeletionPolicy to Retain for all resources
# This prevents CFN from deleting resources when stack is deleted

# Option 1: Update CFN template with RetainPolicy and update stack
# Then delete the stack

# Option 2: Delete stack with retain flag (CLI)
aws cloudformation delete-stack \
  --stack-name anihpj-production \
  --retain-resources \
    "WebServerSecurityGroup" \
    "DatabaseInstance" \
    "MediaBucket" \
    "MainVPC"

# Verify stack is deleted but resources remain
aws cloudformation describe-stacks --stack-name anihpj-production
# Should return: Stack with id anihpj-production does not exist

# Verify resources still exist
aws ec2 describe-vpcs --vpc-ids vpc-0abc123def456789
aws rds describe-db-instances --db-instance-identifier anihpj-production-db
📈

Gradual Migration Strategy

Phased approach for safe production migrations

PHASE 1
🟢 Non-Critical First
  • Dev environment resources
  • Test S3 buckets
  • Non-prod VPCs
  • Monitoring dashboards
PHASE 2
🟡 Staging Environment
  • Staging VPC & subnets
  • Staging RDS instance
  • Test with real traffic
  • Validate monitoring
PHASE 3
🔴 Production
  • Production VPC
  • Production RDS
  • ECS/EC2 clusters
  • ALB & certificates
⏱️ Parallel Run: Run both CFN and TF managing the same resources during validation period. Use terraform plan daily to detect drift.
🔄

Production Cut-Over Plan

Blue/Green approach with rollback procedures

Blue/Green Approach
  1. Create TF-managed infra parallel to CFN
  2. Test TF infra with synthetic traffic
  3. Gradually shift traffic using Route53 weights
  4. Monitor error rates and latency
  5. Full cutover once validated
🌐 DNS Failover Preparation
  • Lower TTL to 60s before migration
  • Configure Route53 health checks
  • Set up weighted routing policy
  • Test failover in staging first
  • Document DNS rollback procedure
⚠️ Rollback Procedures
  • Keep CFN stack in REVIEW_IN_PROGRESS
  • Snapshot all databases before cutover
  • Document terraform state rm commands
  • Test rollback in staging environment
  • Set up PagerDuty alerts for cutover

🛠️ Helpful Migration Tools

cf2tf

Converts CloudFormation templates to Terraform

pip install cf2tf
former2

Generates IaC from existing AWS resources

npm i -g former2
terraformer

Import existing infrastructure to Terraform

brew install terraformer

🔀 Which Migration Path Should You Choose?

⚡

Quick Commands Reference

Essential commands for both migration paths

☁️ CloudFormation Path
# Convert template
cf2tf template.yaml -o main.tf
# Import resource
terraform import aws_vpc.main vpc-xxx
# Validate state
terraform plan -refresh-only
🖱️ Manual Import Path
# Auto-generate with Terraformer
terraformer import aws --resources=vpc,rds,s3
# Or use import blocks (TF 1.5+)
import { to=aws_vpc.main id="vpc-xxx" }
# Generate config from state
terraform plan -generate-config-out=gen.tf

Manual Infrastructure to Terraform Migration

🖱️

Console-Created Resources → Terraform

Bring your manually created AWS resources under Terraform management

1
Discovery Inventory Resources
2
Terraformer Auto-Generate
3
Manual Import Precise Control
4
Refactor Clean & Modularize
5
Validate Test & Verify
⏱️ ~1-2 hrs

📋 Phase 1: Discovery & Inventory

discover_resources.sh 
# Use AWS Resource Explorer or CLI to discover resources
# List all resources in account (requires Resource Explorer setup)
aws resource-explorer-2 search --query-string "*" --region us-east-1

# Or use individual service commands
# VPCs
aws ec2 describe-vpcs --query 'Vpcs[*].[VpcId,CidrBlock,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' --output table

# Subnets
aws ec2 describe-subnets --query 'Subnets[*].[SubnetId,VpcId,CidrBlock,AvailabilityZone]' --output table

# EC2 Instances
aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,InstanceType,State.Name,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' --output table

# RDS Instances
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[*].[DBInstanceIdentifier,Engine,DBInstanceClass,DBInstanceStatus]' --output table

# S3 Buckets
aws s3api list-buckets --query 'Buckets[*].[Name,CreationDate]' --output table

# Security Groups
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query 'SecurityGroups[*].[GroupId,GroupName,VpcId,Description]' --output table
⏱️ ~30 min

🔧 Phase 2: Auto-Generate with Terraformer

terraformer_import.sh 
# Install Terraformer
brew install terraformer  # macOS
# or download from GitHub releases

# Initialize provider first
cat > versions.tf << 'EOF'
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}
EOF

terraform init

# Import all AWS resources
terraformer import aws --resources="*" --regions=us-east-1

# Or import specific resources
terraformer import aws \
  --resources=vpc,subnet,security_group,ec2_instance,rds,s3 \
  --regions=us-east-1 \
  --profile=production

# Import with filters
terraformer import aws \
  --resources=ec2_instance \
  --filter="Name=tags.Environment;Value=production" \
  --regions=us-east-1

# Output will be in generated/aws/ directory
ls -la generated/aws/
⏱️ ~2-4 hrs

📝 Phase 3: Manual Import (for Precise Control)

manual_import.tf 
# Using import blocks (Terraform 1.5+)
# This is the modern, declarative approach

# Import VPC
import {
  to = aws_vpc.main
  id = "vpc-0abc123def456789"
}

resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-production"
    Environment = "production"
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}

# Import EC2 Instance
import {
  to = aws_instance.web
  id = "i-0abc123def456789"
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0abc123def456789"
  instance_type = "t3.medium"
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public.id
  
  vpc_security_group_ids = [
    aws_security_group.web.id
  ]
  
  tags = {
    Name        = "anihpj-web"
    Environment = "production"
  }
}

# Import RDS
import {
  to = aws_db_instance.main
  id = "anihpj-production"
}

resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  identifier        = "anihpj-production"
  engine            = "postgres"
  engine_version    = "14.9"
  instance_class    = "db.t3.medium"
  allocated_storage = 100
  
  # These values will be ignored
  db_name  = "anihpj"
  username = "anihpj_admin"
  password = var.db_password
  
  skip_final_snapshot = false
  
  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [password]
  }
}
run_import.sh 
# With import blocks, just run plan to see the import
terraform plan

# Apply to perform the imports
terraform apply

# Terraform 1.5+ will import and then show if config matches

# For older Terraform versions, use CLI import
terraform import aws_vpc.main vpc-0abc123def456789
terraform import aws_instance.web i-0abc123def456789
terraform import aws_db_instance.main anihpj-production
⏱️ ~1-2 days
🔧

Phase 4: Refactoring & Standardization

Extract modules, standardize naming, apply consistent tagging

📦 Extract Modules
  • Group related resources (VPC module)
  • Create reusable patterns (RDS module)
  • Separate environment configs
  • Use moved blocks for refactoring
🏷️ Standardize Naming
  • Pattern: {project}-{env}-{resource}
  • Example: anihpj-prod-vpc
  • Use terraform state mv for renames
  • Document naming conventions
🔖 Consistent Tagging
  • Required: Name, Environment, Project
  • Cost allocation: CostCenter, Owner
  • Automation: ManagedBy=terraform
  • Use default_tags in provider
refactor_to_modules.tf 
# Using moved blocks for safe refactoring (Terraform 1.1+)
# Move resources into modules without destroying them

# Move VPC resources into a module
moved {
  from = aws_vpc.main
  to   = module.vpc.aws_vpc.this
}

moved {
  from = aws_subnet.public
  to   = module.vpc.aws_subnet.public[0]
}

# Now reference the module
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  name        = "anihpj"
  environment = var.environment
  cidr_block  = "10.0.0.0/16"
  
  # Standardized tags applied automatically
}

# Provider with default tags for consistency
provider "aws" {
  region = var.region
  
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "anihpj"
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      Repository  = "github.com/yourorg/anihpj-infra"
    }
  }
}
⏱️ ~2-4 hrs
✅

Phase 5: Validation

Verify imported resources match actual infrastructure

validate_manual_import.sh 
# 1. Refresh-only plan to detect drift
terraform plan -refresh-only

# Expected: "No changes. Your infrastructure matches the configuration."
# If changes detected, update HCL or use lifecycle ignore_changes

# 2. Compare resource counts with AWS Console
echo "=== Comparing with AWS Console ==="
echo "Terraform resources: $(terraform state list | wc -l)"

# Compare VPCs
TF_VPCS=$(terraform state list | grep aws_vpc | wc -l)
AWS_VPCS=$(aws ec2 describe-vpcs --query 'length(Vpcs)')
echo "VPCs - TF: $TF_VPCS, AWS: $AWS_VPCS"

# Compare EC2 instances
TF_EC2=$(terraform state list | grep aws_instance | wc -l)
AWS_EC2=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'length(Reservations[].Instances[])')
echo "EC2 - TF: $TF_EC2, AWS: $AWS_EC2"

# 3. Test in isolated environment
terraform workspace new test-import
terraform plan  # Should show no changes from fresh import
terraform workspace select default

# 4. Run smoke tests
./scripts/smoke_test.sh

🐍 Django anihpj Migration Checklist

✅ Compute
  • EC2 instances (Django app servers)
  • Auto Scaling Groups
  • Launch Templates
  • ECS Services (if containerized)
✅ Database
  • RDS PostgreSQL instance
  • DB Subnet Groups
  • Parameter Groups
  • Option Groups
✅ Storage
  • S3 buckets (static/media)
  • Bucket policies
  • CORS configurations
  • Lifecycle rules
✅ Networking
  • VPC & Subnets
  • Security Groups
  • ALB/NLB
  • Route 53 records
🔧

Troubleshooting Quick Reference

Common issues and their solutions

❌ "Resource already exists"

Terraform trying to create resource that already exists in AWS

✅ Solution
terraform import aws_resource.name id
⚠️ "Drift detected after import"

HCL doesn't match imported resource configuration

✅ Solution
terraform state show resource_name

Copy attributes to your .tf file

🔗 "Dependency cycle"

Resources have circular references

✅ Solution
depends_on = [aws_resource.first]

Use explicit depends_on

⚠️
Common Migration Pitfalls to Avoid
🚫 No State Backup

Always backup terraform.tfstate before migration. Use versioned S3 backend.

🚫 Skipping Staging

Never migrate production first. Test migration process in dev/staging environments.

🚫 Premature CFN Deletion

Don't delete CloudFormation stacks until Terraform state is fully validated.

🚫 Ignoring Dependencies

Import resources in correct order. RDS needs VPC/subnets imported first.

💡
Migration Best Practices
  • Start with non-production: Always migrate dev/staging environments first
  • Use lifecycle blocks: Add prevent_destroy = true for critical resources
  • Commit early: Check in your Terraform code frequently during migration
  • Plan before apply: Review every plan carefully during migration
  • Document everything: Keep notes on resource mappings and decisions

📊 Migration Impact: Before vs After

❌
Before Migration
  • Manual console changes
  • No version control
  • Undocumented infrastructure
  • Difficult to replicate
  • High error risk
  • Slow deployments
TRANSFORM
✅
After Migration
  • Infrastructure as Code
  • Git version control
  • Self-documenting HCL
  • Reproducible environments
  • Drift detection
  • Automated deployments

📝 Key Takeaways

📥
Import First

Never recreate - always import existing resources

🔒
Backup State

Use versioned S3 backend for state recovery

🧪
Test First

Migrate staging before production

✅
Validate Often

Run plan -refresh-only frequently

🚀

🎯 Success Metrics & KPIs

Measure and track the success of your Terraform and IaC adoption

Metrics Dashboard Overview

Track your IaC journey with data-driven insights

📈 Maturity Model 🎯 KPIs

Infrastructure as Code Maturity Model

📊 Where is your team on the IaC journey?

Assess your Infrastructure as Code maturity level and identify areas for improvement.

🚀 IaC Maturity Journey

1

Level 1: Manual

Ad-hoc infrastructure management

  • Infrastructure created via AWS Console
  • No version control for infrastructure
  • Manual deployments, no automation
  • Knowledge siloed in individuals
2

Level 2: Scripted

Basic automation with scripts

  • Shell scripts or AWS CLI for some tasks
  • Some documentation exists
  • Inconsistent practices across team
  • Limited repeatability
3

Level 3: Terraform Adopted

IaC implemented for new resources

  • New infrastructure defined in Terraform
  • Code stored in Git with PRs
  • Remote state with locking
  • Basic CI/CD pipeline for infra
4

Level 4: Standardized

Consistent practices across organization

  • All infrastructure managed by Terraform
  • Reusable modules in private registry
  • Policy as code (Sentinel/OPA)
  • Automated security scanning
5

Level 5: Optimized

Continuous improvement and innovation

  • Self-service infrastructure platform
  • Automated testing of infrastructure
  • Cost optimization integrated
  • GitOps with drift detection

📋 Quick Assessment Checklist

12 Items 4 Categories
📂 Version Control
💾 State Management
⚙️ Automation
🔒 Security

📊 Maturity Level Benefits Comparison

Capability L1 L2 L3 L4 L5
Deployment Speed Days Hours ~1 Hour Minutes On-demand
Reproducibility ❌ ⚠️ ✅ ✅ ✅✅
Disaster Recovery Weeks Days Hours ~1 Hour <30 min
Drift Detection ❌ ❌ Manual Scheduled Real-time
Compliance Manual Manual Basic Automated Policy-driven
Cost Visibility ❌ ⚠️ Basic Integrated Predictive
💡

Pro Tips for Advancing Maturity

→ L1→L2: Start with simple bash scripts to automate repetitive AWS CLI commands
→ L2→L3: Pick ONE project and convert it fully to Terraform as a learning exercise
→ L3→L4: Create reusable modules and establish coding standards for the team
→ L4→L5: Implement Sentinel/OPA policies and set up automated drift remediation

Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

📈 Why Track KPIs?

KPIs help you measure the effectiveness of your Terraform adoption and identify areas for continuous improvement. Data-driven decisions lead to better infrastructure outcomes.

4
Categories
11
Metrics
Elite
Target

📈 Tracking KPIs with Terraform

outputs.tf - KPI Data Export 
# Export metrics for tracking
output "kpi_metrics" {
  description = "Infrastructure KPIs for tracking"
  value = {
    # Resource counts
    total_resources_managed = length(data.terraform_remote_state.main.outputs)
    
    # Coverage by type
    ec2_instances      = length(aws_instance.web)
    rds_instances      = length(aws_db_instance.main)
    s3_buckets         = length(aws_s3_bucket.media)
    security_groups    = length(aws_security_group.web)
    
    # Compliance
    encrypted_resources = length([for r in aws_ebs_volume.data : r if r.encrypted])
    tagged_resources    = length([for r in aws_instance.web : r if lookup(r.tags, "Environment", "") != ""])
    
    # Last updated
    last_apply_timestamp = timestamp()
    terraform_version    = var.terraform_version
    state_version        = data.terraform_remote_state.main.outputs.state_version
  }
}

# CloudWatch metrics for KPI dashboard
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "deployment_failure" {
  alarm_name          = "terraform-deployment-failure"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "DeploymentFailures"
  namespace           = "Terraform/CICD"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = 0
  alarm_description   = "Terraform deployment failure detected"
  
  alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}

📋 Weekly IaC Review Template

1. Deployment Summary
  • Number of terraform applies this week: ___
  • Failed applies: ___
  • Rollbacks performed: ___
2. Drift Detection
  • Resources with drift detected: ___
  • Drift remediated: ___
  • Manual changes requiring import: ___
3. Security & Compliance
  • Security scan findings: ___
  • Policy violations: ___
  • Remediation completed: ___
4. Cost Tracking
  • Estimated monthly cost: $___
  • Cost change from last week: ____%
  • Cost optimization opportunities identified: ___

🎯 DORA Metrics for IaC

DevOps Research and Assessment metrics applied to Infrastructure as Code

⭐ Elite 🔷 High 🔶 Medium 🔻 Low
🚀
Deployment Frequency
How often you deploy
ELITE
On-demand
HIGH
Daily-Week
MED
Week-Month
LOW
Monthly+
⏱️
Lead Time for Changes
Commit to production time
ELITE
<1 hour
HIGH
1d-1wk
MED
1wk-1mo
LOW
>1 month
📉
Change Failure Rate
% of failed deployments
ELITE
0-5%
HIGH
5-10%
MED
10-15%
LOW
>15%
🔄
Mean Time to Recovery
Time to restore service
ELITE
<1 hour
HIGH
<1 day
MED
1d-1wk
LOW
>1 week
Target Level: Elite
Aim for Elite performance across all 4 DORA metrics to achieve world-class DevOps maturity with Terraform
🚀

Quick Wins (Week 1)

✓ Set up remote S3 backend with DynamoDB locking
✓ Enable terraform fmt in pre-commit hooks
✓ Add tfsec to CI pipeline for security scanning
✓ Create a simple tagging module for consistency
⚠️

Common Pitfalls to Avoid

✗ Storing secrets in .tfvars files in Git
✗ Using local state for team projects
✗ Skipping terraform plan before apply
✗ Ignoring drift detection (manual console changes)

Terraform Cloud

Infracost

tfsec / Checkov

📈
Spacelift / env0
Advanced drift detection
📋
terraform-docs
Auto-generate documentation
🧪
Terratest
Automated infrastructure testing
